Compiling this L](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [A](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py) [TEX source has the effect of gen-](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py) [erating a device-independent representation of a textbook, which can be converted to other formats](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [and printed.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

The L A [TEX source for this book is available from](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [http://www.thinkpython.com](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

# [Preface](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

#### [The strange history of this book](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[In January 1999 I was preparing to teach an introductory programming class in Java. I had](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [taught it three times and I was getting frustrated. The failure rate in the class was too high](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and, even for students who succeeded, the overall level of achievement was too low.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[One of the problems I saw was the books. They were too big, with too much unnecessary](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [detail about Java, and not enough high-level guidance about how to program. And they all](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [suffered from the trap door effect: they would start out easy, proceed gradually, and then](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [somewhere around Chapter 5 the bottom would fall out. The students would get too much](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [new material, too fast, and I would spend the rest of the semester picking up the pieces.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Two weeks before the first day of classes, I decided to write my own book. My goals were:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

- [• Keep it short. It is better for students to read 10 pages than not read 50 pages.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

- [• Be careful with vocabulary. I tried to minimize jargon and define each term at first](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [use.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• Build gradually. To avoid trap doors, I took the most difficult topics and split them](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [into a series of small steps.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

- [• Focus on programming, not the programming language.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [I included the minimum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [useful subset of Java and left out the rest.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

I needed a title, so on a whim I chose How to Think Like a Computer Scientist .

[My first version was rough, but it worked. Students did the reading, and they understood](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [enough that I could spend class time on the hard topics, the interesting topics and (most](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [important) letting the students practice.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[I released the book under the GNU Free Documentation License, which allows users to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [copy, modify, and distribute the book.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[What happened next is the cool part. Jeff Elkner, a high school teacher in Virginia, adopted](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [my book and translated it into Python. He sent me a copy of his translation, and I had the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [unusual experience of learning Python by reading my own book. As Green Tea Press, I](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [published the first Python version in 2001.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[In 2003 I started teaching at Olin College and I got to teach Python for the first time. The](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [contrast with Java was striking. Students struggled less, learned more, worked on more](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [interesting projects, and generally had a lot more fun.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[vi](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py) [Chapter 0. Preface](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Since then I’ve continued to develop the book, correcting errors, improving some of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [examples and adding material, especially exercises.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The result is this book, now with the less grandiose title](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) Think Python [. Some of the changes](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [are:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

- [• I added a section about debugging at the end of each chapter. These sections present](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [general techniques for finding and avoiding bugs, and warnings about Python pit-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [falls.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

- [• I added more exercises, ranging from short tests of understanding to a few substantial](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [projects. Most exercises include a link to my solution.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

- [• I added a series of case studies—longer examples with exercises, solutions, and dis-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [cussion.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

- [• I expanded the discussion of program development plans and basic design patterns.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

- [• I added appendices about debugging and analysis of algorithms.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The second edition of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Think Python](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [has these new features:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

- [• The book and all supporting code have been updated to Python 3.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

- [• I added a few sections, and more details on the web, to help beginners get started](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [running Python in a browser, so you don’t have to deal with installing Python until](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [you want to.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

- [• For Chapter 4.1 I switched from my own turtle graphics package, called Swampy, to a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [more standard Python module,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [turtle](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, which is easier to install and more powerful.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

- [• I added a new chapter called “The Goodies”, which introduces some additional](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Python features that are not strictly necessary, but sometimes handy.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[I hope you enjoy working with this book, and that it helps you learn to program and think](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [like a computer scientist, at least a little bit.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Allen B. Downey](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

Olin College

#### [Contributor List](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[More than 100 sharp-eyed and thoughtful readers have sent in suggestions and corrections](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [over the past few years. Their contributions, and enthusiasm for this project, have been a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [huge help.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[If you have a suggestion or correction, please send email to](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) feedback@thinkpython.com . [If I make a change based on your feedback, I will add you to the contributor list (unless](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [you ask to be omitted).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[If you include at least part of the sentence the error appears in, that makes it easy for me to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [search. Page and section numbers are fine, too, but not quite as easy to work with. Thanks!](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

- [• Patryk Wolowiec helped me with a problem in the HTML version.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

xi

- [• Mark Chonofsky told me about a new keyword in Python 3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

- [• Russell Coleman helped me with my geometry.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

- [• Nam Nguyen found a typo and pointed out that I used the Decorator pattern but didn’t men-](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [tion it by name.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

- [• Stéphane Morin sent in several corrections and suggestions.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

- [• Paul Stoop corrected a typo in](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [uses_only](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

- [• Eric Bronner pointed out a confusion in the discussion of the order of operations.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

- [• Alexandros Gezerlis set a new standard for the number and quality of suggestions he submit-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [ted. We are deeply grateful!](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

- [• Gray Thomas knows his right from his left.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

- [• Giovanni Escobar Sosa sent a long list of corrections and suggestions.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

- [• Daniel Neilson corrected an error about the order of operations.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

- [• Will McGinnis pointed out that](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [polyline](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [was defined differently in two places.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

- [• Frank Hecker pointed out an exercise that was under-specified, and some broken links.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

- [• Animesh B helped me clean up a confusing example.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

- [• Martin Caspersen found two round-off errors.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

- [• Gregor Ulm sent several corrections and suggestions.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

- [• Dimitrios Tsirigkas suggested I clarify an exercise.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

- [• Carlos Tafur sent a page of corrections and suggestions.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

- [• Martin Nordsletten found a bug in an exercise solution.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• Sven Hoexter pointed out that a variable named](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [input](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [shadows a build-in function.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• Stephen Gregory pointed out the problem with](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [cmp](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [in Python 3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py)

- [• Ishwar Bhat corrected my statement of Fermat’s last theorem.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

- [• Andrea Zanella translated the book into Italian, and sent a number of corrections along the](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [way.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

- Many, many thanks to Melissa Lewis and Luciano Ramalho for excellent comments and sug- [gestions on the second edition.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

- [• Thanks to Harry Percival from PythonAnywhere for his help getting people started running](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Python in a browser.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

- [• Xavier Van Aubel made several useful corrections in the second edition.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• William Murray corrected my definition of floor division.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

- [• Per Starbäck brought me up to date on universal newlines in Python 3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

- [• Laurent Rosenfeld and Mihaela Rotaru translated this book into French. Along the way, they](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [sent many corrections and suggestions.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[In addition, people who spotted typos or made corrections include Czeslaw Czapla, Dale Wil-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [son, Francesco Carlo Cimini, Richard Fursa, Brian McGhie, Lokesh Kumar Makani, Matthew](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Shultz, Viet Le, Victor Simeone, Lars O.D. Christensen, Swarup Sahoo, Alix Etienne, Kuang](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [He, Wei Huang, Karen Barber, and Eric Ransom.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

## [Chapter 1](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [The way of the program](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[The goal of this book is to teach you to think like a computer scientist. This way of think-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [ing combines some of the best features of mathematics, engineering, and natural science.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Like mathematicians, computer scientists use formal languages to denote ideas (specifi-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [cally computations). Like engineers, they design things, assembling components into sys-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [tems and evaluating tradeoffs among alternatives. Like scientists, they observe the behav-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [ior of complex systems, form hypotheses, and test predictions.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[The single most important skill for a computer scientist is](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [problem solving](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [. Problem solv-](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [ing means the ability to formulate problems, think creatively about solutions, and express](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [a solution clearly and accurately. As it turns out, the process of learning to program is an](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [excellent opportunity to practice problem-solving skills. That’s why this chapter is called,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [“The way of the program”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[On one level, you will be learning to program, a useful skill by itself. On another level, you](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [will use programming as a means to an end. As we go along, that end will become clearer.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

#### [1.1](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [What is a program?](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

A program is a sequence of instructions that specifies how to perform a computation. The [computation might be something mathematical, such as solving a system of equations or](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [finding the roots of a polynomial, but it can also be a symbolic computation, such as search-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [ing and replacing text in a document or something graphical, like processing an image or](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [playing a video.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The details look different in different languages, but a few basic instructions appear in just](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [about every language:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[input:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Get data from the keyboard, a file, the network, or some other device.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[output:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Display data on the screen, save it in a file, send it over the network, etc.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[math:](https://thinkpython.com/code/double.py) [Perform basic mathematical operations like addition and multiplication.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[conditional execution:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Check for certain conditions and run the appropriate code.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 1. The way of the program](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[repetition:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Perform some action repeatedly, usually with some variation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Believe it or not, that’s pretty much all there is to it.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [Every program you’ve ever used,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [no matter how complicated, is made up of instructions that look pretty much like these.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [So you can think of programming as the process of breaking a large, complex task into](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [smaller and smaller subtasks until the subtasks are simple enough to be performed with](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [one of these basic instructions.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

#### [1.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [Running Python](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[One of the challenges of getting started with Python is that you might have to install](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [Python and related software on your computer. If you are familiar with your operating](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [system, and especially if you are comfortable with the command-line interface, you will](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [have no trouble installing Python. But for beginners, it can be painful to learn about sys-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [tem administration and programming at the same time.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[To avoid that problem, I recommend that you start out running Python in a browser. Later,](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [when you are comfortable with Python, I’ll make suggestions for installing Python on your](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [computer.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[There are a number of web pages you can use to run Python. If you already have a fa-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [vorite, go ahead and use it. Otherwise I recommend PythonAnywhere. I provide detailed](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [instructions for getting started at](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[There are two versions of Python, called Python 2 and Python 3. They are very similar, so](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [if you learn one, it is easy to switch to the other. In fact, there are only a few differences you](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [will encounter as a beginner. This book is written for Python 3, but I include some notes](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [about Python 2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The Python](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [interpreter](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is a program that reads and executes Python code.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [Depending](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [on your environment, you might start the interpreter by clicking on an icon, or by typing](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [python](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [on a command line. When it starts, you should see output like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Python 3.4.0 (default, Jun 19 2015, 14:20:21)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [GCC 4.8.2] on linux [Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) >>>

[The first three lines contain information about the interpreter and the operating system it’s](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [running on, so it might be different for you. But you should check that the version number,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) which is [3.4.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [in this example, begins with 3, which indicates that you are running Python](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [3. If it begins with 2, you are running (you guessed it) Python 2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[The last line is a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [prompt](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [that indicates that the interpreter is ready for you to enter code. If](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [you type a line of code and hit Enter, the interpreter displays the result:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[>>> 1 + 1](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[Now you’re ready to get started. From here on, I assume that you know how to start the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Python interpreter and run code.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[1.3. The first program](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [1.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The first program](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Traditionally, the first program you write in a new language is called “Hello, World!” be-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [cause all it does is display the words “Hello, World!”. In Python, it looks like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[>>> print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [Hello, World!](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[This is an example of a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [print statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [, although it doesn’t actually print anything on](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [paper. It displays a result on the screen. In this case, the result is the words](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Hello, World!](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[The quotation marks in the program mark the beginning and end of the text to be dis-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [played; they don’t appear in the result.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[The parentheses indicate that](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [print](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [is a function. We’ll get to functions in Chapter 3.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[In Python 2, the print statement is slightly different; it is not a function, so it doesn’t use](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [parentheses.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[>>> print](https://thinkpython.com/code/zipf.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/zipf.py) [Hello, World!](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[This distinction will make more sense soon, but that’s enough to get started.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

#### [1.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Arithmetic operators](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[After “Hello, World”, the next step is arithmetic. Python provides](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [operators](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [, which are](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [special symbols that represent computations like addition and multiplication.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The operators](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [+](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [*](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [perform addition, subtraction, and multiplication, as in the fol-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [lowing examples:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[>>> 40 + 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> 43 - 1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> 6 * 7](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[The operator](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) / performs division:

>>> 84 / 2 42.0

[You might wonder why the result is](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [42.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [instead of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [. I’ll explain in the next section.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Finally, the operator](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [**](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [performs exponentiation; that is, it raises a number to a power:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> 6**2 + 6](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[In some other languages,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [^](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is used for exponentiation, but in Python it is a bitwise operator](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [called XOR. If you are not familiar with bitwise operators, the result will surprise you:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[>>> 6 ^ 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[I won’t cover bitwise operators in this book, but you can read about them at](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [http://wiki.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 1. The way of the program](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [1.5](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Values and types](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[A](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [value](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is one of the basic things a program works with, like a letter or a number. Some](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [values we have seen so far are](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [42.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Hello, World!](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ' .

[These values belong to different](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [types](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [is an](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [integer](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [42.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [floating-point number](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) , and ' Hello, World! ' is a string [, so-called because the letters it contains are strung together.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[If you are not sure what type a value has, the interpreter can tell you:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[>>> type(2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) <class ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [int](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> type(42.0)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [<class](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [float](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [>>> type(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Hello, World!](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [<class](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [str](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[In these results, the word “class” is used in the sense of a category; a type is a category of](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [values.](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[Not surprisingly, integers belong to the type](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [int](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [, strings belong to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [str](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [and floating-point](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [numbers belong to](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [float](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[What about values like](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [42.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [? They look like numbers, but they are in quotation](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [marks like strings.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

| <class    | '   | str  | '   | >   |
| --------- | --- | ---- | --- | --- |
| >>> type( | '   | 42.0 | '   | )   |
| <class    | '   | str  | '   | >   |

[They’re strings.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[When you type a large integer, you might be tempted to use commas between groups of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [digits, as in](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [1,000,000](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [. This is not a legal](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [integer](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [in Python, but it is legal:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py)

[>>> 1,000,000](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [(1, 0, 0)](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[That’s not what we expected at all! Python interprets](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [1,000,000](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [as a comma-separated](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [sequence of integers. We’ll learn more about this kind of sequence later.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

#### 1.6 [Formal and natural languages](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Natural languages](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [are the languages people speak, such as English, Spanish, and French.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [They were not designed by people (although people try to impose some order on them);](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [they evolved naturally.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Formal languages](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [are languages that are designed by people for specific applications. For](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [example, the notation that mathematicians use is a formal language that is particularly](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [good at denoting relationships among numbers and symbols. Chemists use a formal lan-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [guage to represent the chemical structure of molecules. And most importantly:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Programming languages are formal languages that have been designed to](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [express computations.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[1.6. Formal and natural languages](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Formal languages tend to have strict](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [syntax](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [rules that govern the structure of statements.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [For example, in mathematics the statement 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [+](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [=](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [6 has correct syntax, but 3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [+ =](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sorting_algorithm) [3$6](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sorting_algorithm) [does not. In chemistry](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [H](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [O](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [is a syntactically correct formula, but](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [Zz](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [is not.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[Syntax rules come in two flavors, pertaining to](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [tokens](https://thinkpython.com/code) [and structure. Tokens are the basic](https://thinkpython.com/code) [elements of the language, such as words, numbers, and chemical elements.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) One of the problems with 3 [+ =](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [3$6 is that $ is not a legal token in mathematics (at least as far as I](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [know). Similarly,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Zz](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [is not legal because there is no element with the abbreviation](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) Zz .

[The second type of syntax rule pertains to the way tokens are combined.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [The equation](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [+](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [/3 is illegal because even though](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [+](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [and / are legal tokens, you can’t have one right](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [after the other. Similarly, in a chemical formula the subscript comes after the element name,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [not before.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[This is @ well-structured Engli$h sentence with invalid t*kens in it. This sentence all valid](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [tokens has, but invalid structure with.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[When you read a sentence in English or a statement in a formal language, you have to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [figure out the structure (although in a natural language you do this subconsciously). This](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [process is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [parsing](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[Although formal and natural languages have many features in common—tokens, struc-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [ture, and syntax—there are some differences:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[ambiguity:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [Natural languages are full of ambiguity, which people deal with by using con-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [textual clues and other information. Formal languages are designed to be nearly or](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [completely unambiguous, which means that any statement has exactly one meaning,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [regardless of context.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[redundancy:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [In order to make up for ambiguity and reduce misunderstandings, natural](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [languages employ lots of redundancy. As a result, they are often verbose. Formal](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [languages are less redundant and more concise.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[literalness:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Natural languages are full of idiom and metaphor.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [If I say, “The penny](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [dropped”, there is probably no penny and nothing dropping (this idiom means that](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [someone understood something after a period of confusion). Formal languages mean](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) exactly what they say.

[Because we all grow up speaking natural languages, it is sometimes hard to adjust to for-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [mal languages. The difference between formal and natural language is like the difference](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [between poetry and prose, but more so:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Poetry:](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py) [Words are used for their sounds as well as for their meaning, and the whole poem](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [together creates an effect or emotional response. Ambiguity is not only common but](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [often deliberate.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Prose:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [The literal meaning of words is more important, and the structure contributes more](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [meaning. Prose is more amenable to analysis than poetry but still often ambiguous.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Programs:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [The meaning of a computer program is unambiguous and literal, and can be](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [understood entirely by analysis of the tokens and structure.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 1. The way of the program](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Formal languages are more dense than natural languages, so it takes longer to read them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Also, the structure is important, so it is not always best to read from top to bottom, left to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [right. Instead, learn to parse the program in your head, identifying the tokens and inter-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [preting the structure. Finally, the details matter. Small errors in spelling and punctuation,](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [which you can get away with in natural languages, can make a big difference in a formal](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) language.

#### [1.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[Programmers make mistakes. For whimsical reasons, programming errors are called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [bugs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.format) [and the process of tracking them down is called](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [debugging](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Programming, and especially debugging, sometimes brings out strong emotions. If you](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [are struggling with a difficult bug, you might feel angry, despondent, or embarrassed.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[There is evidence that people naturally respond to computers as if they were people. When](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [they work well, we think of them as teammates, and when they are obstinate or rude, we](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [respond to them the same way we respond to rude, obstinate people (Reeves and Nass,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [The Media Equation: How People Treat Computers, Television, and New Media Like Real People](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [and Places](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [).](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py)

[Preparing for these reactions might help you deal with them. One approach is to think of](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [the computer as an employee with certain strengths, like speed and precision, and partic-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [ular weaknesses, like lack of empathy and inability to grasp the big picture.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Your job is to be a good manager: find ways to take advantage of the strengths and mitigate](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the weaknesses. And find ways to use your emotions to engage with the problem, without](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [letting your reactions interfere with your ability to work effectively.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Learning to debug can be frustrating, but it is a valuable skill that is useful for many activ-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [ities beyond programming. At the end of each chapter there is a section, like this one, with](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [my suggestions for debugging. I hope they help!](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

#### 1.8 [Glossary](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

problem solving: The process of formulating a problem, finding a solution, and express- ing it.

[high-level language:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [A programming language like Python that is designed to be easy for](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [humans to read and write.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[low-level language:](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [A programming language that is designed to be easy for a computer](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [to run; also called “machine language” or “assembly language”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[portability:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [A property of a program that can run on more than one kind of computer.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[interpreter:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [A program that reads another program and executes it](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

prompt: [Characters displayed by the interpreter to indicate that it is ready to take input](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [from the user.](https://thinkpython.com/code/double.py)

program: [A set of instructions that specifies a computation.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[1.9. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[print statement:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [An instruction that causes the Python interpreter to display a value on](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [the screen.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[operator:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [A special symbol that represents a simple computation like addition, multipli-](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [cation, or string concatenation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

value: [One of the basic units of data, like a number or string, that a program manipulates.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[type:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [A category of values. The types we have seen so far are integers (type](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [int](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [), floating-](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [point numbers (type](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [float](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [), and strings (type](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [str](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py) [).](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py)

[integer:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [A type that represents whole numbers.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[floating-point:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [A type that represents numbers with fractional parts.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[string:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [A type that represents sequences of characters.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[natural language:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Any one of the languages that people speak that evolved naturally.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[formal language:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Any one of the languages that people have designed for specific pur-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [poses, such as representing mathematical ideas or computer programs; all program-](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [ming languages are formal languages.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[token:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [One of the basic elements of the syntactic structure of a program, analogous to a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [word in a natural language.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[syntax:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The rules that govern the structure of a program.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[parse:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [To examine a program and analyze the syntactic structure.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[bug:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [An error in a program.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[debugging:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The process of finding and correcting bugs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [1.9](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Exercise 1.1.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [It is a good idea to read this book in front of a computer so you can try out the](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) examples as you go.

[Whenever you are experimenting with a new feature, you should try to make mistakes. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [in the “Hello, world!” program, what happens if you leave out one of the quotation marks? What if](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [you leave out both? What if you spell](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [wrong?](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[This kind of experiment helps you remember what you read; it also helps when you are programming,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [because you get to know what the error messages mean. It is better to make mistakes now and on](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [purpose than later and accidentally.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

1. [1. In a print statement, what happens if you leave out one of the parentheses, or both?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

2. [2. If you are trying to print a string, what happens if you leave out one of the quotation marks,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [or both?](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

3. [3. You can use a minus sign to make a negative number like](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [-2](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [. What happens if you put a plus](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [sign before a number? What about](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [2++2](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [?](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py)

[Chapter 1. The way of the program](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

4. [4. In math notation, leading zeros are ok, as in](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [. What happens if you try this in Python?](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [What about](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [?](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

5. [5. What happens if you have two values with no operator between them?](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[Exercise 1.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [Start the Python interpreter and use it as a calculator.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

1. [1. How many seconds are there in 42 minutes 42 seconds?](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

2. [2. How many miles are there in 10 kilometers? Hint: there are 1.61 kilometers in a mile.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

3. [3. If you run a 10 kilometer race in 42 minutes 42 seconds, what is your average pace (time per](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [mile in minutes and seconds)? What is your average speed in miles per hour?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

## [Chapter 2](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Variables, expressions and](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[One of the most powerful features of a programming language is the ability to manipulate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [variables](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [. A variable is a name that refers to a value.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [2.1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Assignment statements](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[An](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [assignment statement](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [creates a new variable and gives it a value:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> message =](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [And now for something completely different](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [>>> n = 17](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [>>> pi = 3.1415926535897932](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [This example makes three assignments. The first assigns a string to a new variable named](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [message](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [; the second gives the integer](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [to](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [; the third assigns the (approximate) value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [π](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [to](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [pi](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[A common way to represent variables on paper is to write the name with an arrow pointing](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [to its value. This kind of figure is called a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [state diagram](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [because it shows what state each](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) of the variables is in (think of it as the variable’s state of mind). Figure 2.1 shows the result of the previous example.

#### [2.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Variable names](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Programmers generally choose names for their variables that are meaningful—they docu-](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [ment what the variable is used for.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[message](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [’And now for something completely different’](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[n](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d)

[pi](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [3.1415926535897932](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[Figure 2.1: State diagram.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 2. Variables, expressions and statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Variable names can be as long as you like. They can contain both letters and numbers, but](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [they can’t begin with a number. It is legal to use uppercase letters, but it is conventional to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [use only lower case for variables names.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[The underscore character,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [_](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [, can appear in a name. It is often used in names with multiple](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [words, such as](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [your_name](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [airspeed_of_unladen_swallow](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[If you give a variable an illegal name, you get a syntax error:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[>>> 76trombones =](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [big parade](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [SyntaxError: invalid syntax](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [>>> more@ = 1000000](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [SyntaxError: invalid syntax](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> class =](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [Advanced Theoretical Zymurgy](http://www.thinkpython.com/) ['](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [SyntaxError: invalid syntax](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[76trombones](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [is illegal because it begins with a number.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [more@](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) is illegal because it contains [an illegal character,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [@](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [. But what’s wrong with](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [class](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [?](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[It turns out that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [class](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [is one of Python’s](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [keywords](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [The interpreter uses keywords to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [recognize the structure of the program, and they cannot be used as variable names.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[Python 3 has these keywords:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

| False | class    | finally | is       | return |
| ----- | -------- | ------- | -------- | ------ |
| None  | continue | for     | lambda   | try    |
| True  | def      | from    | nonlocal | while  |
| and   | del      | global  | not      | with   |
| as    | elif     | if      | or       | yield  |

[You don’t have to memorize this list. In most development environments, keywords are](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [displayed in a different color; if you try to use one as a variable name, you’ll know.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [2.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Expressions and statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[An](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [expression](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [is a combination of values, variables, and operators. A value all by itself is](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) considered an expression, and so is a variable, so the following are all legal expressions:

>>> 42

>>> n

[>>> n + 25](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[When you type an expression at the prompt, the interpreter](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [evaluates](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [it, which means that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [it finds the value of the expression. In this example,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [has the value 17 and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [n + 25](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [has the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [value 42.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

A [statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [is a unit of code that has an effect, like creating a variable or displaying a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) value.

[>>> n = 17](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [>>> print(n)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[2.4. Script mode](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The first line is an assignment statement that gives a value to](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [. The second line is a print](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [statement that displays the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[When you type a statement, the interpreter](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [executes](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [it, which means that it does whatever](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [the statement says. In general, statements don’t have values.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [2.4](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Script mode](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[So far we have run Python in](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [interactive mode](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [, which means that you interact directly](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py) [with the interpreter. Interactive mode is a good way to get started, but if you are working](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [with more than a few lines of code, it can be clumsy.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[The alternative is to save code in a file called a](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [script](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [and then run the interpreter in](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [script](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [mode](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [to execute the script. By convention, Python scripts have names that end with](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [.py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[If you know how to create and run a script on your computer, you are ready to go. Oth-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [erwise I recommend using PythonAnywhere again. I have posted instructions for running](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [in script mode at](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Because Python provides both modes, you can test bits of code in interactive mode before](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [you put them in a script. But there are differences between interactive mode and script](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [mode that can be confusing.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[For example, if you are using Python as a calculator, you might type](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[>>> miles = 26.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [>>> miles * 1.61](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [42.182](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The first line assigns a value to](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [miles](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [, but it has no visible effect. The second line is an ex-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [pression, so the interpreter evaluates it and displays the result. It turns out that a marathon](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is about 42 kilometers.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[But if you type the same code into a script and run it, you get no output at all. In script](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [mode an expression, all by itself, has no visible effect. Python evaluates the expression, but](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [it doesn’t display the result. To display the result, you need a](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [print](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [statement like this:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[miles = 26.2](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [print(miles * 1.61)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

This behavior can be confusing at first. To check your understanding, type the following [statements in the Python interpreter and see what they do:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[x = 5](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x + 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Now put the same statements in a script and run it. What is the output? Modify the script](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [by transforming each expression into a print statement and then run it again.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

#### [2.5](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [Order of operations](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[When an expression contains more than one operator, the order of evaluation depends](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [on the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [order of operations](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [For mathematical operators, Python follows mathematical](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [convention. The acronym](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [PEMDAS](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is a useful way to remember the rules:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 2. Variables, expressions and statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

- [•](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [P](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [arentheses have the highest precedence and can be used to force an expression to](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [evaluate in the order you want. Since expressions in parentheses are evaluated first,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [2 * (3-1)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is 4, and](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [(1+1)**(5-2)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [is 8.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [You can also use parentheses to make an](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [expression easier to read, as in](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [(minute * 100) / 60](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [, even if it doesn’t change the](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [result.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

- E [xponentiation has the next highest precedence, so](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [1 + 2**3](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [is 9, not 27, and](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [2 *](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [3**2](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [is 18, not 36.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

- [•](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [M](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [ultiplication and](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [D](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [ivision have higher precedence than](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [A](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [ddition and](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [S](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [ubtraction.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) So [2*3-1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [is 5, not 4, and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [6+4/2](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [is 8, not 5.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

- [• Operators with the same precedence are evaluated from left to right (except exponen-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [tiation). So in the expression](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [degrees / 2 * pi](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [, the division happens first and the](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [result is multiplied by](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) pi . To divide by 2 π [, you can use parentheses or write](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [degrees](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [/ 2 / pi](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[I don’t work very hard to remember the precedence of operators. If I can’t tell by looking](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [at the expression, I use parentheses to make it obvious.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

#### [2.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [String operations](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[In general, you can’t perform mathematical operations on strings, even if the strings look](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [like numbers, so the following are illegal:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [chinese](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [food](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [eggs](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [/](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [easy](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ' third ' * ' a charm '

[But there are two exceptions,](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [*](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

The [+](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [operator performs](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [string concatenation](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [, which means it joins the strings by linking](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [them end-to-end. For example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> first =](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [throat](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> second =](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [warbler](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [>>> first + second](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [throatwarbler](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

The * operator also works on strings; it performs repetition. For example, ' Spam ' *3 is ' SpamSpamSpam ' [. If one of the values is a string, the other has to be an integer.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[This use of](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [+](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [and](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [*](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [makes sense by analogy with addition and multiplication. Just as](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [4*3](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [is equivalent to](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [4+4+4](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [, we expect](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [*3](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [to be the same as](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ' + ' Spam ' , and [it is. On the other hand, there is a significant way in which string concatenation and repe-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [tition are different from integer addition and multiplication. Can you think of a property](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [that addition has that string concatenation does not?](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

#### [2.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [Comments](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[As programs get bigger and more complicated, they get more difficult to read.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Formal](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [languages are dense, and it is often difficult to look at a piece of code and figure out what](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [it is doing, or why.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[2.8. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[For this reason, it is a good idea to add notes to your programs to explain in natural lan-](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [guage what the program is doing. These notes are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [comments](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [, and they start with](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [symbol:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [# compute the percentage of the hour that has elapsed](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [percentage = (minute * 100) / 60](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [In this case, the comment appears on a line by itself. You can also put comments at the end](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [of a line:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [percentage = (minute * 100) / 60](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [# percentage of an hour](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [Everything from the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [to the end of the line is ignored—it has no effect on the execution of](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [the program.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Comments are most useful when they document non-obvious features of the code. It is](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [reasonable to assume that the reader can figure out](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [what](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [the code does; it is more useful to](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [explain](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [why](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[This comment is redundant with the code and useless:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [v = 5](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [# assign 5 to v](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [This comment contains useful information that is not in the code:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [v = 5](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [# velocity in meters/second.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [Good variable names can reduce the need for comments, but long names can make com-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [plex expressions hard to read, so there is a tradeoff.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [2.8](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Debugging](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Three kinds of errors can occur in a program: syntax errors, runtime errors, and semantic](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [errors. It is useful to distinguish between them in order to track them down more quickly.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Syntax error:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [“Syntax” refers to the structure of a program and the rules about that struc-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [ture. For example, parentheses have to come in matching pairs, so](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [(1 + 2)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is legal,](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [but](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [8)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is a](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [syntax error](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py)

[If there is a syntax error anywhere in your program, Python displays an error mes-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [sage and quits, and you will not be able to run the program. During the first few](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [weeks of your programming career, you might spend a lot of time tracking down](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [syntax errors.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) As you gain experience, you will make fewer errors and find them faster.

[Runtime error:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [The second type of error is a runtime error, so called because the error does](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [not appear until after the program has started running. These errors are also called](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [exceptions](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [because they usually indicate that something exceptional (and bad) has](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [happened.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Runtime errors are rare in the simple programs you will see in the first few chapters,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [so it might be a while before you encounter one.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Semantic error:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [The third type of error is “semantic”, which means related to meaning.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [If there is a semantic error in your program, it will run without generating error](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [messages, but it will not do the right thing. It will do something else. Specifically, it](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [will do what you told it to do.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Identifying semantic errors can be tricky because it requires you to work backward](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [by looking at the output of the program and trying to figure out what it is doing.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 2. Variables, expressions and statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [2.9](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[variable:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A name that refers to a value.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[assignment:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [A statement that assigns a value to a variable.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

state diagram: [A graphical representation of a set of variables and the values they refer to.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[keyword:](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [A reserved word that is used to parse a program; you cannot use keywords like](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) if , [def](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [while](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [as variable names.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[operand:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [One of the values on which an operator operates.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[expression:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [A combination of variables, operators, and values that represents a single re-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [sult.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[evaluate:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [To simplify an expression by performing the operations in order to yield a single](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [value.](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[statement:](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [A section of code that represents a command or action. So far, the statements](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [we have seen are assignments and print statements.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[execute:](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [To run a statement and do what it says.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[interactive mode:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [A way of using the Python interpreter by typing code at the prompt.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[script mode:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A way of using the Python interpreter to read code from a script and run it.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[script:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [A program stored in a file.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[order of operations:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [Rules governing the order in which expressions involving multiple](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [operators and operands are evaluated.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[concatenate:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [To join two operands end-to-end.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[comment:](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [Information in a program that is meant for other programmers (or anyone read-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [ing the source code) and has no effect on the execution of the program.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[syntax error:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [An error in a program that makes it impossible to parse (and therefore im-](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) possible to interpret).

exception: [An error that is detected while the program is running.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[semantics:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [The meaning of a program.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[semantic error:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [An error in a program that makes it do something other than what the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [programmer intended.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

#### [2.10](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [Exercises](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Exercise 2.1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Repeating my advice from the previous chapter, whenever you learn a new feature,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [you should try it out in interactive mode and make errors on purpose to see what goes wrong.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

- [We’ve seen that](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [n = 42](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is legal. What about](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [42 = n](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [?](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py)

- [•](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [How about](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x = y = 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [?](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

- [•](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [In some languages every statement ends with a semi-colon,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [;](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [. What happens if you put a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [semi-colon at the end of a Python statement?](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

- [•](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [What if you put a period at the end of a statement?](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)
