1. [1. The volume of a sphere with radius r is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [π](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [r](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [. What is the volume of a sphere with radius 5?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

2. [2. Suppose the cover price of a book is $24.95, but bookstores get a 40% discount. Shipping costs](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [$3 for the first copy and 75 cents for each additional copy. What is the total wholesale cost for](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [60 copies?](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

3. [3. If I leave my house at 6:52 am and run 1 mile at an easy pace (8:15 per mile), then 3 miles at](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [tempo (7:12 per mile) and 1 mile at easy pace again, what time do I get home for breakfast?](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Chapter 2. Variables, expressions and statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

## [Chapter 3](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[In the context of programming, a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [function](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is a named sequence of statements that performs](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [a computation. When you define a function, you specify the name and the sequence of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [statements. Later, you can “call” the function by name.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

#### [3.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Function calls](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[We have already seen one example of a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [function call](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> type(42)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [<class](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [int](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The name of the function is](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [type](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [. The expression in parentheses is called the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [argument](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the function. The result, for this function, is the type of the argument.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[It is common to say that a function “takes” an argument and “returns” a result. The result](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is also called the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [return value](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py)

[Python provides functions that convert values from one type to another. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [int](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [function](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [takes any value and converts it to an integer, if it can, or complains otherwise:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[>>> int(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ' )

[>>> int(](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Hello](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [ValueError: invalid literal for int(): Hello](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[int](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [can convert floating-point values to integers, but it doesn’t round off; it chops off the](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [fraction part:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> int(3.99999)](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[>>> int(-2.3)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [-2](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[float](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [converts integers and strings to floating-point numbers:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> float(32)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [32.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> float(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [3.14159](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [3.14159](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 3. Functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Finally,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [str](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [converts its argument to a string:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[>>> str(32)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [>>> str(3.14159)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ' [3.14159](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [3.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Math functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[Python has a math module that provides most of the familiar mathematical functions. A](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [module](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [is a file that contains a collection of related functions.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Before we can use the functions in a module, we have to import it with an](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) import state- ment :

[>>> import math](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[This statement creates a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [module object](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [named math. If you display the module object, you](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [get some information about it:](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[>>> math](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [<module](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [math](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [(built-in)>](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The module object contains the functions and variables defined in the module. To access](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [one of the functions, you have to specify the name of the module and the name of the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [function, separated by a dot (also known as a period). This format is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) dot notation .

[>>> ratio = signal_power / noise_power](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> decibels = 10 * math.log10(ratio)](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[>>> radians = 0.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> height = math.sin(radians)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[The first example uses](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [math.log10](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [to compute a signal-to-noise ratio in decibels (assuming](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [signal_power](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [noise_power](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [are defined).](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [The math module also provides](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [log](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [which computes logarithms base](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [e](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

The second example finds the sine of radians . The variable name radians is a hint that sin [and the other trigonometric functions (](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [cos](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [tan](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, etc.) take arguments in radians. To](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [convert from degrees to radians, divide by 180 and multiply by](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [π](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> degrees = 45](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [>>> radians = degrees / 180.0 * math.pi](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> math.sin(radians)](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [0.707106781187](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py)

[The expression](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [math.pi](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [gets the variable](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [pi](https://thinkpython.com/code/inlist.py) [from the math module. Its value is a floating-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [point approximation of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [π](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [, accurate to about 15 digits.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[If you know trigonometry, you can check the previous result by comparing it to the square](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [root of two, divided by two:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> math.sqrt(2) / 2.0](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [0.707106781187](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[3.3. Composition](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [3.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Composition](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[So far,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [we have looked at the elements of a program—variables,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [expressions,](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sorting_algorithm) [statements—in isolation, without talking about how to combine them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[One of the most useful features of programming languages is their ability to take small](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [building blocks and](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [compose](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [them. For example, the argument of a function can be any](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [kind of expression, including arithmetic operators:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[x = math.sin(degrees / 360.0 * 2 * math.pi)](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[And even function calls:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[x = math.exp(math.log(x+1))](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Almost anywhere you can put a value, you can put an arbitrary expression, with one ex-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [ception: the left side of an assignment statement has to be a variable name.](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [Any other](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [expression on the left side is a syntax error (we will see exceptions to this rule later).](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

| >>> minutes = hours * 60 | # right  |
| ------------------------ | -------- |
| >>> hours * 60 = minutes | # wrong! |

#### [3.4](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Adding new functions](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[So far, we have only been using the functions that come with Python, but it is also possible](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [to add new functions. A](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [function definition](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [specifies the name of a new function and the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [sequence of statements that run when the function is called.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Here is an example:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def print_lyrics():](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [print("I](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [m a lumberjack, and I](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [m okay.")](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [print("I sleep all night and I work all day.")](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is a keyword that indicates that this is a function definition. The name of the function](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [print_lyrics](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [. The rules for function names are the same as for variable names: letters,](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [numbers and underscore are legal, but the first character can’t be a number. You can’t use a](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [keyword as the name of a function, and you should avoid having a variable and a function](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [with the same name.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

The empty parentheses after the name indicate that this function doesn’t take any argu- ments.

[The first line of the function definition is called the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [header](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [; the rest is called the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [body](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [. The](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [header has to end with a colon and the body has to be indented. By convention, indentation](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is always four spaces. The body can contain any number of statements.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The strings in the print statements are enclosed in double quotes. Single quotes and double](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [quotes do the same thing; most people use single quotes except in cases like this where a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [single quote (which is also an apostrophe) appears in the string.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[All quotation marks (single and double) must be “straight quotes”, usually located next](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [to Enter on the keyboard. “Curly quotes”, like the ones in this sentence, are not legal in](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Python.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[If you type a function definition in interactive mode, the interpreter prints dots (](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [...](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [) to let](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [you know that the definition isn’t complete:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 3. Functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> def print_lyrics():](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [...](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print("I](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [m a lumberjack, and I](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [m okay.")](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [...](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [print("I sleep all night and I work all day.")](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [...](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[To end the function, you have to enter an empty line.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Defining a function creates a](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [function object](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [, which has type](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [function](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[>>> print(print_lyrics)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [<function print_lyrics at 0xb7e99e9c>](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [>>> type(print_lyrics)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [<class](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[The syntax for calling the new function is the same as for built-in functions:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[>>> print_lyrics()](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) I ' [m a lumberjack, and I](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [m okay.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [I sleep all night and I work all day.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Once you have defined a function, you can use it inside another function. For example, to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [repeat the previous refrain, we could write a function called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [repeat_lyrics](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py)

[def repeat_lyrics():](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [print_lyrics()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [print_lyrics()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[And then call](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [repeat_lyrics](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[>>> repeat_lyrics()](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [I](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [m a lumberjack, and I](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [m okay.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [I sleep all night and I work all day.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) I ' [m a lumberjack, and I](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [m okay.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [I sleep all night and I work all day.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[But that’s not really how the song goes.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [3.5](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Definitions and uses](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Pulling together the code fragments from the previous section, the whole program looks](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) like this:

[def print_lyrics():](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [print("I](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [m a lumberjack, and I](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [m okay.")](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [print("I sleep all night and I work all day.")](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[def repeat_lyrics():](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [print_lyrics()](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [print_lyrics()](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[repeat_lyrics()](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[This program contains two function definitions:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [print_lyrics](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [repeat_lyrics](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [. Func-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [tion definitions get executed just like other statements, but the effect is to create function](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [objects. The statements inside the function do not run until the function is called, and the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [function definition generates no output.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[3.6. Flow of execution](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[As you might expect, you have to create a function before you can run it. In other words,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [the function definition has to run before the function gets called.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[As an exercise, move the last line of this program to the top, so the function call appears](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [before the definitions. Run the program and see what error message you get.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Now move the function call back to the bottom and move the definition of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [print_lyrics](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [after the definition of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [repeat_lyrics](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [. What happens when you run this program?](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

#### [3.6](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [Flow of execution](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[To ensure that a function is defined before its first use, you have to know the order state-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [ments run in, which is called the](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [flow of execution](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Execution always begins at the first statement of the program. Statements are run one at a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [time, in order from top to bottom.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Function definitions do not alter the flow of execution of the program, but remember that](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [statements inside the function don’t run until the function is called.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[A function call is like a detour in the flow of execution. Instead of going to the next state-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [ment, the flow jumps to the body of the function, runs the statements there, and then comes](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [back to pick up where it left off.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[That sounds simple enough, until you remember that one function can call another. While](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [in the middle of one function, the program might have to run the statements in another](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [function. Then, while running that new function, the program might have to run yet an-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [other function!](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Fortunately, Python is good at keeping track of where it is, so each time a function com-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [pletes, the program picks up where it left off in the function that called it. When it gets to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the end of the program, it terminates.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[In summary, when you read a program, you don’t always want to read from top to bottom.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [Sometimes it makes more sense if you follow the flow of execution.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

#### 3.7 [Parameters and arguments](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Some of the functions we have seen require arguments.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [For example, when you call](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [math.sin](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [you pass a number as an argument.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Some functions take more than one ar-](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [gument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [math.pow](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [takes two, the base and the exponent.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Inside the function, the arguments are assigned to variables called](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [parameters](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [. Here is a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [definition for a function that takes an argument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def print_twice(bruce):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [print(bruce)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [print(bruce)](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[This function assigns the argument to a parameter named](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [bruce](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [When the function is](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [called, it prints the value of the parameter (whatever it is) twice.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[This function works with any value that can be printed.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 3. Functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> print_twice(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [>>> print_twice(42)](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[>>> print_twice(math.pi)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [3.14159265359](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [3.14159265359](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[The same rules of composition that apply to built-in functions also apply to programmer-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [defined functions, so we can use any kind of expression as an argument for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [print_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book1.py) [:](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.format)

[>>> print_twice(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ' *4) [Spam Spam Spam Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Spam Spam Spam Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [>>> print_twice(math.cos(math.pi))](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [-1.0](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) -1.0

[The argument is evaluated before the function is called, so in the examples the expressions](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [Spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [*4](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [math.cos(math.pi)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [are only evaluated once.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[You can also use a variable as an argument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[>>> michael =](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1_soln.py) [Eric, the half a bee.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> print_twice(michael)](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [Eric, the half a bee.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Eric, the half a bee.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The name of the variable we pass as an argument (](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [michael](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [) has nothing to do with the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [name of the parameter (](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [bruce](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [). It doesn’t matter what the value was called back home (in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the caller); here in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, we call everybody](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [bruce](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py)

#### [3.8](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Variables and parameters are local](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

When you create a variable inside a function, it is local , which means that it only exists inside the function. For example:

[def cat_twice(part1, part2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [cat = part1 + part2](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [print_twice(cat)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[This function takes two arguments, concatenates them, and prints the result twice. Here is](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [an example that uses it:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[>>> line1 =](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Bing tiddle](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [>>> line2 =](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [tiddle bang.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [>>> cat_twice(line1, line2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [Bing tiddle tiddle bang.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Bing tiddle tiddle bang.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

When [cat_twice](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [terminates, the variable](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [cat](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is destroyed. If we try to print it, we get an](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [exception:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[3.9. Stack diagrams](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

| line1 | ’Bing tiddle ’ |
| ----- | -------------- |
| line2 | ’tiddle bang.’ |

[part1](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [’Bing tiddle ’](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[cat_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [part2](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [’tiddle bang.’](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[cat](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [’Bing tiddle tiddle bang.’](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[print_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) bruce [’Bing tiddle tiddle bang.’](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[Figure 3.1: Stack diagram.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>> print(cat)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [NameError: name](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [cat](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [is not defined](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Parameters are also local.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [For example, outside](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [print_twice](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [, there is no such thing as](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [bruce](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

#### [3.9](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Stack diagrams](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[To keep track of which variables can be used where, it is sometimes useful to draw a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [stack](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [diagram](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [. Like state diagrams, stack diagrams show the value of each variable, but they](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [also show the function each variable belongs to.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Each function is represented by a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) frame . [A frame is a box with the name of a function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [beside it and the parameters and variables of the function inside it. The stack diagram for](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the previous example is shown in Figure 3.1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The frames are arranged in a stack that indicates which function called which, and so](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [on.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [In this example,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [print_twice](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [was called by](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [cat_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [, and](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [cat_twice](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [was called](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [by](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [__main__](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, which is a special name for the topmost frame. When you create a variable](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [outside of any function, it belongs to](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [__main__](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Each parameter refers to the same value as its corresponding argument. So,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [part1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [has the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [same value as](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) line1 , part2 has the same value as line2 , and bruce has the same value as cat .

[If an error occurs during a function call, Python prints the name of the function, the name](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [of the function that called it, and the name of the function that called](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) that [, all the way back](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [__main__](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[For example, if you try to access](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [cat](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [from within](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [print_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, you get a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [NameError](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[Traceback (innermost last):](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [File "test.py", line 13, in __main__](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [cat_twice(line1, line2)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [File "test.py", line 5, in cat_twice](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [print_twice(cat)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [File "test.py", line 9, in print_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [print(cat)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [NameError: name](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [cat](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is not defined](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 3. Functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This list of functions is called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [traceback](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [. It tells you what program file the error occurred](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [in, and what line, and what functions were executing at the time. It also shows the line of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [code that caused the error.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[The order of the functions in the traceback is the same as the order of the frames in the](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [stack diagram. The function that is currently running is at the bottom.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [3.10](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Fruitful functions and void functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[Some of the functions we have used, such as the math functions, return results; for lack of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [a better name, I call them](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [fruitful functions](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [. Other functions, like](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [print_twice](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [, perform](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [an action but don’t return a value. They are called](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [void functions](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[When you call a fruitful function, you almost always want to do something with the result;](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [for example, you might assign it to a variable or use it as part of an expression:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[x = math.cos(radians)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [golden = (math.sqrt(5) + 1) / 2](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[When you call a function in interactive mode, Python displays the result:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[>>> math.sqrt(5)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [2.2360679774997898](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[But in a script, if you call a fruitful function all by itself, the return value is lost forever!](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[math.sqrt(5)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[This script computes the square root of 5, but since it doesn’t store or display the result, it](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is not very useful.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[Void functions might display something on the screen or have some other effect, but they](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [don’t have a return value. If you assign the result to a variable, you get a special value](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [called](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [None](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> result = print_twice(](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [Bing](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ' ) [Bing](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Bing](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [>>> print(result)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) None

The value None [is not the same as the string](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. It is a special value that has its own](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) type:

[>>> type(None)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) <class ' [NoneType](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The functions we have written so far are all void. We will start writing fruitful functions in](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [a few chapters.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

#### 3.11 [Why functions?](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[It may not be clear why it is worth the trouble to divide a program into functions. There](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [are several reasons:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[3.12. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

- [• Creating a new function gives you an opportunity to name a group of statements,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [which makes your program easier to read and debug.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• Functions can make a program smaller by eliminating repetitive code. Later, if you](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [make a change, you only have to make it in one place.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

- [• Dividing a long program into functions allows you to debug the parts one at a time](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [and then assemble them into a working whole.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

- [• Well-designed functions are often useful for many programs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [Once you write and](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [debug one, you can reuse it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

#### [3.12](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[One of the most important skills you will acquire is debugging. Although it can be frus-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [trating, debugging is one of the most intellectually rich, challenging, and interesting parts](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [of programming.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[In some ways debugging is like detective work. You are confronted with clues and you](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [have to infer the processes and events that led to the results you see.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[Debugging is also like an experimental science. Once you have an idea about what is going](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [wrong, you modify your program and try again. If your hypothesis was correct, you can](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [predict the result of the modification, and you take a step closer to a working program. If](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [your hypothesis was wrong, you have to come up with a new one. As Sherlock Holmes](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [pointed out, “When you have eliminated the impossible, whatever remains, however im-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [probable, must be the truth.” (A. Conan Doyle,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [The Sign of Four](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[For some people, programming and debugging are the same thing. That is, programming](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is the process of gradually debugging a program until it does what you want. The idea is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [that you should start with a working program and make small modifications, debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [them as you go.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[For example, Linux is an operating system that contains millions of lines of code, but it](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [started out as a simple program Linus Torvalds used to explore the Intel 80386 chip. Ac-](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [cording to Larry Greenfield, “One of Linus’s earlier projects was a program that would](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) switch between printing AAAA and BBBB. This later evolved to Linux.” ( The Linux Users’ Guide [Beta Version 1).](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

#### [3.13](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[function:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [A named sequence of statements that performs some useful operation. Func-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [tions may or may not take arguments and may or may not produce a result.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[function definition:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [A statement that creates a new function, specifying its name, param-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [eters, and the statements it contains.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[function object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [A value created by a function definition. The name of the function is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [variable that refers to a function object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[header:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [The first line of a function definition.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 3. Functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[body:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The sequence of statements inside a function definition.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[parameter:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A name used inside a function to refer to the value passed as an argument.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[function call:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [A statement that runs a function. It consists of the function name followed](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [by an argument list in parentheses.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[argument:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [A value provided to a function when the function is called. This value is as-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [signed to the corresponding parameter in the function.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[local variable:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [A variable defined inside a function.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [A local variable can only be used](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [inside its function.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[return value:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [The result of a function. If a function call is used as an expression, the return](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [value is the value of the expression.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[fruitful function:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [A function that returns a value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[void function:](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [A function that always returns](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [None](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

None : [A special value returned by void functions.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

module: [A file that contains a collection of related functions and other definitions.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[import statement:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [A statement that reads a module file and creates a module object.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[module object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [A value created by an](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [import](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [statement that provides access to the values](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [defined in a module.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[dot notation:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [The syntax for calling a function in another module by specifying the mod-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [ule name followed by a dot (period) and the function name.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[composition:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Using an expression as part of a larger expression, or a statement as part of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a larger statement.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[flow of execution:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The order statements run in.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[stack diagram:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [A graphical representation of a stack of functions, their variables, and the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [values they refer to.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

frame: [A box in a stack diagram that represents a function call. It contains the local vari-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ables and parameters of the function.

traceback: [A list of the functions that are executing, printed when an exception occurs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> right_justify(](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ' monty ' [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [monty](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

Also, Python provides a built-in function called](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [len](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [that returns the length of a string, so the value of](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [len(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [monty](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [is 5.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture)

For](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [do_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is a function that takes a function object as an argument and calls it twice:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def do_twice(f):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [f()](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [f()](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Here’s an example that uses](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [do_twice](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [to call a function named](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [print_spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [twice.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[def print_spam():](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[do_twice(print_spam)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

| |   | |   | |   |

[Hint: to print more than one value on a line, you can print a comma-separated sequence of](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [values:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[print(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [+](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[By default,](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [advances to the next line, but you can override that behavior and put a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [space at the end, like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[print(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [+](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, end=](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [' '](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [print(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

The output from the next print](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [statement would begin on the next line.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)
