## [Chapter 4](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Case study: interface design](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[This chapter presents a case study that demonstrates a process for designing functions that](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [work together.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[It introduces the](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [turtle](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [module, which allows you to create images using turtle graphics.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [turtle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [module is included in most Python installations, but if you are running Python](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [using PythonAnywhere, you won’t be able to run the turtle examples (at least you couldn’t](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [when I wrote this).](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[If you have already installed Python on your computer, you should be able to run the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [examples. Otherwise, now is a good time to install. I have posted instructions at](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [http:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [//tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Code examples from this chapter are available from](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [polygon.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [4.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [The turtle module](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[To check whether you have the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) turtle module, open the Python interpreter and type

>>> import turtle [>>> bob = turtle.Turtle()](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[When you run this code, it should create a new window with small arrow that represents](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [the turtle. Close the window.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Create a file named](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [mypolygon.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and type in the following code:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[import turtle](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [bob = turtle.Turtle()](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [print(bob)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [turtle.mainloop()](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [turtle](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [module (with a lowercase ’t’) provides a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Turtle](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [(with an up-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [percase ’T’) that creates a Turtle object, which we assign to a variable named](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [bob](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [. Printing](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [bob](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [displays something like:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[<turtle.Turtle object at 0xb7bfbf4c>](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 4. Case study: interface design](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This means that](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [bob](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [refers to an object with type](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Turtle](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [as defined in module](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [turtle](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py)

[mainloop](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [tells the window to wait for the user to do something, although in this case](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [there’s not much for the user to do except close the window.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[Once you create a Turtle, you can call a](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [method](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [to move it around the window. A method](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [is similar to a function, but it uses slightly different syntax. For example, to move the turtle](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [forward:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[bob.fd(100)](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt)

[The method,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [fd](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [, is associated with the turtle object we’re calling](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [bob](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py) [. Calling a method is](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [like making a request: you are asking](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [bob](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [to move forward.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[The argument of](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [fd](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [is a distance in pixels, so the actual size depends on your display.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Other methods you can call on a Turtle are](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) bk to move backward, lt for left turn, and rt [right turn. The argument for](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [lt](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [rt](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [is an angle in degrees.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Also, each Turtle is holding a pen, which is either down or up; if the pen is down, the Turtle](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [leaves a trail when it moves. The methods](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [pu](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [pd](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [stand for “pen up” and “pen down”.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[To draw a right angle, add these lines to the program (after creating](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [bob](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [and before calling](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [mainloop](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[bob.fd(100)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [bob.lt(90)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [bob.fd(100)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[When you run this program, you should see](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) bob move east and then north, leaving two [line segments behind.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Now modify the program to draw a square. Don’t go on until you’ve got it working!](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [4.2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Simple repetition](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Chances are you wrote something like this:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[bob.fd(100)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) bob.lt(90)

bob.fd(100) [bob.lt(90)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[bob.fd(100)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [bob.lt(90)](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[bob.fd(100)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[We can do the same thing more concisely with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [statement.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [Add this example to](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [mypolygon.py](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [and run it again:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[for i in range(4):](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [print(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Hello!](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[You should see something like this:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[4.3. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Hello!](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Hello!](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Hello!](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Hello!](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [This is the simplest use of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [statement; we will see more later. But that should be](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [enough to let you rewrite your square-drawing program. Don’t go on until you do.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Here is a](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [for](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [statement that draws a square:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [for i in range(4):](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [bob.fd(100)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [bob.lt(90)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [The syntax of a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [statement is similar to a function definition. It has a header that ends](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [with a colon and an indented body. The body can contain any number of statements.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[A](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [statement is also called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) loop because the flow of execution runs through the body [and then loops back to the top. In this case, it runs the body four times.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[This version is actually a little different from the previous square-drawing code because it](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [makes another turn after drawing the last side of the square. The extra turn takes more](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [time, but it simplifies the code if we do the same thing every time through the loop. This](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [version also has the effect of leaving the turtle back in the starting position, facing in the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [starting direction.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [4.3](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[The following is a series of exercises using the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) turtle [module. They are meant to be fun,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [but they have a point, too. While you are working on them, think about what the point is.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The following sections have solutions to the exercises, so don’t look until you have finished](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [(or at least tried).](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

1. [1. Write a function called](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [square](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [that takes a parameter named](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [t](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, which is a turtle. It](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [should use the turtle to draw a square.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Write a function call that passes](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [bob](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [as an argument to](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [square](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [, and then run the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [program again.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

2. [2. Add another parameter, named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) length , to square . Modify the body so length of the sides is length , and then modify the function call to provide a second argument. Run [the program again. Test your program with a range of values for](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [length](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

3. [3. Make a copy of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [square](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and change the name to](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [polygon](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [. Add another parameter](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [named](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and modify the body so it draws an n-sided regular polygon. Hint: The](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [exterior angles of an n-sided regular polygon are 360/](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](http://gutenberg.org/) [degrees.](http://gutenberg.org/)

4. [4. Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [circle](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [that takes a turtle,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, and radius,](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [r](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [, as parameters and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [that draws an approximate circle by calling](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [polygon](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [with an appropriate length and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [number of sides. Test your function with a range of values of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [r](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py)

[Hint: figure out the circumference of the circle and make sure that](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [length * n =](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [circumference](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d)

5. [5. Make a more general version of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [circle](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [called](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [arc](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [that takes an additional parameter](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [angle](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, which determines what fraction of a circle to draw.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [angle](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [is in units of degrees,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [so when](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [angle=360](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [arc](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [should draw a complete circle.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 4. Case study: interface design](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [4.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Encapsulation](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[The first exercise asks you to put your square-drawing code into a function definition and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [then call the function, passing the turtle as a parameter. Here is a solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[def square(t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) for i in range(4): [t.fd(100)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [t.lt(90)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[square(bob)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

[The innermost statements,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [fd](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [lt](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [are indented twice to show that they are inside the](http://www.thinkpython.com/) for [loop, which is inside the function definition. The next line,](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [square(bob)](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [, is flush with](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [the left margin, which indicates the end of both the](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [for](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [loop and the function definition.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Inside the function,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [refers to the same turtle](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [bob](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [, so](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [t.lt(90)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [has the same effect as](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [bob.lt(90)](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [. In that case, why not call the parameter](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [bob](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [? The idea is that](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [t](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [can be any](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [turtle, not just](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [bob](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [, so you could create a second turtle and pass it as an argument to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [square](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[alice = turtle.Turtle()](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [square(alice)](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[Wrapping a piece of code up in a function is called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [encapsulation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [. One of the benefits of](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [encapsulation is that it attaches a name to the code, which serves as a kind of documenta-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [tion. Another advantage is that if you re-use the code, it is more concise to call a function](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [twice than to copy and paste the body!](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### 4.5 [Generalization](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The next step is to add a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [length](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [parameter to](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [square](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [. Here is a solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py)

[def square(t, length):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [for i in range(4):](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [t.fd(length)](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [t.lt(90)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

square(bob, 100)

[Adding a parameter to a function is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [generalization](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [because it makes the function](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [more general: in the previous version, the square is always the same size; in this version it](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [can be any size.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The next step is also a generalization. Instead of drawing squares,](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [polygon](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [draws regular](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [polygons with any number of sides. Here is a solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def polygon(t, n, length):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [angle = 360 / n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [for i in range(n):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [t.fd(length)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [t.lt(angle)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[polygon(bob, 7, 70)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[4.6. Interface design](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This example draws a 7-sided polygon with side length 70.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[If you are using Python 2, the value of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [angle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [might be off because of integer division. A](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [simple solution is to compute](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [angle = 360.0 / n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [. Because the numerator is a floating-](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [point number, the result is floating point.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[When a function has more than a few numeric arguments, it is easy to forget what they are,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [or what order they should be in. In that case it is often a good idea to include the names of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [the parameters in the argument list:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[polygon(bob, n=7, length=70)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[These are called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [keyword arguments](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [because they include the parameter names as “key-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [words” (not to be confused with Python keywords like](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [while](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [def](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [).](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py)

[This syntax makes the program more readable. It is also a reminder about how arguments](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [and parameters work: when you call a function, the arguments are assigned to the param-](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [eters.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

#### [4.6](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Interface design](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[The next step is to write](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [circle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, which takes a radius,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [r](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [, as a parameter. Here is a simple](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [solution that uses](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [polygon](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [to draw a 50-sided polygon:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[import math](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[def circle(t, r):](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [circumference = 2 * math.pi * r](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [n = 50](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [length = circumference / n](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [polygon(t, n, length)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The first line computes the circumference of a circle with radius](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [r](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [using the formula 2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) π r . [Since we use](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [math.pi](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, we have to import](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [math](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [By convention,](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [import](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [statements are](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [usually at the beginning of the script.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[n](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [is the number of line segments in our approximation of a circle, so](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [length](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [is the length](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [of each segment. Thus,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [polygon](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [draws a 50-sided polygon that approximates a circle with](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) radius r .

[One limitation of this solution is that](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [is a constant, which means that for very big circles,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [the line segments are too long, and for small circles, we waste time drawing very small](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [segments. One solution would be to generalize the function by taking](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) n [as a parameter.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [This would give the user (whoever calls](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [circle](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [) more control, but the interface would be](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) less clean.

[The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [interface](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [of a function is a summary of how it is used: what are the parameters? What](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [does the function do? And what is the return value? An interface is “clean” if it allows the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [caller to do what they want without dealing with unnecessary details.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[In this example,](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) r [belongs in the interface because it specifies the circle to be drawn.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [is](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [less appropriate because it pertains to the details of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [how](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [the circle should be rendered.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[Rather than clutter up the interface, it is better to choose an appropriate value of](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [n](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [depend-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [ing on](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [circumference](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 4. Case study: interface design](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def circle(t, r):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [circumference = 2 * math.pi * r](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n = int(circumference / 3) + 3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [length = circumference / n](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [polygon(t, n, length)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Now the number of segments is an integer near](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [circumference/3](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [, so the length of each](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [segment is approximately 3, which is small enough that the circles look good, but big](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [enough to be efficient, and acceptable for any size circle.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Adding 3 to](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [guarantees that the polygon has at least 3 sides.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

#### [4.7](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Refactoring](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[When I wrote](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [circle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [, I was able to re-use](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [polygon](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [because a many-sided polygon is a good](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [approximation of a circle. But](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [arc](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [is not as cooperative; we can’t use](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [polygon](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [or](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [circle](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [to](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [draw an arc.](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[One alternative is to start with a copy of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [polygon](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [and transform it into](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [arc](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [The result](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [might look like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[def arc(t, r, angle):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [arc_length = 2 * math.pi * r * angle / 360](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [n = int(arc_length / 3) + 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [step_length = arc_length / n](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [step_angle = angle / n](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[for i in range(n):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [t.fd(step_length)](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [t.lt(step_angle)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The second half of this function looks like](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [polygon](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [, but we can’t re-use](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [polygon](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [without](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [changing the interface. We could generalize](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [polygon](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [to take an angle as a third argument,](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [but then](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [polygon](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [would no longer be an appropriate name!](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [Instead, let’s call the more](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [general function](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [polyline](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[def polyline(t, n, length, angle):](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [for i in range(n):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) t.fd(length) [t.lt(angle)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Now we can rewrite](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [polygon](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [arc](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [to use](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [polyline](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[def polygon(t, n, length):](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [angle = 360.0 / n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [polyline(t, n, length, angle)](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[def arc(t, r, angle):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [arc_length = 2 * math.pi * r * angle / 360](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [n = int(arc_length / 3) + 1](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [step_length = arc_length / n](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [step_angle = float(angle) / n](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [polyline(t, n, step_length, step_angle)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Finally, we can rewrite](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [circle](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [to use](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [arc](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[4.8. A development plan](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def circle(t, r):](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [arc(t, r, 360)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[This process—rearranging a program to improve interfaces and facilitate code re-use—is](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [called](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [refactoring](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [. In this case, we noticed that there was similar code in](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) arc and polygon , [so we “factored it out” into](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [polyline](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[If we had planned ahead, we might have written](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [polyline](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [first and avoided refactoring,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [but often you don’t know enough at the beginning of a project to design all the interfaces.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Once you start coding, you understand the problem better. Sometimes refactoring is a sign](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [that you have learned something.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

#### [4.8](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [A development plan](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[A](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [development plan](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [is a process for writing programs. The process we used in this case](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [study is “encapsulation and generalization”. The steps of this process are:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

1. [1. Start by writing a small program with no function definitions.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

2. [2. Once you get the program working, identify a coherent piece of it, encapsulate the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [piece in a function and give it a name.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

3. [3. Generalize the function by adding appropriate parameters.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

4. [4. Repeat steps 1–3 until you have a set of working functions. Copy and paste working](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [code to avoid retyping (and re-debugging).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

5. [5. Look for opportunities to improve the program by refactoring. For example, if you](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [have similar code in several places, consider factoring it into an appropriately general](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [function.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[This process has some drawbacks—we will see alternatives later—but it can be useful if](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [you don’t know ahead of time how to divide the program into functions. This approach](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [lets you design as you go along.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

#### 4.9 [docstring](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[A](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [docstring](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [is a string at the beginning of a function that explains the interface (“doc” is](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [short for “documentation”). Here is an example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def polyline(t, n, length, angle):](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ["""Draws n line segments with the given length and](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [angle (in degrees) between them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [t is a turtle.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ["""](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [for i in range(n):](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [t.fd(length)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [t.lt(angle)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[By convention, all docstrings are triple-quoted strings, also known as multiline strings](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [because the triple quotes allow the string to span more than one line.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 4. Case study: interface design](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[It is terse, but it contains the essential information someone would need to use this func-](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [tion. It explains concisely what the function does (without getting into the details of how](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [it does it). It explains what effect each parameter has on the behavior of the function and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [what type each parameter should be (if it is not obvious).](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[Writing this kind of documentation is an important part of interface design.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [A well-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [designed interface should be simple to explain; if you have a hard time explaining one](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [of your functions, maybe the interface could be improved.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

#### [4.10](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [Debugging](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[An interface is like a contract between a function and a caller. The caller agrees to provide](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [certain parameters and the function agrees to do certain work.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [polyline](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [requires four arguments:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [has to be a Turtle;](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [has to be an integer;](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [length](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [should be a positive number; and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [angle](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [has to be a number, which is understood](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [to be in degrees.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[These requirements are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [preconditions](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [because they are supposed to be true before](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [the function starts executing. Conversely, conditions at the end of the function are](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [post-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [conditions](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [. Postconditions include the intended effect of the function (like drawing line](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [segments) and any side effects (like moving the Turtle or making other changes).](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Preconditions are the responsibility of the caller.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [If the caller violates a (properly docu-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [mented!) precondition and the function doesn’t work correctly, the bug is in the caller, not](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the function.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[If the preconditions are satisfied and the postconditions are not, the bug is in the function.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [If your pre- and postconditions are clear, they can help with debugging.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [4.11](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[method:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [A function that is associated with an object and called using dot notation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

loop: [A part of a program that can run repeatedly.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

encapsulation: [The process of transforming a sequence of statements into a function defi-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) nition.

[generalization:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [The process of replacing something unnecessarily specific (like a number)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [with something appropriately general (like a variable or parameter).](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[keyword argument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [An argument that includes the name of the parameter as a “key-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [word”.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[interface:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [A description of how to use a function, including the name and descriptions of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [the arguments and return value.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[refactoring:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [The process of modifying a working program to improve function interfaces](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and other qualities of the code.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[development plan:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [A process for writing programs.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[4.12. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Figure 4.1: Turtle flowers.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[Figure 4.2: Turtle pies.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[docstring:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [A string that appears at the top of a function definition to document the func-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [tion’s interface.](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[precondition:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [A requirement that should be satisfied by the caller before a function starts.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[postcondition:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [A requirement that should be satisfied by the function before it ends.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)
