## [Chapter 5](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Conditionals and recursion](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[The main topic of this chapter is the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [if](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [statement, which executes different code depending](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [on the state of the program. But first I want to introduce two new operators: floor division](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [and modulus.](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

#### [5.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Floor division and modulus](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [floor division](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [operator,](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [//](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [, divides two numbers and rounds down to an integer. For](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [example, suppose the run time of a movie is 105 minutes. You might want to know how](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [long that is in hours. Conventional division returns a floating-point number:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> minutes = 105](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> minutes / 60](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [1.75](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[But we don’t normally write hours with decimal points. Floor division returns the integer](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [number of hours, rounding down:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[>>> minutes = 105](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [>>> hours = minutes // 60](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) >>> hours

[To get the remainder, you could subtract off one hour in minutes:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[>>> remainder = minutes - hours * 60](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [>>> remainder](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[An alternative is to use the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [modulus operator](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [%](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [, which divides two numbers and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [the remainder.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[>>> remainder = minutes % 60](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [>>> remainder](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[The modulus operator is more useful than it seems. For example, you can check whether](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [one number is divisible by another—if](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [x % y](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [is zero, then](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [x](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting) [is divisible by](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_db.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_db.py)

[Chapter 5. Conditionals and recursion](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Also, you can extract the right-most digit or digits from a number. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [x % 10](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [yields the right-most digit of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [(in base 10). Similarly](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x % 100](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [yields the last two digits.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[If you are using Python 2, division works differently. The division operator,](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [/](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [, performs](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [floor division if both operands are integers, and floating-point division if either operand is](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) a float .

#### [5.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Boolean expressions](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[A](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [boolean expression](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [is an expression that is either true or false. The following examples](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [use the operator](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [==](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [, which compares two operands and produces](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [True](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [if they are equal](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) and [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [otherwise:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[>>> 5 == 5](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) True [>>> 5 == 6](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [False](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

True and [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [are special values that belong to the type](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [bool](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [; they are not strings:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py)

[>>> type(True)](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [<class](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [bool](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> type(False)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [<class](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [bool](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [==](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [operator is one of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [relational operators](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [; the others are:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

| x != y | # x is not equal to y             |
| ------ | --------------------------------- |
| x > y  | # x is greater than y             |
| x < y  | # x is less than y                |
| x >= y | # x is greater than or equal to y |
| x <= y | # x is less than or equal to y    |

[Although these operations are probably familiar to you, the Python symbols are different](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [from the mathematical symbols. A common error is to use a single equal sign (](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [) instead of](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [a double equal sign (](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [==](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [). Remember that](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [=](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [is an assignment operator and](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [==](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [is a relational](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [operator. There is no such thing as](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [=<](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [or](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [=>](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

#### 5.3 [Logical operators](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[There are three](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [logical operators](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [not](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [The semantics (meaning) of these](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [operators is similar to their meaning in English. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x > 0 and x < 10](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [is true](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) only if x [is greater than 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [less than 10.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[n%2 == 0 or n%3 == 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is true if](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [either or both](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [of the conditions is true, that is, if the number](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is divisible by 2](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [or](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [3.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Finally, the](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [not](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [operator negates a boolean expression, so](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [not (x > y)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [is true if](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [x > y](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [is](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [false, that is, if](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [is less than or equal to](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d)

[Strictly speaking, the operands of the logical operators should be boolean expressions, but](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Python is not very strict. Any nonzero number is interpreted as](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [True](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting) [:](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting)

[5.4. Conditional execution](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> 42 and True](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[This flexibility can be useful, but there are some subtleties to it that might be confusing.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [You might want to avoid it (unless you know what you are doing).](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [5.4](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Conditional execution](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[In order to write useful programs, we almost always need the ability to check conditions](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [and change the behavior of the program accordingly.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [Conditional statements](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [give us this](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [ability. The simplest form is the](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [if](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [statement:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[if x > 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [print(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [x is positive](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[The boolean expression after](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [is called the](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [condition](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [. If it is true, the indented statement](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [runs. If not, nothing happens.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[if](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [statements have the same structure as function definitions: a header followed by an](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [indented body. Statements like this are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [compound statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py)

[There is no limit on the number of statements that can appear in the body, but there has to](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [be at least one. Occasionally, it is useful to have a body with no statements (usually as a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [place keeper for code you haven’t written yet). In that case, you can use the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [pass](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [statement,](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [which does nothing.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[if x < 0:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [pass](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [# TODO: need to handle negative values!](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [5.5](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Alternative execution](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[A second form of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [if](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [statement is “alternative execution”, in which there are two possi-](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [bilities and the condition determines which one runs. The syntax looks like this:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

if x % 2 == 0: print( ' x is even ' ) else: [print(](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [x is odd](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[If the remainder when](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is divided by 2 is 0, then we know that](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [x](http://gutenberg.org/) [is even, and the program](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [displays an appropriate message.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [If the condition is false, the second set of statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [runs. Since the condition must be true or false, exactly one of the alternatives will run. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [alternatives are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [branches](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [, because they are branches in the flow of execution.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

#### [5.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Chained conditionals](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Sometimes there are more than two possibilities and we need more than two branches.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [One way to express a computation like that is a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [chained conditional](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [:](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting)

[Chapter 5. Conditionals and recursion](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[if x < y:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x is less than y](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [elif x > y:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [x is greater than y](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) print( ' [x and y are equal](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[elif](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [is an abbreviation of “else if”. Again, exactly one branch will run. There is no limit on](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [the number of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [elif](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [statements. If there is an](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [else](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [clause, it has to be at the end, but there](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [doesn’t have to be one.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[if choice ==](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [draw_a()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [elif choice ==](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [draw_b()](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [elif choice ==](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [draw_c()](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Each condition is checked in order. If the first is false, the next is checked, and so on. If one](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [of them is true, the corresponding branch runs and the statement ends. Even if more than](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [one condition is true, only the first true branch runs.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

#### [5.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [Nested conditionals](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[One conditional can also be nested within another. We could have written the example in](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the previous section like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[if x == y:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [print(](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [x and y are equal](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) else: [if x < y:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x is less than y](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [else:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [x is greater than y](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ' )

[The outer conditional contains two branches. The first branch contains a simple statement.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [The second branch contains another](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [if](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [statement, which has two branches of its own.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Those two branches are both simple statements, although they could have been conditional](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) statements as well.

[Although the indentation of the statements makes the structure apparent,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [nested condi-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) tionals [become difficult to read very quickly. It is a good idea to avoid them when you](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) can.

[Logical operators often provide a way to simplify nested conditional statements. For ex-](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [ample, we can rewrite the following code using a single conditional:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[if 0 < x:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [if x < 10:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [x is a positive single-digit number.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [)](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

The [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [statement runs only if we make it past both conditionals, so we can get the same](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [effect with the](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [operator:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[if 0 < x and x < 10:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [print(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [x is a positive single-digit number.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture)

[5.8. Recursion](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[For this kind of condition, Python provides a more concise option:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[if 0 < x < 10:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [x is a positive single-digit number.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

#### [5.8](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Recursion](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[It is legal for one function to call another; it is also legal for a function to call itself. It may](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [not be obvious why that is a good thing, but it turns out to be one of the most magical](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [things a program can do. For example, look at the following function:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[def countdown(n):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [if n <= 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [Blastoff!](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [print(n)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [countdown(n-1)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[If](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [is 0 or negative, it outputs the word, “Blastoff!” Otherwise, it outputs](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) n [and then calls](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [a function named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [countdown](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [—itself—passing](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [n-1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [as an argument.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[What happens if we call this function like this?](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[>>> countdown(3)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The execution of](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [countdown](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [begins with](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [n=3](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [, and since](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1_soln.py) [is greater than 0, it outputs the](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1_soln.py) [value 3, and then calls itself...](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The execution of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [countdown](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) begins with n=2 , and since n [is greater than 0, it](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [outputs the value 2, and then calls itself...](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The execution of](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [countdown](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [begins with](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [n=1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, and since](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is greater](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [than 0, it outputs the value 1, and then calls itself...](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The execution of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [countdown](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [begins with](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) n=0 [, and since](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [not greater than 0, it outputs the word, “Blastoff!” and then](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [returns.](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py)

[The](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [countdown](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [that got](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [n=1](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [returns.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[The](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [countdown](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [that got](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [n=2](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [returns.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

The countdown that got n=3 returns.

[And then you’re back in](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [__main__](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. So, the total output looks like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Blastoff!](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[A function that calls itself is](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [recursive](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [; the process of executing it is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [recursion](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[As another example, we can write a function that prints a string](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [n](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [times.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[def print_n(s, n):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [if n <= 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [print(s)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [print_n(s, n-1)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 5. Conditionals and recursion](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[__main__](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[countdown](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[countdown](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[countdown](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

[countdown](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[Figure 5.1: Stack diagram.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[If](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [n <= 0](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [the](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [return statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [exits the function. The flow of execution immediately re-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [turns to the caller, and the remaining lines of the function don’t run.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[The rest of the function is similar to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [countdown](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [: it displays](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [s](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and then calls itself to display](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) s n − [1 additional times. So the number of lines of output is](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [1 + (n - 1)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, which adds up](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) to n .

[For simple examples like this, it is probably easier to use a](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [loop.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [But we will see](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [examples later that are hard to write with a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [loop and easy to write with recursion, so it](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [is good to start early.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [5.9](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [Stack diagrams for recursive functions](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[In Section 3.9, we used a stack diagram to represent the state of a program during a function](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [call. The same kind of diagram can help interpret a recursive function.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Every time a function gets called, Python creates a frame to contain the function’s local](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [variables and parameters. For a recursive function, there might be more than one frame on](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [the stack at the same time.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Figure 5.1 shows a stack diagram for](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [countdown](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [called with](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [n = 3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[As usual, the top of the stack is the frame for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) __main__ . It is empty because we did not create any variables in __main__ or pass any arguments to it.

The four [countdown](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [frames have different values for the parameter](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. The bottom of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [stack, where](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [n=0](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [, is called the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [base case](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. It does not make a recursive call, so there are no](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [more frames.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[As an exercise, draw a stack diagram for](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print_n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [called with](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [s =](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [Hello](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [n=2](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [. Then](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [do_n](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [that takes a function object and a number,](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, as arguments, and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [that calls the given function](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [times.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

#### [5.10](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [Infinite recursion](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[If a recursion never reaches a base case, it goes on making recursive calls forever, and the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [program never terminates. This is known as](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [infinite recursion](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, and it is generally not a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [good idea. Here is a minimal program with an infinite recursion:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[5.11. Keyboard input](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def recurse():](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [recurse()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [In most programming environments, a program with infinite recursion does not really run](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [forever. Python reports an error message when the maximum recursion depth is reached:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [File "<stdin>", line 2, in recurse](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [File "<stdin>", line 2, in recurse](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [File "<stdin>", line 2, in recurse](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [File "<stdin>", line 2, in recurse](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [RuntimeError: Maximum recursion depth exceeded](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [This traceback is a little bigger than the one we saw in the previous chapter. When the error](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [occurs, there are 1000](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) recurse frames on the stack!

[If you encounter an infinite recursion by accident, review your function to confirm that](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [there is a base case that does not make a recursive call. And if there is a base case, check](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [whether you are guaranteed to reach it.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [5.11](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Keyboard input](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The programs we have written so far accept no input from the user. They just do the same](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [thing every time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[Python provides a built-in function called](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) input [that stops the program and waits for the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [user to type something. When the user presses](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) Return or [Enter](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [, the program resumes and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [input](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [returns what the user typed as a string.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [In Python 2, the same function is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [raw_input](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> text = input()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [What are you waiting for?](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> text](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [What are you waiting for?](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ' [Before getting input from the user, it is a good idea to print a prompt telling the user what](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [to type.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [input](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) can take a prompt as an argument: >>> name = input( ' What...is your name?\n ' ) [What...is your name?](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Arthur, King of the Britons!](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> name](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Arthur, King of the Britons!](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [The sequence](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [\n](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [at the end of the prompt represents a](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [newline](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, which is a special character](http://gutenberg.org/) [that causes a line break. That’s why the user’s input appears below the prompt.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[If you expect the user to type an integer, you can try to convert the return value to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [int](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [>>> prompt =](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [What...is the airspeed velocity of an unladen swallow?\n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> speed = input(prompt)](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [What...is the airspeed velocity of an unladen swallow?](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> int(speed)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 5. Conditionals and recursion](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[But if the user types something other than a string of digits, you get an error:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [>>> speed = input(prompt)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [What...is the airspeed velocity of an unladen swallow?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [What do you mean, an African or a European swallow?](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [>>> int(speed)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [We will see how to handle this kind of error later.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

#### 5.12 [Debugging](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[When a syntax or runtime error occurs, the error message contains a lot of information, but](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [it can be overwhelming. The most useful parts are usually:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

- [• What kind of error it was, and](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

- [• Where it occurred.](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[Syntax errors are usually easy to find, but there are a few gotchas. Whitespace errors can](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [be tricky because spaces and tabs are invisible and we are used to ignoring them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [>>> x = 5](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [>>>](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [y = 6](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [File "<stdin>", line 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [y = 6](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [^](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [IndentationError: unexpected indent](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [In this example, the problem is that the second line is indented by one space. But the error](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [message points to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [y](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [, which is misleading. In general, error messages indicate where the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [problem was discovered, but the actual error might be earlier in the code, sometimes on a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [previous line.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The same is true of runtime errors. Suppose you are trying to compute a signal-to-noise](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [ratio in decibels. The formula is](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [SNR](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) db [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [10 log](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [P](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [signal](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [/](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [P](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [noise](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [. In Python, you might](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [write something like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [import math](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [signal_power = 9](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) noise_power = 10 [ratio = signal_power // noise_power](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [decibels = 10 * math.log10(ratio)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [print(decibels)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [When you run this program, you get an exception:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Traceback (most recent call last):](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [File "snr.py", line 5, in ?](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [decibels = 10 * math.log10(ratio)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [ValueError: math domain error](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [The error message indicates line 5, but there is nothing wrong with that line. To find the](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [real error, it might be useful to print the value of](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [ratio](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [, which turns out to be 0.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [The](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [problem is in line 4, which uses floor division instead of floating-point division.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[You should take the time to read error messages carefully, but don’t assume that everything](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [they say is correct.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[5.13. Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [5.13](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[floor division:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [An operator, denoted](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [//](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [, that divides two numbers and rounds down (to-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [ward negative infinity) to an integer.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[modulus operator:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [An operator, denoted with a percent sign (](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) % ), that works on integers [and returns the remainder when one number is divided by another.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[boolean expression:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [An expression whose value is either](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[relational operator:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [One of the operators that compares its operands:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [==](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [!=](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [<](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [>=](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [<=](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[logical operator:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [One of the operators that combines boolean expressions:](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [not](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[conditional statement:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A statement that controls the flow of execution depending on some](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [condition.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[condition:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [The boolean expression in a conditional statement that determines which](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [branch runs.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[compound statement:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [A statement that consists of a header and a body. The header ends](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [with a colon (:). The body is indented relative to the header.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[branch:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [One of the alternative sequences of statements in a conditional statement.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[chained conditional:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [A conditional statement with a series of alternative branches.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[nested conditional:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [A conditional statement that appears in one of the branches of another](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [conditional statement.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[return statement:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [A statement that causes a function to end immediately and return to the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [caller.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[recursion:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The process of calling the function that is currently executing.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[base case:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [A conditional branch in a recursive function that does not make a recursive call.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

infinite recursion: A recursion that doesn’t have a base case, or never reaches it. Eventu- [ally, an infinite recursion causes a runtime error.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

On](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [UNIX systems, the epoch is 1 January 1970.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Exercise 5.3.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [If you are given three sticks, you may or may not be able to arrange them in a triangle.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [For example, if one of the sticks is 12 inches long and the other two are one inch long, you will not](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [be able to get the short sticks to meet in the middle. For any three lengths, there is a simple test to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [see if it is possible to form a triangle:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[If any of the three lengths is greater than the sum of the other two, then you cannot](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [form a triangle. Otherwise, you can. (If the sum of two lengths equals the third, they](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [form what is called a “degenerate” triangle.)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

To draw a Koch](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [curve with length x, all you have to do is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)
