## [Chapter 6](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Fruitful functions](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Many of the Python functions we have used, such as the math functions, produce return](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [values. But the functions we’ve written are all void: they have an effect, like printing a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [value or moving a turtle, but they don’t have a return value. In this chapter you will learn](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [to write fruitful functions.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [6.1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Return values](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Calling the function generates a return value, which we usually assign to a variable or use](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [as part of an expression.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[e = math.exp(1.0)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [height = radius * math.sin(radians)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The functions we have written so far are void.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [Speaking casually, they have no return](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [value; more precisely, their return value is](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[In this chapter, we are (finally) going to write fruitful functions. The first example is](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [area](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [which returns the area of a circle with the given radius:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[def area(radius):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [a = math.pi * radius**2](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return a](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[We have seen the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [statement before, but in a fruitful function the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [includes an expression.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [This statement means: “Return immediately from this function](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [and use the following expression as a return value.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [The expression can be arbitrarily](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [complicated, so we could have written this function more concisely:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def area(radius):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [return math.pi * radius**2](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[On the other hand,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [temporary variables](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [like](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [can make debugging easier.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Sometimes it is useful to have multiple return statements, one in each branch of a condi-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [tional:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 6. Fruitful functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def absolute_value(x):](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [if x < 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return -x](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [return x](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

Since these return [statements are in an alternative conditional, only one runs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

| quent statements. Code that appears after a | return    | statement, or any other place the flow |
| ------------------------------------------- | --------- | -------------------------------------- |
| of execution can never reach, is called     | dead code | .                                      |

[In a fruitful function, it is a good idea to ensure that every possible path through the pro-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [gram hits a](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [statement. For example:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[def absolute_value(x):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [if x < 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [return -x](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [if x > 0:](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [return x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

| This function is incorrect because if | x      | happens to be 0, neither condition is true, and the |
| ------------------------------------- | ------ | --------------------------------------------------- |
| function ends without hitting a       | return | statement. If the flow of execution gets to the end |
| of a function, the return value is    | None   | , which is not the absolute value of 0.             |

[>>> print(absolute_value(0))](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py)

[By the way, Python provides a built-in function called](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [abs](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [that computes absolute values.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[As an exercise, write a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [compare](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [function that takes two values,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) x [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [y](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [, and returns](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [if](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) > y , if [x == y](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [-1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [if](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [x < y](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [6.2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Incremental development](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[As you write larger functions, you might find yourself spending more time debugging.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[To deal with increasingly complex programs, you might want to try a process called](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [in-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [cremental development](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) . The goal of incremental development is to avoid long debugging sessions by adding and testing only a small amount of code at a time.

[As an example, suppose you want to find the distance between two points, given by the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [coordinates](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [(](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [x](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [y](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. By the Pythagorean theorem, the distance is:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[√](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [distance](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [+ (](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[The first step is to consider what a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [distance](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [function should look like in Python. In other](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [words, what are the inputs (parameters) and what is the output (return value)?](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[In this case, the inputs are two points, which you can represent using four numbers. The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [return value is the distance represented by a floating-point value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Immediately you can write an outline of the function:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[def distance(x1, y1, x2, y2):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return 0.0](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[6.2. Incremental development](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Obviously, this version doesn’t compute distances; it always returns zero. But it is syn-](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [tactically correct, and it runs, which means that you can test it before you make it more](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [complicated.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[To test the new function, call it with sample arguments:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [>>> distance(1, 2, 4, 6)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) 0.0 [I chose these values so that the horizontal distance is 3 and the vertical distance is 4; that](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [way, the result is 5, the hypotenuse of a 3-4-5 right triangle. When testing a function, it is](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [useful to know the right answer.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[At this point we have confirmed that the function is syntactically correct, and we can start](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [adding code to the body.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [A reasonable next step is to find the differences](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [. The next version stores those values in temporary variables and prints them.](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [def distance(x1, y1, x2, y2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [dx = x2 - x1](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [dy = y2 - y1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [print(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [dx is](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, dx)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [print(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [dy is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [, dy)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [return 0.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [If the function is working, it should display](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [dx is 3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [dy is 4](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [. If so, we know that the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [function is getting the right arguments and performing the first computation correctly. If](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [not, there are only a few lines to check.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Next we compute the sum of squares of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [dx](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [dy](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [def distance(x1, y1, x2, y2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [dx = x2 - x1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [dy = y2 - y1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [dsquared = dx**2 + dy**2](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [dsquared is:](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [, dsquared)](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [return 0.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Again, you would run the program at this stage and check the output (which should be](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [25). Finally, you can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [math.sqrt](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [to compute and return the result:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [def distance(x1, y1, x2, y2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [dx = x2 - x1](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [dy = y2 - y1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) dsquared = dx**2 + dy**2 [result = math.sqrt(dsquared)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return result](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [If that works correctly, you are done.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Otherwise, you might want to print the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [result](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [before the return statement.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The final version of the function doesn’t display anything when it runs; it only returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [a value. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [statements we wrote are useful for debugging, but once you get the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [function working, you should remove them. Code like that is called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [scaffolding](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [because it](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is helpful for building the program but is not part of the final product.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[When you start out, you should add only a line or two of code at a time. As you gain more](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [experience, you might find yourself writing and debugging bigger chunks.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) Either way, [incremental development can save you a lot of debugging time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[The key aspects of the process are:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 6. Fruitful functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

1. [1. Start with a working program and make small incremental changes. At any point, if](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [there is an error, you should have a good idea where it is.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

2. [2. Use variables to hold intermediate values so you can display and check them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

3. [3. Once the program is working, you might want to remove some of the scaffolding or](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [consolidate multiple statements into compound expressions, but only if it does not](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [make the program difficult to read.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[As an exercise, use incremental development to write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [hypotenuse](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [returns the length of the hypotenuse of a right triangle given the lengths of the other two](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [legs as arguments. Record each stage of the development process as you go.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

#### [6.3](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Composition](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[As you should expect by now, you can call one function from within another. As an exam-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [ple, we’ll write a function that takes two points, the center of the circle and a point on the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [perimeter, and computes the area of the circle.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[Assume that the center point is stored in the variables](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [xc](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [yc](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, and the perimeter point is](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [in](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [xp](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [yp](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [. The first step is to find the radius of the circle, which is the distance between](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [the two points. We just wrote a function,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [distance](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [, that does that:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[radius = distance(xc, yc, xp, yp)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The next step is to find the area of a circle with that radius; we just wrote that, too:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[result = area(radius)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Encapsulating these steps in a function, we get:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def circle_area(xc, yc, xp, yp):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [radius = distance(xc, yc, xp, yp)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [result = area(radius)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [return result](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[The temporary variables](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [radius](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [and](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [result](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [are useful for development and debugging,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [but once the program is working, we can make it more concise by composing the function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) calls:

[def circle_area(xc, yc, xp, yp):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return area(distance(xc, yc, xp, yp))](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

#### 6.4 [Boolean functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Functions can return booleans, which is often convenient for hiding complicated tests in-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [side functions. For example:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def is_divisible(x, y):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [if x % y == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [else:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return False](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[6.5. More recursion](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[It](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [common](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [give](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [boolean](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [names](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [sound](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [like](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [yes/no](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [questions;](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [is_divisible](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [returns either](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to indicate whether](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [is divisible by](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sorting_algorithm)

[Here is an example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [>>> is_divisible(6, 4)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [>>> is_divisible(6, 3)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [True](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [The result of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [==](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [operator is a boolean, so we can write the function more concisely by](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [returning it directly:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [def is_divisible(x, y):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [return x % y == 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [Boolean functions are often used in conditional statements:](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [if is_divisible(x, y):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [x is divisible by y](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [It might be tempting to write something like:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [if is_divisible(x, y) == True:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [x is divisible by y](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [But the extra comparison is unnecessary.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[As an exercise, write a function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [is_between(x, y, z)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [that returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [≤](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [≤](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [z](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [otherwise.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [6.5](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [More recursion](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[We have only covered a small subset of Python, but you might be interested to know that](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [this subset is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [complete](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [programming language, which means that anything that can be](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [computed can be expressed in this language. Any program ever written could be rewritten](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [using only the language features you have learned so far (actually, you would need a few](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [commands to control devices like the mouse, disks, etc., but that’s all).](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Proving that claim is a nontrivial exercise first accomplished by Alan Turing, one of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [first computer scientists (some would argue that he was a mathematician, but a lot of early](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [computer scientists started as mathematicians).](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) Accordingly, it is known as the Turing Thesis. For a more complete (and accurate) discussion of the Turing Thesis, I recommend [Michael Sipser’s book](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Introduction to the Theory of Computation](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[To give you an idea of what you can do with the tools you have learned so far, we’ll eval-](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [uate a few recursively defined mathematical functions. A recursive definition is similar to](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [a circular definition, in the sense that the definition contains a reference to the thing being](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [defined. A truly circular definition is not very useful:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[vorpal:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [An adjective used to describe something that is vorpal.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[If you saw that definition in the dictionary, you might be annoyed.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [On the other hand,](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [if you looked up the definition of the factorial function, denoted with the symbol !, you](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [might get something like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[0!](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [!](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [−](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [)](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting) [!](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting)

[Chapter 6. Fruitful functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This definition says that the factorial of 0 is 1, and the factorial of any other value,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [, is](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [multiplied by the factorial of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[So 3! is 3 times 2!, which is 2 times 1!, which is 1 times 0!. Putting it all together, 3! equals 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [times 2 times 1 times 1, which is 6.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[If you can write a recursive definition of something, you can write a Python program to](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [evaluate it. The first step is to decide what the parameters should be. In this case it should](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [be clear that](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [factorial](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [takes an integer:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[def factorial(n):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[If the argument happens to be 0, all we have to do is return 1:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[def factorial(n):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [if n == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [return 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Otherwise, and this is the interesting part, we have to make a recursive call to find the](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [factorial of](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [−](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [1 and then multiply it by](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [n](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def factorial(n):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [if n == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [return 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [else:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [recurse = factorial(n-1)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [result = n * recurse](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return result](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[The flow of execution for this program is similar to the flow of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [countdown](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [in Section 5.8. If](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) we call [factorial](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [with the value 3:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Since 3 is not 0, we take the second branch and calculate the factorial of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n-1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [...](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Since 2 is not 0, we take the second branch and calculate the factorial of](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [n-1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [...](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Since 1 is not 0, we take the second branch and calculate the factorial](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [of](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [n-1](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [...](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Since 0 equals 0, we take the first branch and return 1 without](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) making any more recursive calls.

[The return value, 1, is multiplied by](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, which is 1, and the result is](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [returned.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[The return value, 1, is multiplied by](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, which is 2, and the result is returned.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[The return value (2) is multiplied by](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, which is 3, and the result, 6, becomes the return](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [value of the function call that started the whole process.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Figure 6.1 shows what the stack diagram looks like for this sequence of function calls.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[The return values are shown being passed back up the stack.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [In each frame, the return](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [value is the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [result](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [, which is the product of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [recurse](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[In the last frame, the local variables](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [recurse](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [result](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [do not exist, because the branch](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [that creates them does not run.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[6.6. Leap of faith](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[__main__](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[factorial](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [recurse](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [result](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[factorial](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [recurse](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [result](https://thinkpython.com/code)

[factorial](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [n](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [recurse](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) result

[factorial](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[Figure 6.1: Stack diagram.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

#### [6.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Leap of faith](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Following the flow of execution is one way to read programs, but it can quickly become](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [overwhelming.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [An alternative is what I call the “leap of faith”.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [When you come to a](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [function call, instead of following the flow of execution, you](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [assume](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [that the function works](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [correctly and returns the right result.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[In fact, you are already practicing this leap of faith when you use built-in functions. When](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [you call](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [math.cos](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [math.exp](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [, you don’t examine the bodies of those functions. You just](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [assume that they work because the people who wrote the built-in functions were good](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [programmers.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[The same is true when you call one of your own functions. For example, in Section 6.4, we](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [wrote a function called](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is_divisible](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [that determines whether one number is divisible by](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [another. Once we have convinced ourselves that this function is correct—by examining the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [code and testing—we can use the function without looking at the body again.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The same is true of recursive programs.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [When you get to the recursive call, instead of](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [following the flow of execution, you should assume that the recursive call works (returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [the correct result) and then ask yourself, “Assuming that I can find the factorial of](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) − 1, [can I compute the factorial of](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [?” It is clear that you can, by multiplying by](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[Of course, it’s a bit strange to assume that the function works correctly when you haven’t](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) finished writing it, but that’s why it’s called a leap of faith!

#### [6.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [One more example](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[After](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [factorial](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, the most common example of a recursively defined mathematical func-](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [tion is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [fibonacci](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [, which has the following definition (see](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [http://en.wikipedia.org/](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [wiki/Fibonacci_number](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

| fibonacci | (   | ) = |
| --------- | --- | --- |
| fibonacci | (   | ) = |

[Translated into Python, it looks like this:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 6. Fruitful functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def fibonacci(n):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [if n == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return 0](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [elif](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [n == 1:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [return 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) else: [return fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[If you try to follow the flow of execution here, even for fairly small values of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [, your head](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [explodes. But according to the leap of faith, if you assume that the two recursive calls work](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [correctly, then it is clear that you get the right result by adding them together.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

#### [6.8](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Checking types](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[What happens if we call](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [factorial](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [and give it 1.5 as an argument?](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[>>> factorial(1.5)](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [RuntimeError: Maximum recursion depth exceeded](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[It looks like an infinite recursion. How can that be? The function has a base case—when](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) == 0 [. But if](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [is not an integer, we can](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [miss](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [the base case and recurse forever.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[In the first recursive call, the value of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [is 0.5.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [In the next, it is -0.5.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [From there, it gets](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [smaller (more negative), but it will never be 0.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[We have two choices.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [We can try to generalize the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [factorial](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [function to work with](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [floating-point numbers, or we can make](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [factorial](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [check the type of its argument. The](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [first option is called the gamma function and it’s a little beyond the scope of this book. So](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [we’ll go for the second.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[We can use the built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [isinstance](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [to verify the type of the argument.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [While](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [we’re at it, we can also make sure the argument is positive:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def factorial(n):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [if not isinstance(n, int):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Factorial is only defined for integers.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [return None](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [elif n < 0:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) print( ' Factorial is not defined for negative integers. ' ) return None [elif n == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return 1](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return n * factorial(n-1)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The first base case handles nonintegers; the second handles negative integers.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [In both](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [cases, the program prints an error message and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [to indicate that something](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [went wrong:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[>>> print(factorial(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [fred](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [))](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Factorial is only defined for integers.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) None [>>> print(factorial(-2))](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Factorial is not defined for negative integers.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) None

[6.9. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[If we get past both checks, we know that](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [is a non-negative integer, so we can prove that](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [the recursion terminates.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[This program demonstrates a pattern sometimes called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) guardian [. The first two condi-](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [tionals act as guardians, protecting the code that follows from values that might cause an](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [error. The guardians make it possible to prove the correctness of the code.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[In Section 11.4 we will see a more flexible alternative to printing an error message: raising](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [an exception.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

#### [6.9](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py)

[Breaking a large program into smaller functions creates natural checkpoints for debugging.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [If a function is not working, there are three possibilities to consider:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

- [• There is something wrong with the arguments the function is getting; a precondition](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is violated.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

- [• There is something wrong with the function; a postcondition is violated.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

- [• There is something wrong with the return value or the way it is being used.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[To rule out the first possibility, you can add a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [print](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [statement at the beginning of the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [function and display the values of the parameters (and maybe their types).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Or you can](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [write code that checks the preconditions explicitly.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[If the parameters look good, add a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [statement before each](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [statement and](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [display the return value. If possible, check the result by hand. Consider calling the function](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [with values that make it easy to check the result (as in Section 6.2).](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[If the function seems to be working, look at the function call to make sure the return value](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [is being used correctly (or used at all!).](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Adding print statements at the beginning and end of a function can help make the flow of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) execution more visible. For example, here is a version of factorial with print statements:

[def factorial(n):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [space =](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [' '](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [* (4 * n)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [print(space,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [factorial](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, n)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [if n == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(space,](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [returning 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [return 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [recurse = factorial(n-1)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [result = n * recurse](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [print(space,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [returning](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [, result)](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [return result](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[space](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is a string of space characters that controls the indentation of the output. Here is the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [result of](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [factorial(4)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 6. Fruitful functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[factorial 4](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [factorial 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [factorial 2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [factorial 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [factorial 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) returning 1 [returning 1](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [returning 2](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [returning 6](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [returning 24](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[If you are confused about the flow of execution, this kind of output can be helpful. It takes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [some time to develop effective scaffolding, but a little bit of scaffolding can save a lot of](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [debugging.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

#### [6.10](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [Glossary](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[temporary variable:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [A variable used to store an intermediate value in a complex calcula-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [tion.](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[dead code:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Part of a program that can never run, often because it appears after a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [statement.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[incremental development:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [A program development plan intended to avoid debugging by](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [adding and testing only a small amount of code at a time.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[scaffolding:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Code that is used during program development but is not part of the final](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [version.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[guardian:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [A programming pattern that uses a conditional statement to check for and han-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [dle circumstances that might cause an error.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [6.11](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Exercises](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

Exercise 6.1. Draw a stack diagram for the following program. What does the program print?

def b(z): [prod = a(z, z)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [print(z, prod)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [return prod](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def a(x, y):](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [x = x + 1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [return x * y](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def c(x, y, z):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [total = x + y + z](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [square = b(total)**2](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return square](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[6.11. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[x = 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [y = x + 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(c(x, y+3, x+y))](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [Exercise 6.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [The Ackermann function, A](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [(](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [m](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) , is defined:

[](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [n](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [+](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [if m](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) = [A](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [(](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [m](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) m](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [and n](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [))](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [if m](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py) [See](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [http: // en. wikipedia. org/ wiki/ Ackermann_ function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [. Write a function named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [ack](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [that evaluates the Ackermann function. Use your function to evaluate](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [ack(3, 4)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [, which should be](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [125. What happens for larger values of](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [m](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [and](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [? Solution:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [ackermann. py](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Exercise 6.3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [A palindrome is a word that is spelled the same backward and forward, like “noon”](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [and “redivider”. Recursively, a word is a palindrome if the first and last letters are the same and the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [middle is a palindrome.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The following are functions that take a string argument and return the first, last, and middle letters:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[def first(word):](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [return word[0]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[def last(word):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [return word[-1]](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[def middle(word):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return word[1:-1]](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[We’ll see how they work in Chapter 8.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

1. [1. Type these functions into a file named](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [palindrome.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [and test them out. What happens if](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [you call](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [middle](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [with a string with two letters? One letter? What about the empty string,](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [which is written](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [''](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [and contains no letters?](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

2. [2. Write a function called](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [is_palindrome](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [that takes a string argument and returns](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [True](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [if it](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [is a palindrome and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) False otherwise. Remember that you can use the built-in function len to check the length of a string.

[Solution:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ palindrome_ soln. py](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Exercise 6.4.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [A number, a, is a power of b if it is divisible by b and a](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) / [b is a power of b. Write a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is_power](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [that takes parameters](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [True](http://gutenberg.org/) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [is a power of](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. Note:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [you will have to think about the base case.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Exercise 6.5.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [The greatest common divisor (GCD) of a and b is the largest number that divides](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [both of them with no remainder.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[One way to find the GCD of two numbers is based on the observation that if r is the remainder when](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [a is divided by b, then gcd](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [) =](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [gcd](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [r](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [. As a base case, we can use gcd](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [, 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [) =](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [a.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [gcd](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [that takes parameters](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [and returns their greatest common divisor.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)
