## [Chapter 7](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Iteration](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[This chapter is about iteration, which is the ability to run a block of statements repeatedly.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [We saw a kind of iteration, using recursion, in Section 5.8. We saw another kind, using a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [loop, in Section 4.2. In this chapter we’ll see yet another kind, using a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [while](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [statement.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [But first I want to say a little more about variable assignment.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [7.1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Reassignment](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[As you may have discovered, it is legal to make more than one assignment to the same](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [variable.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [A new assignment makes an existing variable refer to a new value (and stop](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [referring to the old value).](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> x = 5](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> x](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> x = 7](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> x](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

The first time we display x , its value is 5; the second time, its value is 7.

[Figure 7.1 shows what](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [reassignment](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [looks like in a state diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[At this point I want to address a common source of confusion. Because Python uses the](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [equal sign (](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [) for assignment, it is tempting to interpret a statement like](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a = b](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [as a mathe-](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [matical proposition of equality; that is, the claim that](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [are equal. But this interpre-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [tation is wrong.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[First, equality is a symmetric relationship and assignment is not. For example, in math-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [ematics, if](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [7 then 7](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [But in Python, the statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [a = 7](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [is legal and](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [7 = a](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [is](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [not.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[Also, in mathematics, a proposition of equality is either true or false for all time. If](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [=](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [b](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [now, then](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [will always equal](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [b](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [In Python, an assignment statement can make two](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [variables equal, but they don’t have to stay that way:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 7. Iteration](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Figure 7.1: State diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[>>> a = 5](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [>>> b = a](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [# a and b are now equal](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [>>> a = 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [# a and b are no longer equal](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) >>> b

[The third line changes the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [but does not change the value of](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [, so they are no](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [longer equal.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Reassigning variables is often useful, but you should use it with caution. If the values of](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [variables change frequently, it can make the code difficult to read and debug.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

#### [7.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [Updating variables](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[A common kind of reassignment is an](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [update](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, where the new value of the variable depends](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [on the old.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[>>> x = x + 1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[This means “get the current value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [, add one, and then update](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [with the new value.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[If you try to update a variable that doesn’t exist, you get an error, because Python evaluates](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the right side before it assigns a value to](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py)

[>>> x = x + 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [NameError: name](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [x](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is not defined](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Before you can update a variable, you have to](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [initialize](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [it, usually with a simple assign-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [ment:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[>>> x = 0](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [>>> x = x + 1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

Updating a variable by adding 1 is called an increment ; subtracting 1 is called a decrement .

#### 7.3 [The](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [while](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Computers are often used to automate repetitive tasks. Repeating identical or similar tasks](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [without making errors is something that computers do well and people do poorly. In a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [computer program, repetition is also called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [iteration](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[We have already seen two functions,](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [countdown](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [print_n](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [, that iterate using recursion.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Because iteration is so common, Python provides language features to make it easier. One](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) is the [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/double.py) [statement we saw in Section 4.2. We’ll get back to that later.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[Another is the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [while](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [statement. Here is a version of](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [countdown](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [that uses a](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting) [while](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting) [statement:](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[7.3. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py) [while](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py) [statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def countdown(n):](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [while n > 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(n)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [n = n - 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [Blastoff!](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[You can almost read the](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [while](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [statement as if it were English. It means, “While](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [n](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) is greater [than 0, display the value of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [n](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [and then decrement](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [. When you get to 0, display the word](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Blastoff!](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [”](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[More formally, here is the flow of execution for a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [while](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py) [statement:](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py)

1. [1. Determine whether the condition is true or false.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

2. [2. If false, exit the](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [while](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [statement and continue execution at the next statement.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

3. [3. If the condition is true, run the body and then go back to step 1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[This type of flow is called a loop because the third step loops back around to the top.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The body of the loop should change the value of one or more variables so that the condition](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [becomes false eventually and the loop terminates. Otherwise the loop will repeat forever,](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [which is called an](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [infinite loop](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [. An endless source of amusement for computer scientists](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [is the observation that the directions on shampoo, “Lather, rinse, repeat”, are an infinite](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [loop.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[In the case of](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [countdown](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, we can prove that the loop terminates: if](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [is zero or negative, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [loop never runs. Otherwise,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [n](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [gets smaller each time through the loop, so eventually we](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [have to get to 0.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[For some other loops, it is not so easy to tell. For example:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def sequence(n):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [while n != 1:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(n)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [if n % 2 == 0:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [# n is even](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [n = n / 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [# n is odd](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [n = n*3 + 1](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[The condition for this loop is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) n != 1 , so the loop will continue until n is , which makes the condition false.

[Each time through the loop, the program outputs the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [and then checks whether](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [it is even or odd. If it is even,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is divided by 2. If it is odd, the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) n [is replaced with](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n*3 + 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. For example, if the argument passed to](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [sequence](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is 3, the resulting values of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [are 3, 10, 5, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Since](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [sometimes increases and sometimes decreases, there is no obvious proof that](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [will](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [ever reach 1, or that the program terminates. For some particular values of](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [, we can prove](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [termination.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [For example, if the starting value is a power of two,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [will be even every](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [time through the loop until it reaches 1. The previous example ends with such a sequence,](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [starting with 16.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[The hard question is whether we can prove that this program terminates for](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [all](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [posi-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [tive values of](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [n](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [So far, no one has been able to prove it](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [disprove it!](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [(See](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [http:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [//en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [.)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture)

[Chapter 7. Iteration](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[As an exercise, rewrite the function](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [print_n](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [from Section 5.8 using iteration instead of](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [recursion.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### 7.4 [break](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Sometimes you don’t know it’s time to end a loop until you get half way through the body.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [In that case you can use the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [break](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [statement to jump out of the loop.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[For example, suppose you want to take input from the user until they type](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [done](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [. You could](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) write: [while True:](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [line = input(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [if line ==](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [done](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [break](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [print(line)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

print( ' [Done!](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [The loop condition is](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [, which is always true, so the loop runs until it hits the break](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [statement.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[Each time through, it prompts the user with an angle bracket. If the user types](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [done](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [break](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [statement exits the loop. Otherwise the program echoes whatever the user types and](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [goes back to the top of the loop. Here’s a sample run:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [> not done](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [not done](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) > done Done! [This way of writing](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [while](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [loops is common because you can check the condition anywhere](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [in the loop (not just at the top) and you can express the stop condition affirmatively (“stop](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [when this happens”) rather than negatively (“keep going until that happens”).](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

#### [7.5](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Square roots](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

Loops are often used in programs that compute numerical results by starting with an ap- [proximate answer and iteratively improving it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[For example, one way of computing square roots is Newton’s method. Suppose that you](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [want to know the square root of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [. If you start with almost any estimate,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) x , you can com- [pute a better estimate with the following formula:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[x](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [/](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[For example, if](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is 4 and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is 3:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [>>> a = 4](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> x = 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> y = (x + a/x) / 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) >>> y [2.16666666667](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[7.6. Algorithms](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[√](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [The result is closer to the correct answer (](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [2). If we repeat the process with the new](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [estimate, it gets even closer:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> x = y](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> y = (x + a/x) / 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [>>> y](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) 2.00641025641

[After a few more updates, the estimate is almost exact:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[>>> x = y](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [>>> y = (x + a/x) / 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [>>> y](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [2.00001024003](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> x = y](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [>>> y = (x + a/x) / 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>>> y](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [2.00000000003](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[In general we don’t know ahead of time how many steps it takes to get to the right answer,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [but we know when we get there because the estimate stops changing:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> x = y](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [>>> y = (x + a/x) / 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [>>> y](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [2.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> x = y](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> y = (x + a/x) / 2](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> y](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [2.0](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[When](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [y == x](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [, we can stop. Here is a loop that starts with an initial estimate,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, and im-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [proves it until it stops changing:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[while True:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(x)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [y = (x + a/x) / 2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [if y == x:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [break](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [x = y](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

For most values of a this works fine, but in general it is dangerous to test float equality. [Floating-point values are only approximately right: most rational numbers, like 1/3, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [√](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [irrational numbers, like](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [2, can’t be represented exactly with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [float](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[Rather than checking whether](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [are exactly equal, it is safer to use the built-in func-](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [tion](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [abs](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to compute the absolute value, or magnitude, of the difference between them:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[if abs(y-x) < epsilon:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [break](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Where](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [epsilon](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [has a value like](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [0.0000001](https://thinkpython.com/code/inlist.py) [that determines how close is close enough.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

#### [7.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Algorithms](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Newton’s method is an example of an](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [algorithm](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [: it is a mechanical process for solving a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [category of problems (in this case, computing square roots).](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 7. Iteration](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[To understand what an algorithm is, it might help to start with something that is not an](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [algorithm. When you learned to multiply single-digit numbers, you probably memorized](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the multiplication table.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [In effect, you memorized 100 specific solutions.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [That kind of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [knowledge is not algorithmic.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[But if you were “lazy”, you might have learned a few tricks.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [For example, to find the](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) product of n [and 9, you can write](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [1 as the first digit and 10](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [as the second digit.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [This trick is a general solution for multiplying any single-digit number by 9.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [That’s an](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [algorithm!](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Similarly, the techniques you learned for addition with carrying, subtraction with borrow-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [ing, and long division are all algorithms. One of the characteristics of algorithms is that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [they do not require any intelligence to carry out.](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [They are mechanical processes where](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [each step follows from the last according to a simple set of rules.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Executing algorithms is boring, but designing them is interesting, intellectually challeng-](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [ing, and a central part of computer science.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Some of the things that people do naturally, without difficulty or conscious thought, are](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the hardest to express algorithmically. Understanding natural language is a good example.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [We all do it, but so far no one has been able to explain](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [how](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [we do it, at least not in the form](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [of an algorithm.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

#### [7.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[As you start writing bigger programs, you might find yourself spending more time debug-](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [ging. More code means more chances to make an error and more places for bugs to hide.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[One way to cut your debugging time is “debugging by bisection”. For example, if there](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [are 100 lines in your program and you check them one at a time, it would take 100 steps.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Instead, try to break the problem in half. Look at the middle of the program, or near it, for](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [an intermediate value you can check. Add a](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [statement (or something else that has a](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [verifiable effect) and run the program.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[If the mid-point check is incorrect, there must be a problem in the first half of the program.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) If it is correct, the problem is in the second half.

[Every time you perform a check like this, you halve the number of lines you have to search.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [After six steps (which is fewer than 100), you would be down to one or two lines of code,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [at least in theory.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[In practice it is not always clear what the “middle of the program” is and not always pos-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [sible to check it. It doesn’t make sense to count lines and find the exact midpoint. Instead,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [think about places in the program where there might be errors and places where it is easy](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [to put a check. Then choose a spot where you think the chances are about the same that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [the bug is before or after the check.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

#### 7.8 [Glossary](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[reassignment:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Assigning a new value to a variable that already exists.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[7.9. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[update:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [An assignment where the new value of the variable depends on the old.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[initialization:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [An assignment that gives an initial value to a variable that will be updated.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[increment:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [An update that increases the value of a variable (often by one).](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

decrement: [An update that decreases the value of a variable.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[iteration:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Repeated execution of a set of statements using either a recursive function call](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [or a loop.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[infinite loop:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [A loop in which the terminating condition is never satisfied.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[algorithm:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [A general process for solving a category of problems.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

#### [7.9](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [Exercises](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Exercise 7.1.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Copy the loop from Section 7.5 and encapsulate it in a function called](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [mysqrt](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [takes](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [as a parameter, chooses a reasonable value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [, and returns an estimate of the square root of](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[To test it, write a function named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [test_square_root](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [that prints a table like this:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [mysqrt(a)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [math.sqrt(a)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [diff](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1_soln.py) [---------](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [------------](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [----](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [1.0 1.0](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [1.0](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [0.0](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [2.0 1.41421356237 1.41421356237 2.22044604925e-16](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [3.0 1.73205080757 1.73205080757 0.0](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [4.0 2.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [2.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [0.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [5.0 2.2360679775](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [2.2360679775](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [0.0](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [6.0 2.44948974278 2.44948974278 0.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [7.0 2.64575131106 2.64575131106 0.0](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [8.0 2.82842712475 2.82842712475 4.4408920985e-16](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [9.0 3.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [3.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [0.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[The first column is a number, a; the second column is the square root of a computed with](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [mysqrt](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [;](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [the third column is the square root computed by](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) math.sqrt ; the fourth column is the absolute value of the difference between the two estimates. [Exercise 7.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [The built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [eval](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [takes a string and evaluates it using the Python inter-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [preter. For example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> eval(](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [1 + 2 * 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[>>> import math](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> eval(](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [math.sqrt(5)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [2.2360679774997898](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [>>> eval(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [type(math.pi)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [<class](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [float](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [>](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Write a function called](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [eval_loop](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [that iteratively prompts the user, takes the resulting input and](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [evaluates it using](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [eval](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [, and prints the result.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[It should continue until the user enters](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [done](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [, and then return the value of the last expression it](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [evaluated.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

## [Chapter 8](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Strings are not like integers, floats, and booleans. A string is a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) sequence [, which means it is](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [an ordered collection of other values. In this chapter you’ll see how to access the characters](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [that make up a string, and you’ll learn about some of the methods strings provide.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

#### [8.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [A string is a sequence](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[A string is a sequence of characters. You can access the characters one at a time with the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [bracket operator:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> fruit =](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [banana](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> letter = fruit[1]](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[The second statement selects character number 1 from](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [fruit](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and assigns it to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [letter](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The expression in brackets is called an](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [index](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [. The index indicates which character in the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [sequence you want (hence the name).](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[But you might not get what you expect:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[>>> letter](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[For most people, the first letter of](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [is](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, not](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. But for computer scientists, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [index is an offset from the beginning of the string, and the offset of the first letter is zero.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> letter = fruit[0]](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [>>> letter](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[So](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py) [is the 0th letter (“zero-eth”) of](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is the 1th letter (“one-eth”), and](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [is the 2th](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [letter (“two-eth”).](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[As an index you can use an expression that contains variables and operators:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[>>> i = 1](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> fruit[i]](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> fruit[i+1]](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [n](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 8. Strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[But the value of the index has to be an integer. Otherwise you get:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

>>> letter = fruit[1.5] [TypeError: string indices must be integers](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

#### [8.2](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [len](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[len](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [is a built-in function that returns the number of characters in a string:](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[>>> fruit =](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [>>> len(fruit)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[To get the last letter of a string, you might be tempted to try something like this:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[>>> length = len(fruit)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>>> last = fruit[length]](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [IndexError: string index out of range](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The reason for the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [IndexError](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is that there is no letter in](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [’banana’](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [with the index 6. Since](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [we started counting at zero, the six letters are numbered 0 to 5. To get the last character,](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [you have to subtract 1 from](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [length](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[>>> last = fruit[length-1]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> last](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py)

[Or you can use negative indices, which count backward from the end of the string. The](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [expression](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [fruit[-1]](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [yields the last letter,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) fruit[-2] yields the second to last, and so on.

#### 8.3 [Traversal with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [for](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [loop](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[A lot of computations involve processing a string one character at a time. Often they start](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [at the beginning, select each character in turn, do something to it, and continue until the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [end. This pattern of processing is called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [traversal](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [. One way to write a traversal is with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [while](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [loop:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[index = 0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) while index < len(fruit): [letter = fruit[index]](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [print(letter)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [index = index + 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[This loop traverses the string and displays each letter on a line by itself. The loop condition](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) is [index < len(fruit)](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [, so when](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [index](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [is equal to the length of the string, the condition is](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [false, and the body of the loop doesn’t run. The last character accessed is the one with the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) index [len(fruit)-1](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [, which is the last character in the string.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[As an exercise, write a function that takes a string as an argument and displays the letters](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [backward, one per line.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[Another way to write a traversal is with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [loop:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[for letter in fruit:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [print(letter)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[8.4. String slices](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[fruit](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

### [’](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [b a n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [a ’](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[index](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Figure 8.1: Slice indices.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Each time through the loop, the next character in the string is assigned to the variable](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [letter](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [. The loop continues until no characters are left.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[The following example shows how to use concatenation (string addition) and a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) for loop [to generate an abecedarian series (that is, in alphabetical order). In Robert McCloskey’s](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [book](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [Make Way for Ducklings](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [, the names of the ducklings are Jack, Kack, Lack, Mack, Nack,](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [Ouack, Pack, and Quack. This loop outputs these names in order:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[prefixes =](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [JKLMNOPQ](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [suffix =](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [ack](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[for letter in prefixes:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [print(letter + suffix)](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[The output is:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[Jack](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Kack](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Lack](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Mack](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Nack](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Oack](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Pack](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [Qack](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Of course, that’s not quite right because “Ouack” and “Quack” are misspelled.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) As an [exercise, modify the program to fix this error.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

#### [8.4](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [String slices](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

A segment of a string is called a slice . Selecting a slice is similar to selecting a character:

>>> s = ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Monty Python](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> s[0:5]](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Monty](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> s[6:12]](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [Python](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) ['](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pdb.html)

[The operator](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [[n:m]](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [returns the part of the string from the “n-eth” character to the “m-eth”](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [character, including the first but excluding the last. This behavior is counterintuitive, but](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [it might help to imagine the indices pointing](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [between](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [the characters, as in Figure 8.1.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[If you omit the first index (before the colon), the slice starts at the beginning of the string.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [If you omit the second index, the slice goes to the end of the string:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> fruit =](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [banana](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [>>> fruit[:3]](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 8. Strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

| '   | ban | '   |
| --- | --- | --- |
| '   | ana | '   |

[If the first index is greater than or equal to the second the result is an](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) empty string , repre- [sented by two quotation marks:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[>>> fruit =](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [banana](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [>>> fruit[3:3]](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [''](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt)

[An empty string contains no characters and has length 0, but other than that, it is the same](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [as any other string.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Continuing this example, what do you think](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [fruit[:]](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [means? Try it and see.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

#### [8.5](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [Strings are immutable](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[It is tempting to use the](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [[]](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [operator on the left side of an assignment, with the intention of](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [changing a character in a string. For example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[>>> greeting =](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Hello, world!](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> greeting[0] =](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [J](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [TypeError:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [str](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [object does not support item assignment](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The “object” in this case is the string and the “item” is the character you tried to assign.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [For now, an object is the same thing as a value, but we will refine that definition later](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [(Section 10.10).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The reason for the error is that strings are](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [immutable](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [, which means you can’t change an](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [existing string. The best you can do is create a new string that is a variation on the original:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> greeting =](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Hello, world!](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> new_greeting =](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [J](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [+ greeting[1:]](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [>>> new_greeting](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Jello, world!](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

This example concatenates a new first letter onto a slice of greeting . It has no effect on the original string.

#### 8.6 [Searching](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[What does the following function do?](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def find(word, letter):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [index = 0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [while index < len(word):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [if word[index] == letter:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return index](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [index = index + 1](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return -1](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[8.7. Looping and counting](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[In a sense,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [find](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is the inverse of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [[]](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [operator. Instead of taking an index and extracting](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [the corresponding character, it takes a character and finds the index where that character](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [appears. If the character is not found, the function returns](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [-1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[This is the first example we have seen of a](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [statement inside a loop. If](https://thinkpython.com/code) word[index] [== letter](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [, the function breaks out of the loop and returns immediately.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[If the character doesn’t appear in the string, the program exits the loop normally and re-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [turns](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [-1](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[This pattern of computation—traversing a sequence and returning when we find what we](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [are looking for—is called a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [search](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[As an exercise, modify](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [find](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [so that it has a third parameter, the index in](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [word](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [where it](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [should start looking.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

#### [8.7](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Looping and counting](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The following program counts the number of times the letter](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [appears in a string:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[word =](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [banana](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [count = 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [for letter in word:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [if letter ==](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [count = count + 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [print(count)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[This program demonstrates another pattern of computation called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [counter](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. The variable](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [count](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is initialized to 0 and then incremented each time an](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is found. When the loop exits,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [count](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [contains the result—the total number of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [’s.](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[As an exercise, encapsulate this code in a function named](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [count](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [, and generalize it so that](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [it accepts the string and the letter as arguments.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Then rewrite the function so that instead of traversing the string, it uses the three-](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [parameter version of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) find from the previous section.

#### [8.8](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [String methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Strings provide methods that perform a variety of useful operations. A method is similar](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to a function—it takes arguments and returns a value—but the syntax is different.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [For](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [example, the method](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [upper](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [takes a string and returns a new string with all uppercase](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [letters.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Instead of the function syntax](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [upper(word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [, it uses the method syntax](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [word.upper()](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> word =](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [>>> new_word = word.upper()](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> new_word](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [BANANA](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 8. Strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This form of dot notation specifies the name of the method,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [upper](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [, and the name of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [string to apply the method to,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [word](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The empty parentheses indicate that this method](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [takes no arguments.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[A method call is called an](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [invocation](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [; in this case, we would say that we are invoking](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) upper on [word](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[As it turns out, there is a string method named](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [find](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [that is remarkably similar to the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [function we wrote:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[>>> word =](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [>>> index = word.find(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> index](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

[In this example, we invoke](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) find on word [and pass the letter we are looking for as a param-](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [eter.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Actually, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [find](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [method is more general than our function; it can find substrings, not just](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [characters:](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[>>> word.find(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [na](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[By default,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [find](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [starts at the beginning of the string, but it can take a second argument, the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [index where it should start:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[>>> word.find(](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [na](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [, 3)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[This is an example of an](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [optional argument](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [;](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [find](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [can also take a third argument, the index](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [where it should stop:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> name =](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [bob](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [>>> name.find(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, 1, 2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) -1

[This search fails because](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [b](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [does not appear in the index range from](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [to](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [, not including](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Searching up to, but not including, the second index makes](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [find](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [consistent with the slice](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [operator.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

#### 8.9 The in [operator](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[The word](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [in](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [is a boolean operator that takes two strings and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [if the first ap-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [pears as a substring in the second:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

>>> ' a ' [in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) True >>> ' [seed](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [in](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) False

[For example, the following function prints all the letters from](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [word1](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [that also appear in](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [word2](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py)

[def in_both(word1, word2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [for letter in word1:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [if letter in word2:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [print(letter)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[8.10. String comparison](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[With well-chosen variable names, Python sometimes reads like English. You could read](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [this loop, “for (each) letter in (the first) word, if (the) letter (appears) in (the second) word,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print (the) letter.”](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Here’s what you get if you compare apples and oranges:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

>>> in_both( ' [apples](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [oranges](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [a](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [e](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [s](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

#### [8.10](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [String comparison](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[The relational operators work on strings. To see if two strings are equal:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[if word ==](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [print(](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [All right, bananas.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Other relational operations are useful for putting words in alphabetical order:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[if word <](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [Your word,](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [+ word +](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [, comes before banana.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [elif word >](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [banana](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Your word,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [+ word +](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [, comes after banana.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1.py) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [All right, bananas.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Python does not handle uppercase and lowercase letters the same way people do. All the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [uppercase letters come before all the lowercase letters, so:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Your word, Pineapple, comes before banana.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[A common way to address this problem is to convert strings to a standard format, such as](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [all lowercase, before performing the comparison. Keep that in mind in case you have to](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [defend yourself against a man armed with a Pineapple.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

#### [8.11](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [Debugging](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[When you use indices to traverse the values in a sequence, it is tricky to get the beginning](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [and end of the traversal right. Here is a function that is supposed to compare two words](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [and return](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [if one of the words is the reverse of the other, but it contains two errors:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[def is_reverse(word1, word2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [if len(word1) != len(word2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[i = 0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [j = len(word2)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[while j > 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [if word1[i] != word2[j]:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return False](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [i = i+1](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 8. Strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[j = j-1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[return True](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The first](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [statement checks whether the words are the same length. If not, we can return](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [immediately. Otherwise, for the rest of the function, we can assume that the words](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [are the same length. This is an example of the guardian pattern in Section 6.8.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

| find two letters that don’t match, we can return | False | immediately. If we get through the |
| ------------------------------------------------ | ----- | ---------------------------------- |
| whole loop and all the letters match, we return  | True  | .                                  |

[If we test this function with the words “pots” and “stop”, we expect the return value](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [True](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.format) [,](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.format) [but we get an IndexError:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [>>> is_reverse(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [pots](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [stop](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [...](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [File "reverse.py", line 15, in is_reverse](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [if word1[i] != word2[j]:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [IndexError: string index out of range](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [For debugging this kind of error, my first move is to print the values of the indices imme-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [diately before the line where the error appears.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [while j > 0:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [print(i, j)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [# print here](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[if word1[i] != word2[j]:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [i = i+1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [j = j-1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Now when I run the program again, I get more information:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [>>> is_reverse(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [pots](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [,](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [stop](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [)](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) 0 4 [...](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [IndexError: string index out of range](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [The first time through the loop, the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [j](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [is 4, which is out of range for the](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [string](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [pots](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [The index of the last character is 3, so the initial value for](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [j](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [should be](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [len(word2)-1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

If I fix that error and run the program again, I get: >>> is_reverse( ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [pots](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [stop](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) 0 3 1 2 2 1 True [This time we get the right answer, but it looks like the loop only ran three times, which is](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [suspicious. To get a better idea of what is happening, it is useful to draw a state diagram.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [During the first iteration, the frame for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is_reverse](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is shown in Figure 8.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[I took some license by arranging the variables in the frame and adding dotted lines to show](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [that the values of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [i](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [j](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [indicate characters in](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [word1](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [word2](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[Starting with this diagram, run the program on paper, changing the values of](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [i](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newline) [and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [j](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [during each iteration. Find and fix the second error in this function.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[8.12. Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[word1](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [’pots’](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [word2](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [’stop’](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[i](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [j](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)
