#### [8.12](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Glossary](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [Something a variable can refer to.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [For now, you can use “object” and “value”](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [interchangeably.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[sequence:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [An ordered collection of values where each value is identified by an integer](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [index.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[item:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [One of the values in a sequence.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[index:](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [An integer value used to select an item in a sequence, such as a character in a string.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [In Python indices start from 0.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[slice:](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [A part of a string specified by a range of indices.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[empty string:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [A string with no characters and length 0, represented by two quotation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [marks.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[immutable:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [The property of a sequence whose items cannot be changed.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[traverse:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [To iterate through the items in a sequence, performing a similar operation on](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [each.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[search:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [A pattern of traversal that stops when it finds what it is looking for.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[counter:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [A variable used to count something, usually initialized to zero and then incre-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [mented.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[invocation:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A statement that calls a method.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[optional argument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [A function or method argument that is not required.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

#### 8.13 [Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Exercise 8.1.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [Read the documentation of the string methods at](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [http: // docs. python. org/ 3/](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [library/ stdtypes. html# string-methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [. You might want to experiment with some of them](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [to make sure you understand how they work.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [strip](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and](http://gutenberg.org/) [replace](http://gutenberg.org/) [are particularly useful.](http://gutenberg.org/)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [documentation](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [uses](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [syntax](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [might](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [be](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [confusing.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [For](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [example,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [in](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [find(sub[, start[, end]])](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [, the brackets indicate optional arguments. So](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [sub](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is required, but](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [start](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is optional, and if you include](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [start](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [, then](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [end](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [is optional.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Exercise 8.2.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [There is a string method called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [count](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [that is similar to the function in Section 8.7.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [Read the documentation of this method and write an invocation that counts the number of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [’s in](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Exercise 8.3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [A string slice can take a third index that specifies the “step size”; that is, the number](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [of spaces between successive characters. A step size of 2 means every other character; 3 means every](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [third, etc.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 8. Strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> fruit =](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> fruit[0:5:2]](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [bnn](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[A step size of -1 goes through the word backwards, so the slice](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [::-1] generates a reversed string.

[Use this idiom to write a one-line version of](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [is_palindrome](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [from Exercise 6.3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [Exercise 8.4.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [The following functions are all](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [intended](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [to check whether a string contains any](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [lowercase letters, but at least some of them are wrong. For each function, describe what the function](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [actually does (assuming that the parameter is a string).](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[def any_lowercase1(s):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [for c in s:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [if c.islower():](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [return False](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[def any_lowercase2(s):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [for c in s:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [.islower():](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [else:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[def any_lowercase3(s):](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [for c in s:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [flag = c.islower()](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return flag](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[def any_lowercase4(s):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [flag = False](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [for c in s:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [flag = flag or c.islower()](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [return flag](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[def any_lowercase5(s):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) for c in s: [if not c.islower():](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return True](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [Exercise 8.5.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [A Caesar cypher is a weak form of encryption that involves “rotating” each letter by](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [a fixed number of places. To rotate a letter means to shift it through the alphabet, wrapping around](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to the beginning if necessary, so ’A’ rotated by 3 is ’D’ and ’Z’ rotated by 1 is ’A’.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[To rotate a word, rotate each letter by the same amount. For example, “cheer” rotated by 7 is “jolly”](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [and “melon” rotated by -10 is “cubed”. In the movie](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [2001: A Space Odyssey](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [, the ship computer](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [is called HAL, which is IBM rotated by -1.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [rotate_word](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [that takes a string and an integer as parameters, and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [a new string that contains the letters from the original string rotated by the given amount.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[You might want to use the built-in function](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [ord](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, which converts a character to a numeric code, and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[8.13. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[chr](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, which converts numeric codes to characters. Letters of the alphabet are encoded in alphabetical](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [order, so for example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> ord(](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [c](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [) - ord(](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [a](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Because](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [is the two-eth letter of the alphabet.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [But beware: the numeric codes for upper case](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) letters are different.

[Potentially offensive jokes on the Internet are sometimes encoded in ROT13, which is a Caesar](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [cypher with rotation 13. If you are not easily offended, find and decode some of them. Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ rotate. py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Chapter 8. Strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

## [Chapter 9](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Case study: word play](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[This chapter presents the second case study, which involves solving word puzzles by](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [searching for words that have certain properties. For example, we’ll find the longest palin-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [dromes in English and search for words whose letters appear in alphabetical order. And](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [I will present another program development plan: reduction to a previously solved prob-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [lem.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [9.1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Reading word lists](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[For the exercises in this chapter we need a list of English words. There are lots of word](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [lists available on the Web, but the one most suitable for our purpose is one of the word lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [collected and contributed to the public domain by Grady Ward as part of the Moby lexi-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [con project (see](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [). It is a list of 113,809 official](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [crosswords; that is, words that are considered valid in crossword puzzles and other word](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [games. In the Moby collection, the filename is](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [113809of.fic](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [; you can download a copy,](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [with the simpler name](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) , from [https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[This file is in plain text, so you can open it with a text editor, but you can also read it from](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Python. The built-in function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) open takes the name of the file as a parameter and returns a file object you can use to read the file.

[>>> fin = open(](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[fin](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [is a common name for a file object used for input.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [The file object provides several](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [methods for reading, including](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [readline](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, which reads characters from the file until it gets](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [to a newline and returns the result as a string:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> fin.readline()](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [aa\n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[The first word in this particular list is “aa”, which is a kind of lava.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [The sequence](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [\n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [represents the newline character that separates this word from the next.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[The file object keeps track of where it is in the file, so if you call](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [readline](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [again, you get](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [the next word:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> fin.readline()](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [aah\n](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 9. Case study: word play](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The next word is “aah”, which is a perfectly legitimate word, so stop looking at me like](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [that.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Or, if it’s the newline character that’s bothering you, we can get rid of it with the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [string method](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [strip](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> line = fin.readline()](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [>>> word = line.strip()](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) >>> word ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [aahed](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[You can also use a file object as part of a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [loop.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [This program reads](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [prints each word, one per line:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[fin = open(](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [words.txt](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [for line in fin:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [word = line.strip()](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [print(word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

#### 9.2 [Exercises](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[There are solutions to these exercises in the next section. You should at least attempt each](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [one before you read the solutions.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Exercise 9.1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Write a program that reads](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [and prints only the words with more than 20](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [characters (not counting whitespace).](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Exercise 9.2.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [In 1939 Ernest Vincent Wright published a 50,000 word novel called](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Gadsby](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [does not contain the letter “e”. Since “e” is the most common letter in English, that’s not easy to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) do.

[In fact, it is difficult to construct a solitary thought without using that most common symbol. It is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [slow going at first, but with caution and hours of training you can gradually gain facility.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[All right, I’ll stop now.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [has_no_e](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [that returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) True [if the given word doesn’t have the letter “e” in](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Write a program that reads](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) words.txt and prints only the words that have no “e”. Compute the percentage of words in the list that have no “e”. Exercise 9.3. [Write a function named](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [avoids](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [that takes a word and a string of forbidden letters,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [and that returns](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [if the word doesn’t use any of the forbidden letters.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Write a program that prompts the user to enter a string of forbidden letters and then prints the](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [number of words that don’t contain any of them. Can you find a combination of 5 forbidden letters](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [that excludes the smallest number of words?](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [Exercise 9.4.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [Write a function named](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [uses_only](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [that takes a word and a string of letters, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [that returns](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [True](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [if the word contains only letters in the list. Can you make a sentence using only](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [the letters](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [acefhlo](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [? Other than “Hoe alfalfa”?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Exercise 9.5.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [Write a function named](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [uses_all](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [that takes a word and a string of required letters,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and that returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [if the word uses all the required letters at least once. How many words are](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [there that use all the vowels](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [aeiou](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [? How about](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [aeiouy](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [?](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [Exercise 9.6.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Write a function called](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is_abecedarian](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [that returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [if the letters in a word](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [appear in alphabetical order (double letters are ok). How many abecedarian words are there?](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[9.3. Search](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [9.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Search](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[All of the exercises in the previous section have something in common; they can be solved](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [with the search pattern we saw in Section 8.6. The simplest example is:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[def has_no_e(word):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [for letter in word:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [if letter ==](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [e](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [return False](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [loop traverses the characters in](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [word](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [. If we find the letter “e”, we can immediately](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [return](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [False](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [; otherwise we have to go to the next letter. If we exit the loop normally, that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [means we didn’t find an “e”, so we return](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [True](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[You could write this function more concisely using the](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [in](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [operator, but I started with this](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [version because it demonstrates the logic of the search pattern.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[avoids](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [is a more general version of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [has_no_e](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [but it has the same structure:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def avoids(word, forbidden):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [for letter in word:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [if letter in forbidden:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [return False](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [return True](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

| We can return | False | as soon as we find a forbidden letter; if we get to the end of the loop, |
| ------------- | ----- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| we return     | True  | .                                                                        |

[uses_only](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is similar except that the sense of the condition is reversed:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def uses_only(word, available):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [for letter in word:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [if letter not in available:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return False](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [return True](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Instead of a list of forbidden letters, we have a list of available letters. If we find a letter in](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [word](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [that is not in](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) available , we can return False .

[uses_all](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [is similar except that we reverse the role of the word and the string of letters:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

def uses_all(word, required): [for letter in required:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [if letter not in word:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Instead of traversing the letters in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [word](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, the loop traverses the required letters. If any of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [required letters do not appear in the word, we can return](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[If you were really thinking like a computer scientist, you would have recognized that](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [uses_all](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [was an instance of a previously solved problem, and you would have written:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def uses_all(word, required):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [return uses_only(required, word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[This is an example of a program development plan called](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [reduction to a previously solved](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [problem](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, which means that you recognize the problem you are working on as an instance](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [of a solved problem and apply an existing solution.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 9. Case study: word play](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [9.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Looping with indices](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[I wrote the functions in the previous section with](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [loops because I only needed the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [characters in the strings; I didn’t have to do anything with the indices.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

For [is_abecedarian](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [we have to compare adjacent letters, which is a little tricky with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) for loop:

[def is_abecedarian(word):](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [previous = word[0]](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [for c in word:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [if c < previous:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [return False](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [previous = c](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[An alternative is to use recursion:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[def is_abecedarian(word):](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [if len(word) <= 1:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [return True](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [if word[0] > word[1]:](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [return is_abecedarian(word[1:])](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Another option is to use a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [while](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [loop:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[def is_abecedarian(word):](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [i = 0](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [while i < len(word)-1:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [if word[i+1] < word[i]:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [i = i+1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The loop starts at](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [i=0](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [and ends when](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [i=len(word)-1](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [. Each time through the loop, it com-](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [pares the](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [i](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [th character (which you can think of as the current character) to the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [i](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [1th](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [character (which you can think of as the next).](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[If the next character is less than (alphabetically before) the current one, then we have dis-](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) covered a break in the abecedarian trend, and we return False .

[If we get to the end of the loop without finding a fault, then the word passes the test. To](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [convince yourself that the loop ends correctly, consider an example like](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [flossy](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [The](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [length of the word is 6, so the last time the loop runs is when](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [i](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is 4, which is the index of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [the second-to-last character. On the last iteration, it compares the second-to-last character](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [to the last, which is what we want.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Here is a version of](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is_palindrome](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [(see Exercise 6.3) that uses two indices; one starts at](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [the beginning and goes up; the other starts at the end and goes down.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def is_palindrome(word):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [i = 0](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [j = len(word)-1](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[while i<j:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [if word[i] != word[j]:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[9.5. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [i = i+1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [j = j-1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Or we could reduce to a previously solved problem and write:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[def is_palindrome(word):](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [return is_reverse(word, word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[Using](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [is_reverse](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [from Section 8.11.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

#### [9.5](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Testing programs is hard. The functions in this chapter are relatively easy to test because](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [you can check the results by hand. Even so, it is somewhere between difficult and impos-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [sible to choose a set of words that test for all possible errors.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Taking](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [has_no_e](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [as an example, there are two obvious cases to check: words that have an](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [‘e’ should return](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [, and words that don’t should return](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [You should have no](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [trouble coming up with one of each.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Within each case, there are some less obvious subcases. Among the words that have an](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [“e”, you should test words with an “e” at the beginning, the end, and somewhere in the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [middle. You should test long words, short words, and very short words, like the empty](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [string. The empty string is an example of a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) special case [, which is one of the non-obvious](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [cases where errors often lurk.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[In addition to the test cases you generate, you can also test your program with a word list](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [like](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. By scanning the output, you might be able to catch errors, but be careful:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [you might catch one kind of error (words that should not be included, but are) and not](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [another (words that should be included, but aren’t).](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[In general, testing can help you find bugs, but it is not easy to generate a good set of](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [test cases, and even if you do, you can’t be sure your program is correct. According to a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [legendary computer scientist:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [their absence!](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

- [— Edsger W. Dijkstra](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

#### [9.6](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[file object:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [A value that represents an open file.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[reduction to a previously solved problem:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [A way of solving a problem by expressing it](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [as an instance of a previously solved problem.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[special case:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [A test case that is atypical or non-obvious (and less likely to be handled cor-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [rectly).](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 9. Case study: word play](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [9.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Exercise 9.7.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [This question is based on a Puzzler that was broadcast on the radio program](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [Car](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [Talk](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [http: // www. cartalk. com/ content/ puzzlers](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Give me a word with three consecutive double letters. I’ll give you a couple of words](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [that almost qualify, but don’t. For example, the word committee, c-o-m-m-i-t-t-e-e. It](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [would be great except for the ‘i’ that sneaks in there. Or Mississippi: M-i-s-s-i-s-s-i-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [p-p-i. If you could take out those i’s it would work. But there is a word that has three](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [consecutive pairs of letters and to the best of my knowledge this may be the only word.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [Of course there are probably 500 more but I can only think of one. What is the word?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Write a program to find it. Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ cartalk1. py](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Exercise](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [9.8.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Here’s](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [another](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) Car Talk Puzzler [(](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [http: // www. cartalk. com/ content/](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [puzzlers](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[“I was driving on the highway the other day and I happened to notice my odometer.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Like most odometers, it shows six digits, in whole miles only. So, if my car had 300,000](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [miles, for example, I’d see 3-0-0-0-0-0.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[“Now, what I saw that day was very interesting. I noticed that the last 4 digits were](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [palindromic; that is, they read the same forward as backward. For example, 5-4-4-5 is a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [palindrome, so my odometer could have read 3-1-5-4-4-5.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[“One mile later, the last 5 numbers were palindromic. For example, it could have read](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [3-6-5-4-5-6. One mile after that, the middle 4 out of 6 numbers were palindromic. And](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [you ready for this? One mile later, all 6 were palindromic!](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[“The question is, what was on the odometer when I first looked?”](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Write a Python program that tests all the six-digit numbers and prints any numbers that satisfy](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [these requirements. Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ cartalk2. py](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk2.py) [Exercise 9.9.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Here’s another](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Car Talk](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Puzzler you can solve with a search (](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [http: // www.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [cartalk. com/ content/ puzzlers](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[“Recently I had a visit with my mom and we realized that the two digits that make](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [up my age when reversed resulted in her age.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [For example, if she’s 73, I’m 37.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [We](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) wondered how often this has happened over the years but we got sidetracked with other topics and we never came up with an answer.

[“When I got home I figured out that the digits of our ages have been reversible six times](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [so far. I also figured out that if we’re lucky it would happen again in a few years, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [if we’re really lucky it would happen one more time after that. In other words, it would](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [have happened 8 times over all. So the question is, how old am I now?”](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Write a Python program that searches for solutions to this Puzzler. Hint: you might find the string](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) method [zfill](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [useful.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ cartalk3. py](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

## [Chapter 10](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[This chapter presents one of Python’s most useful built-in types, lists. You will also learn](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [more about objects and what can happen when you have more than one name for the same](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

#### [10.1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A list is a sequence](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Like a string, a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [list](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is a sequence of values. In a string, the values are characters; in a list,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [they can be any type. The values in a list are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [elements](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [or sometimes](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [items](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[There are several ways to create a new list; the simplest is to enclose the elements in square](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [brackets (](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [[](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [and](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) []](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[[10, 20, 30, 40]](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [crunchy frog](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [ram bladder](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [lark vomit](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ' ]

[The first example is a list of four integers. The second is a list of three strings. The elements](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) of a list don’t have to be the same type. The following list contains a string, a float, an [integer, and (lo!) another list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[[](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [spam](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [, 2.0, 5, [10, 20]]](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[A list within another list is](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [nested](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[A list that contains no elements is called an empty list; you can create one with empty](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [brackets,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [[]](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[As you might expect, you can assign list values to variables:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[>>> cheeses = [](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_duck_debugging) [Cheddar](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [Edam](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [Gouda](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) []](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> numbers = [42, 123]](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> empty = []](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> print(cheeses, numbers, empty)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [[](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Cheddar](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Edam](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Gouda](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [] [42, 123] []](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 10. Lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[’Gouda’](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Figure 10.1: State diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

#### 10.2 [Lists are mutable](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The syntax for accessing the elements of a list is the same as for accessing the characters](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [of a string—the bracket operator. The expression inside the brackets specifies the index.](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [Remember that the indices start at 0:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> cheeses[0]](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [Cheddar](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Unlike strings, lists are mutable. When the bracket operator appears on the left side of an](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [assignment, it identifies the element of the list that will be assigned.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> numbers = [42, 123]](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> numbers[1] = 5](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [>>> numbers](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [[42, 5]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [The one-eth element of](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [numbers](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, which used to be 123, is now 5.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Figure 10.1 shows the state diagram for](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) cheeses , [numbers](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [empty](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Lists are represented by boxes with the word “list” outside and the elements of the list](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) inside. [cheeses](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [refers to a list with three elements indexed 0, 1 and 2.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) numbers contains two elements; the diagram shows that the value of the second element has been reassigned from 123 to 5. [empty](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [refers to a list with no elements.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[List indices work the same way as string indices:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

- [• Any integer expression can be used as an index.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

- [• If you try to read or write an element that does not exist, you get an](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [IndexError](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

- [• If an index has a negative value, it counts backward from the end of the list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

The in [operator also works on lists.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [>>> cheeses = [](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [Cheddar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_duck_debugging) ['](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_duck_debugging) [,](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_duck_debugging) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [Edam](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [Gouda](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) >>> ' [Edam](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [in cheeses](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) True >>> ' [Brie](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [in cheeses](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) False

[10.3. Traversing a list](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [10.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Traversing a list](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[The most common way to traverse the elements of a list is with a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [loop. The syntax is](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [the same as for strings:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[for cheese in cheeses:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [print(cheese)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[This works well if you only need to read the elements of the list. But if you want to write](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [or update the elements, you need the indices. A common way to do that is to combine the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [built-in functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [range](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [len](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[for i in range(len(numbers)):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [numbers[i] = numbers[i] * 2](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[This loop traverses the list and updates each element.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [len](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [returns the number of elements](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [in the list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [range](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [returns a list of indices from 0 to](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) n − 1, where n is the length of the list. [Each time through the loop](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [i](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [gets the index of the next element. The assignment statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [in the body uses](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [i](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [to read the old value of the element and to assign the new value.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[A](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [loop over an empty list never runs the body:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[for x in []:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [print(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [This never happens.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Although a list can contain another list, the nested list still counts as a single element. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [length of this list is four:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[[](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [spam](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, 1, [](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [Brie](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Roquefort](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Pol le Veq](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [], [1, 2, 3]]](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [10.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [List operations](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [+](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [operator concatenates lists:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> a = [1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [>>> b = [4, 5, 6]](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> c = a + b](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [>>> c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

The * [operator repeats a list a given number of times:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> [0] * 4](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [[0, 0, 0, 0]](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [>>> [1, 2, 3] * 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [[1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[The first example repeats](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [[0]](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [four times. The second example repeats the list](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [[1, 2, 3]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [three times.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

#### [10.5](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [List slices](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[The slice operator also works on lists:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 10. Lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> t = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [e](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [f](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [>>> t[1:3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [c](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) []](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> t[:4]](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) >>> t[3:] [[](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [d](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [e](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [f](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) []](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[If you omit the first index, the slice starts at the beginning. If you omit the second, the slice](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [goes to the end. So if you omit both, the slice is a copy of the whole list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[>>> t[:]](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [e](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [f](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py)

[Since lists are mutable, it is often useful to make a copy before performing operations that](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [modify lists.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[A slice operator on the left side of an assignment can update multiple elements:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> t = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [d](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) []](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> t[1:3] = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) >>> t [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [y](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)
