#### [10.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [List methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[Python provides methods that operate on lists. For example,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [append](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [adds a new element](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to the end of a list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> t = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) []](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [>>> t.append(](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [d](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> t](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py)

[extend](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [takes a list as an argument and appends all of the elements:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

>>> t1 = [ ' a ' , ' b ' , ' c ' ] >>> t2 = [ ' d ' , ' e ' ] [>>> t1.extend(t2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) >>> t1 [ ' a ' , ' b ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [e](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[This example leaves](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [t2](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [unmodified.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

sort [arranges the elements of the list from low to high:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[>>> t = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [e](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) []](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [>>> t.sort()](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [ ' a ' , ' [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/double.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [e](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Most list methods are void; they modify the list and return](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [None](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [. If you accidentally write](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [t = t.sort()](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, you will be disappointed with the result.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[10.7. Map, filter and reduce](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [10.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Map, filter and reduce](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[To add up all the numbers in a list, you can use a loop like this:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[def add_all(t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [total = 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) for x in t: [total += x](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [return total](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[total](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [is initialized to 0.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [Each time through the loop,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [x](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [gets one element from the list.](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [+=](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [operator provides a short way to update a variable. This](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [augmented assignment](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book1.py) [statement](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[total += x](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[is equivalent to](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[total = total + x](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[As the loop runs,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [total](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [accumulates the sum of the elements; a variable used this way is](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [sometimes called an](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [accumulator](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[Adding up the elements of a list is such a common operation that Python provides it as a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [built-in function,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [sum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[>>> t = [1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> sum(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[An operation like this that combines a sequence of elements into a single value is some-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [times called](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [reduce](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Sometimes you want to traverse one list while building another. For example, the following](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [function takes a list of strings and returns a new list that contains capitalized strings:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def capitalize_all(t):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [res = []](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [for s in t:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [res.append(s.capitalize())](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [return res](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

res is initialized with an empty list; each time through the loop, we append the next ele- ment. So [res](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [is another kind of accumulator.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[An operation like](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [capitalize_all](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is sometimes called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [map](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [because it “maps” a function](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [(in this case the method](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [capitalize](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [) onto each of the elements in a sequence.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Another common operation is to select some of the elements from a list and return a sublist.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [For example, the following function takes a list of strings and returns a list that contains](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [only the uppercase strings:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def only_upper(t):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [res = []](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [for s in t:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [if s.isupper():](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [res.append(s)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return res](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 10. Lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[isupper](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is a string method that returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [if the string contains only upper case letters.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[An operation like](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [only_upper](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is called a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [filter](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [because it selects some of the elements and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [filters out the others.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[Most common list operations can be expressed as a combination of map, filter and reduce.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [10.8](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Deleting elements](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[There are several ways to delete elements from a list. If you know the index of the element](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [you want, you can use](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [pop](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> t = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [>>> x = t.pop(1)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>>> t](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [ ' a ' , ' [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [>>> x](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [b](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) pop [modifies the list and returns the element that was removed. If you don’t provide an](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [index, it deletes and returns the last element.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[If you don’t need the removed value, you can use the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [del](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [operator:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> t = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> del t[1]](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) []](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [If you know the element you want to remove (but not the index), you can use](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [remove](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [>>> t = [](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [>>> t.remove(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> t](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [[](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [a](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [c](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) []](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The return value from](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [remove](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [None](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py)

[To remove more than one element, you can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [del](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [with a slice index:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [>>> t = [](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [b](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [c](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [d](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [e](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [f](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) []](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) >>> del t[1:5] >>> t [ ' a ' , ' f ' ] [As usual, the slice selects all the elements up to but not including the second index.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

#### 10.9 [Lists and strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[A string is a sequence of characters and a list is a sequence of values, but a list of characters](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is not the same as a string. To convert from a string to a list of characters, you can use](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [list](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

>>> s = ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> t = list(s)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) >>> t [ ' s ' , ' p ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [m](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) []](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[10.10. Objects and values](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

| a   | ’banana’ | a   |
| --- | -------- | --- |
| b   | ’banana’ | b   |

[Figure 10.2: State diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Because](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [list](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [is the name of a built-in function, you should avoid using it as a variable](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [name. I also avoid](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [l](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [because it looks too much like](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [. So that’s why I use](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [list](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [function breaks a string into individual letters. If you want to break a string into](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [words, you can use the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [split](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [method:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>> s =](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [pining for the fjords](http://www.thinkpython.com/) ['](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [>>> t = s.split()](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [pining](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [the](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [fjords](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[An optional argument called a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [delimiter](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [specifies which characters to use as word bound-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [aries. The following example uses a hyphen as a delimiter:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> s =](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [spam-spam-spam](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [>>> delimiter =](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [-](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [>>> t = s.split(delimiter)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [spam](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) []](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[join](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is the inverse of](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [split](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [. It takes a list of strings and concatenates the elements.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [join](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [a string method, so you have to invoke it on the delimiter and pass the list as a parameter:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> t = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [pining](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [,](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [for](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [,](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [the](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [,](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [fjords](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) []](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [>>> delimiter =](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [' '](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [>>> s = delimiter.join(t)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> s](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [pining for the fjords](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) '

[In this case the delimiter is a space character, so](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [join](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [puts a space between words.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [To](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [concatenate strings without spaces, you can use the empty string,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) '' , as a delimiter.

#### [10.10](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [Objects and values](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[If we run these assignment statements:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

| a = | '   | banana | '   |
| --- | --- | ------ | --- |
| b = | '   | banana | '   |

[We know that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [both refer to a string, but we don’t know whether they refer to the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [same](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [string. There are two possible states, shown in Figure 10.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[In one case,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [refer to two different objects that have the same value. In the second](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [case, they refer to the same object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[To check whether two variables refer to the same object, you can use the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_db.py) [operator.](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Chapter 10. Lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[a](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [[ 1, 2, 3 ]](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [[ 1, 2, 3 ]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[Figure 10.3: State diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [[ 1, 2, 3 ]](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [b](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Figure 10.4: State diagram.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

>>> a = ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [>>> b =](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [banana](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>>> a is b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [True](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[In this example, Python only created one string object, and both](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) b [refer to it. But](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [when you create two lists, you get two objects:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> a = [1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [>>> b = [1, 2, 3]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> a is b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py)

[So the state diagram looks like Figure 10.3.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[In this case we would say that the two lists are](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) equivalent , because they have the same el- [ements, but not](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [identical](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, because they are not the same object. If two objects are identical,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [they are also equivalent, but if they are equivalent, they are not necessarily identical.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Until now, we have been using “object” and “value” interchangeably, but it is more precise](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to say that an object has a value. If you evaluate](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [[1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [, you get a list object whose](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [value is a sequence of integers.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [If another list has the same elements, we say it has the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [same value, but it is not the same object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

#### 10.11 Aliasing

If a [refers to an object and you assign](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [b = a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, then both variables refer to the same object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> a = [1, 2, 3]](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [>>> b = a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> b is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) True

[The state diagram looks like Figure 10.4.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[The association of a variable with an object is called a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [reference](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [. In this example, there are](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [two references to the same object.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[An object with more than one reference has more than one name, so we say that the object](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) is [aliased](https://thinkpython.com/code/double.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/double.py)

[If the aliased object is mutable, changes made with one alias affect the other:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[10.12. List arguments](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[list](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [__main__](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [letters](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [’a’](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[’b’](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [delete_head](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [t](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ’c’

[Figure 10.5: Stack diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[>>> b[0] = 42](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [>>> a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [[42, 2, 3]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Although this behavior can be useful, it is error-prone.](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [In general, it is safer to avoid](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [aliasing when you are working with mutable objects.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[For immutable objects like strings, aliasing is not as much of a problem. In this example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

| a = | '   | banana | '   |
| --- | --- | ------ | --- |
| b = | '   | banana | '   |

[It almost never makes a difference whether](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) b [refer to the same string or not.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py)

#### [10.12](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [List arguments](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[When you pass a list to a function, the function gets a reference to the list. If the function](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [modifies the list, the caller sees the change. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) delete_head [removes the first](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [element from a list:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def delete_head(t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [del t[0]](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Here’s how it is used:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> letters = [](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [a](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [b](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [>>> delete_head(letters)](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> letters](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [[](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [b](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [c](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) []](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

The parameter t and the variable letters are aliases for the same object. The stack diagram looks like Figure 10.5.

[Since the list is shared by two frames, I drew it between them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[It is important to distinguish between operations that modify lists and operations that cre-](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [ate new lists. For example, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [append](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [method modifies a list, but the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [operator creates a](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [new list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[Here’s an example using](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [append](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[>>> t1 = [1, 2]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [>>> t2 = t1.append(3)](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> t1](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [[1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> t2](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [None](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 10. Lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The return value from](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [append](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [is](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Here’s an example using the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [+](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [operator:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[>>> t3 = t1 + [4]](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) >>> t1 [1, 2, 3] [>>> t3](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [[1, 2, 3, 4]](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[The result of the operator is a new list, and the original list is unchanged.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[This difference is important when you write functions that are supposed to modify lists.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [For example, this function](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [does not](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [delete the head of a list:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[def bad_delete_head(t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [t = t[1:]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [# WRONG!](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[The slice operator creates a new list and the assignment makes](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [t](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [refer to it, but that doesn’t](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [affect the caller.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> t4 = [1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [>>> bad_delete_head(t4)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> t4](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [[1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[At the beginning of](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [bad_delete_head](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [t](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [t4](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [refer to the same list. At the end,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [refers](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [to a new list, but](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [t4](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [still refers to the original, unmodified list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[An alternative is to write a function that creates and returns a new list. For example,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [tail](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [returns all but the first element of a list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[def tail(t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return t[1:]](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[This function leaves the original list unmodified. Here’s how it is used:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[>>> letters = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [>>> rest = tail(letters)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) >>> rest [ ' b ' , ' c ' ]

#### 10.13 [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Careless use of lists (and other mutable objects) can lead to long hours of debugging. Here](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [are some common pitfalls and ways to avoid them:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

1. [1. Most list methods modify the argument and return](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [. This is the opposite of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [string methods, which return a new string and leave the original alone.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[If you are used to writing string code like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[word = word.strip()](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[It is tempting to write list code like this:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[10.13. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[t = t.sort()](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [# WRONG!](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Because](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [sort](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [returns](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [None](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [, the next operation you perform with](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [is likely to fail.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[Before using list methods and operators, you should read the documentation care-](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [fully and then test them in interactive mode.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

2. [2. Pick an idiom and stick with it.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Part of the problem with lists is that there are too many ways to do things. For exam-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [ple, to remove an element from a list, you can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [pop](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [,](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [remove](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [,](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [del](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [, or even a slice](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [assignment.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[To add an element, you can use the](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [append](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [method or the](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [operator. Assuming that](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [is a list and](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [is a list element, these are correct:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[t.append(x)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [t = t + [x]](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [t += [x]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[And these are wrong:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

| t.append([x])   | # WRONG! |
| --------------- | -------- |
| t = t.append(x) | # WRONG! |
| t + [x]         | # WRONG! |
| t = t + x       | # WRONG! |

[Try out each of these examples in interactive mode to make sure you understand](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [what they do. Notice that only the last one causes a runtime error; the other three are](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [legal, but they do the wrong thing.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

3. [3. Make copies to avoid aliasing.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[If you want to use a method like](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [sort](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [that modifies the argument, but you need to](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [keep the original list as well, you can make a copy.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[>>> t = [3, 1, 2]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) >>> t2 = t[:] [>>> t2.sort()](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [[3, 1, 2]](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [>>> t2](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [[1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[In this example you could also use the built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [sorted](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [, which returns a new,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [sorted list and leaves the original alone.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[>>> t2 = sorted(t)](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [[3, 1, 2]](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> t2](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [[1, 2, 3]](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 10. Lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [10.14](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [A sequence of values.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[element:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [One of the values in a list (or other sequence), also called items.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[nested list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [A list that is an element of another list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[accumulator:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [A variable used in a loop to add up or accumulate a result.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[augmented assignment:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [A statement that updates the value of a variable using an opera-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [tor like](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [+=](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[reduce:](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [A processing pattern that traverses a sequence and accumulates the elements into](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [a single result.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[map:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A processing pattern that traverses a sequence and performs an operation on each](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [element.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[filter:](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [A processing pattern that traverses a list and selects the elements that satisfy some](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [criterion.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

object: [Something a variable can refer to. An object has a type and a value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[equivalent:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Having the same value.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[identical:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Being the same object (which implies equivalence).](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[reference:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The association between a variable and its value.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[aliasing:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [A circumstance where two or more variables refer to the same object.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[delimiter:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [A character or string used to indicate where a string should be split.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

For example:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

>>> t = [[1, 2], [3], [4, 5, 6]] [>>> nested_sum(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Exercise 10.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [cumsum](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [that takes a list of numbers and returns the cumu-](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [lative sum; that is, a new list where the ith element is the sum of the first i](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [+](http://gutenberg.org/) [elements from the](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [original list. For example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[>>> t = [1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [>>> cumsum(t)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [[1, 3, 6]](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [Exercise 10.3.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [Write a function called](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [middle](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [that takes a list and returns a new list that contains](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [all but the first and last elements. For example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> t = [1, 2, 3, 4]](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> middle(t)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [2, 3]

[10.15. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Exercise 10.4.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [chop](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [that takes a list, modifies it by removing the first and](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [last elements, and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. For example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> t = [1, 2, 3, 4]](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> chop(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [2, 3] [Exercise 10.5.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Write a function called](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [is_sorted](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [that takes a list as a parameter and returns](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) True [if the list is sorted in ascending order and](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [False](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [otherwise. For example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[>>> is_sorted([1, 2, 2])](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [True](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> is_sorted([](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [])](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [Exercise 10.6.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Two words are anagrams if you can rearrange the letters from one to spell the other.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) is_anagram that takes two strings and returns True if they are anagrams. [Exercise 10.7.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [has_duplicates](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [that takes a list and returns](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [True](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) if there [is any element that appears more than once. It should not modify the original list.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Exercise 10.8.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [This exercise pertains to the so-called Birthday Paradox, which you can read about](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [at](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [http: // en. wikipedia. org/ wiki/ Birthday_ paradox](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[If there are 23 students in your class, what are the chances that two of them have the same birthday?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [You can estimate this probability by generating random samples of 23 birthdays and checking for](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [matches.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Hint: you can generate random birthdays with the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [randint](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [function in the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [random](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [module.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[You can download my solution from](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ birthday. py](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Exercise 10.9.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Write a function that reads the file](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) words.txt [and builds a list with one element](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [per word. Write two versions of this function, one using the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [append](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [method and the other using](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [the idiom](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [t = t + [x]](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [. Which one takes longer to run? Why?](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ wordlist. py](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [Exercise 10.10.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [To check whether a word is in the word list, you could use the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [in](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [operator, but it](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [would be slow because it searches through the words in order.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Because the words are in alphabetical order, we can speed things up with a bisection search (also](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [known as binary search), which is similar to what you do when you look a word up in the dictionary](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [(the book, not the data structure). You start in the middle and check to see whether the word you are](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) looking for comes before the word in the middle of the list. If so, you search the first half of the list [the same way. Otherwise you search the second half.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Either way, you cut the remaining search space in half. If the word list has 113,809 words, it will](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [take about 17 steps to find the word or conclude that it’s not there.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [in_bisect](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [that takes a sorted list and a target value and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [the word is in the list and](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [if it’s not.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Or you could read the documentation of the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [bisect](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [module and use that!](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Solution:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [https:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [// thinkpython. com/ code/ inlist. py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [Exercise 10.11.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [Two words are a “reverse pair” if each is the reverse of the other. Write a program](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [that finds all the reverse pairs in the word list. Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [reverse_ pair. py](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Exercise 10.12.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Two words “interlock” if taking alternating letters from each forms a new](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [word.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [For example, “shoe” and “cold” interlock to form “schooled”.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_db.py) [https: //](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)
