2. [2. Can you find any words that are three-way interlocked; that is, every third letter forms a](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [word, starting from the first, second or third?](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

## [Chapter 11](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Dictionaries](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[This chapter presents another built-in type called a dictionary.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Dictionaries are one of](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [Python’s best features; they are the building blocks of many efficient and elegant algo-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [rithms.](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

#### [11.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [A dictionary is a mapping](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[A](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [dictionary](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [is like a list, but more general. In a list, the indices have to be integers; in a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [dictionary they can be (almost) any type.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[A dictionary contains a collection of indices, which are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [keys](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, and a collection of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [values. Each key is associated with a single value. The association of a key and a value is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [key-value pair](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [or sometimes an](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [item](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [.](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[In mathematical language, a dictionary represents a](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [mapping](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [from keys to values, so you](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [can also say that each key “maps to” a value. As an example, we’ll build a dictionary that](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [maps from English to Spanish words, so the keys and the values are all strings.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[The function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) dict creates a new dictionary with no items. Because dict is the name of a built-in function, you should avoid using it as a variable name.

[>>> eng2sp = dict()](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> eng2sp](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [{}](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[The squiggly-brackets,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [{}](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, represent an empty dictionary. To add items to the dictionary,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [you can use square brackets:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[>>> eng2sp[](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [one](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [] =](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [uno](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[This line creates an item that maps from the key](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [one](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [to the value](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [uno](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [. If we print the](https://thinkpython.com/code/inlist.py) [dictionary again, we see a key-value pair with a colon between the key and value:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[>>> eng2sp](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [{](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [one](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [uno](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [}](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[This output format is also an input format. For example, you can create a new dictionary](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [with three items:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 11. Dictionaries](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> eng2sp = {](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [one](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [uno](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [two](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [dos](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [three](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [tres](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [}](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

But if you print eng2sp [, you might be surprised:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[>>> eng2sp](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [{](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [one](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [uno](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [three](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [tres](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [two](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [dos](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [}](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

| The order of the key-value pairs might not be the same. | If you type the same example        |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| on your computer, you might get a different result.     | In general, the order of items in a |

[But that’s not a problem because the elements of a dictionary are never indexed with inte-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [ger indices. Instead, you use the keys to look up the corresponding values:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>> eng2sp[](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [two](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ' dos '

[The key](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [two](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [always maps to the value](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [dos](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [so the order of the items doesn’t matter.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[If the key isn’t in the dictionary, you get an exception:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> eng2sp[](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [four](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) []](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [KeyError:](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [four](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [len](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [function works on dictionaries; it returns the number of key-value pairs:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[>>> len(eng2sp)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [in](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [operator works on dictionaries, too; it tells you whether something appears as a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [key](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [in the dictionary (appearing as a value is not good enough).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

>>> ' one ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [in eng2sp](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) True >>> ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [uno](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [in eng2sp](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [False](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings)

[To see whether something appears as a value in a dictionary, you can use the method](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [values](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, which returns a collection of values, and then use the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [in](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [operator:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[>>> vals = eng2sp.values()](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>>](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [uno](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [in vals](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) True

The in operator uses different algorithms for lists and dictionaries. For lists, it searches the [elements of the list in order, as in Section 8.6. As the list gets longer, the search time gets](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [longer in direct proportion.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Python dictionaries use a data structure called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [hashtable](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [that has a remarkable property:](http://gutenberg.org/) the in [operator takes about the same amount of time no matter how many items are in the](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [dictionary. I explain how that’s possible in Section B.4, but the explanation might not make](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [sense until you’ve read a few more chapters.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

#### 11.2 [Dictionary as a collection of counters](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Suppose you are given a string and you want to count how many times each letter appears.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [There are several ways you could do it:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[11.2. Dictionary as a collection of counters](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

1. [1. You could create 26 variables, one for each letter of the alphabet. Then you could tra-](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [verse the string and, for each character, increment the corresponding counter, proba-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [bly using a chained conditional.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

2. [2. You could create a list with 26 elements. Then you could convert each character to](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [a number (using the built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [ord](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [), use the number as an index into the list,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [and increment the appropriate counter.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

3. [3. You could create a dictionary with characters as keys and counters as the correspond-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [ing values. The first time you see a character, you would add an item to the dictionary.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [After that you would increment the value of an existing item.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Each of these options performs the same computation, but each of them implements that](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [computation in a different way.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[An](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [implementation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [is a way of performing a computation; some implementations are](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [better than others. For example, an advantage of the dictionary implementation is that we](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [don’t have to know ahead of time which letters appear in the string and we only have to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [make room for the letters that do appear.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Here is what the code might look like:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[def histogram(s):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [d = dict()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [for c in s:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [if c not in d:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [d[c] = 1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [else:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [d[c] += 1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return d](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[The name of the function is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [histogram](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [, which is a statistical term for a collection of counters](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [(or frequencies).](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The first line of the function creates an empty dictionary. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [loop traverses the string.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [Each time through the loop, if the character](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [is not in the dictionary, we create a new item](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [with key](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [c](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [and the initial value 1 (since we have seen this letter once). If](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [is already in the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [dictionary we increment](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) d[c] .

Here’s how it works:

[>>> h = histogram(](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [brontosaurus](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> h](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [{](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [: 1,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [: 1,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [o](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [: 2,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [: 1,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [s](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [: 2,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [r](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [: 2,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [u](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [: 2,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ' t ' : 1}

[The histogram indicates that the letters](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [appear once;](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [o](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [appears twice, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [so on.](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[Dictionaries have a method called](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [get](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [that takes a key and a default value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [If the key](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [appears in the dictionary,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [get](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [returns the corresponding value; otherwise it returns the](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [default value. For example:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[>>> h = histogram(](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [>>> h](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [{](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [: 1}](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [>>> h.get(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, 0)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 11. Dictionaries](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> h.get(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, 0)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[As an exercise, use](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [get](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [to write](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [histogram](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [more concisely. You should be able to eliminate](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [the](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [statement.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [11.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Looping and dictionaries](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[If you use a dictionary in a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [statement, it traverses the keys of the dictionary. For exam-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [ple,](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [print_hist](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [prints each key and the corresponding value:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[def print_hist(h):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [for c in h:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [print(c, h[c])](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Here’s what the output looks like:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[>>> h = histogram(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [parrot](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> print_hist(h)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) a 1 [p 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [r 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [t 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [o 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py)

[Again, the keys are in no particular order. To traverse the keys in sorted order, you can use](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [sorted](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[>>> for key in sorted(h):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ... [print(key, h[key])](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) a 1 o 1 [p 1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [r 2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [t 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

#### 11.4 Reverse lookup

Given a dictionary [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [and a key](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [k](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, it is easy to find the corresponding value](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [v = d[k]](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. This](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [operation is called a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [lookup](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[But what if you have](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [v](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and you want to find](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [k](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [? You have two problems: first, there might](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [be more than one key that maps to the value](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [v](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [. Depending on the application, you might](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [be able to pick one, or you might have to make a list that contains all of them. Second,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [there is no simple syntax to do a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [reverse lookup](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [; you have to search.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Here is a function that takes a value and returns the first key that maps to that value:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def reverse_lookup(d, v):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [for k in d:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [if d[k] == v:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return k](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [raise LookupError()](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[11.5. Dictionaries and lists](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This function is yet another example of the search pattern, but it uses a feature we haven’t](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [seen before,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [raise](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [raise statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [causes an exception; in this case it causes a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [LookupError](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, which is a built-in exception used to indicate that a lookup operation failed.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[If we get to the end of the loop, that means](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [v](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [doesn’t appear in the dictionary as a value, so](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [we raise an exception.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[Here is an example of a successful reverse lookup:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[>>> h = histogram(](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [parrot](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [>>> key = reverse_lookup(h, 2)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> key](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [r](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[And an unsuccessful one:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[>>> key = reverse_lookup(h, 3)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [Traceback (most recent call last):](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [File "<stdin>", line 5, in reverse_lookup](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [LookupError](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py)

[The effect when you raise an exception is the same as when Python raises one: it prints a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [traceback and an error message.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[When you raise an exception, you can provide a detailed error message as an optional](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [argument. For example:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> raise LookupError(](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [value does not appear in the dictionary](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ' ) [Traceback (most recent call last):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [File "<stdin>", line 1, in ?](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [LookupError: value does not appear in the dictionary](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[A reverse lookup is much slower than a forward lookup; if you have to do it often, or if the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [dictionary gets big, the performance of your program will suffer.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

#### [11.5](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Dictionaries and lists](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

Lists can appear as values in a dictionary. For example, if you are given a dictionary that [maps from letters to frequencies, you might want to invert it; that is, create a dictionary](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [that maps from frequencies to letters. Since there might be several letters with the same](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [frequency, each value in the inverted dictionary should be a list of letters.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Here is a function that inverts a dictionary:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def invert_dict(d):](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [inverse = dict()](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [for key in d:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [val = d[key]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [if val not in inverse:](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [inverse[val] = [key]](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [inverse[val].append(key)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return inverse](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 11. Dictionaries](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[dict](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [dict](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [list](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[hist](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [’a’](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [inv](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [’a’](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[’p’](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [’p’](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[’r’](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ’t’

[’t’](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [’o’](https://thinkpython.com/code)

’o’ [list](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

[’r’](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[Figure 11.1: State diagram.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Each time through the loop,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [key](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [gets a key from](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [d](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [and](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [val](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [gets the corresponding value.](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [If](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [val](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is not in](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [inverse](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [, that means we haven’t seen it before, so we create a new item and](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [initialize it with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [singleton](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [(a list that contains a single element). Otherwise we have seen](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [this value before, so we append the corresponding key to the list.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Here is an example:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> hist = histogram(](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) ' parrot ' ) [>>> hist](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [{](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [: 1,](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [p](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [: 1,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [r](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [: 2,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [t](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [: 1,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [o](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [: 1}](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> inverse = invert_dict(hist)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> inverse](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [{1: [](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [p](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [o](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [], 2: [](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [r](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) []}](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Figure 11.1 is a state diagram showing](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [hist](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [inverse](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [. A dictionary is represented as a](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [box with the type](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [dict](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [above it and the key-value pairs inside. If the values are integers,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [floats or strings, I draw them inside the box, but I usually draw lists outside the box, just](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to keep the diagram simple.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Lists can be values in a dictionary, as this example shows, but they cannot be keys. Here’s](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [what happens if you try:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> t = [1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> d = dict()](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> d[t] =](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [oops](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Traceback (most recent call last):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) File "<stdin>", line 1, in ? [TypeError: list objects are unhashable](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[I mentioned earlier that a dictionary is implemented using a hashtable and that means that](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [the keys have to be](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [hashable](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

A hash [is a function that takes a value (of any kind) and returns an integer. Dictionaries](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [use these integers, called hash values, to store and look up key-value pairs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[This system works fine if the keys are immutable. But if the keys are mutable, like lists,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [bad things happen. For example, when you create a key-value pair, Python hashes the key](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [and stores it in the corresponding location. If you modify the key and then hash it again, it](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [would go to a different location. In that case you might have two entries for the same key,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [or you might not be able to find a key. Either way, the dictionary wouldn’t work correctly.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[That’s why keys have to be hashable, and why mutable types like lists aren’t. The simplest](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [way to get around this limitation is to use tuples, which we will see in the next chapter.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[11.6. Memos](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[fibonacci](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

| fibonacci | fibonacci |
| --------- | --------- |
| n         | n         |

| fibonacci | fibonacci | fibonacci | fibonacci |
| --------- | --------- | --------- | --------- |
| n         | n         | n         | n         |

| fibonacci | fibonacci |
| --------- | --------- |
| n         | n         |

Figure 11.2: Call graph.

[Since dictionaries are mutable, they can’t be used as keys, but they](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) can [be used as values.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

#### [11.6](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Memos](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[If you played with the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [fibonacci](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [function from Section 6.7, you might have noticed that](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [the bigger the argument you provide, the longer the function takes to run. Furthermore,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the run time increases quickly.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[To understand why, consider Figure 11.2, which shows the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [call graph](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [fibonacci](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [with](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [n=4](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[A call graph shows a set of function frames, with lines connecting each frame to the frames](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [of the functions it calls. At the top of the graph,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [fibonacci](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [with](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [n=4](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [calls](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [fibonacci](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [with](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [n=3](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [and](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [n=2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [. In turn,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [fibonacci](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [with](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [n=3](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [calls](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [fibonacci](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [with](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [n=2](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [n=1](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [. And so on.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Count how many times](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [fibonacci(0)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [and](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [fibonacci(1)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [are called. This is an inefficient](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [solution to the problem, and it gets worse as the argument gets bigger.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

One solution is to keep track of values that have already been computed by storing them [in a dictionary. A previously computed value that is stored for later use is called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [memo](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [Here is a “memoized” version of](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [fibonacci](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[known = {0:0, 1:1}](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[def fibonacci(n):](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [if n in known:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [return known[n]](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[res = fibonacci(n-1) + fibonacci(n-2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [known[n] = res](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [return res](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[known](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is a dictionary that keeps track of the Fibonacci numbers we already know. It starts](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [with two items: 0 maps to 0 and 1 maps to 1.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 11. Dictionaries](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Whenever](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [fibonacci](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is called, it checks](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [known](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [. If the result is already there, it can return](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [immediately.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Otherwise it has to compute the new value, add it to the dictionary, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return it.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[If you run this version of](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [fibonacci](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [and compare it with the original, you will find that it](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [is much faster.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [11.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Global variables](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[In the previous example,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [known](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [is created outside the function, so it belongs to the special](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [frame called](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [__main__](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [. Variables in](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [__main__](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [are sometimes called](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [global](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [because they](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [can be accessed from any function.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [Unlike local variables, which disappear when their](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [function ends, global variables persist from one function call to the next.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[It is common to use global variables for](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [flags](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [; that is, boolean variables that indicate (“flag”)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [whether a condition is true.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [For example, some programs use a flag named](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [verbose](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [to](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [control the level of detail in the output:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[verbose = True](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[def example1():](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [if verbose:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Running example1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[If you try to reassign a global variable, you might be surprised. The following example is](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [supposed to keep track of whether the function has been called:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[been_called = False](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def example2():](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [been_called = True](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [# WRONG](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[But if you run it you will see that the value of](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [been_called](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [doesn’t change. The problem](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is that](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [example2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [creates a new local variable named](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [been_called](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [. The local variable goes](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [away when the function ends, and has no effect on the global variable.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[To reassign a global variable inside a function you have to](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [declare](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [the global variable before](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [you use it:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

been_called = False

[def example2():](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [global been_called](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [been_called = True](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

The [global statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [tells the interpreter something like, “In this function, when I say](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [been_called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [, I mean the global variable; don’t create a local one.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Here’s an example that tries to update a global variable:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[count = 0](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[def example3():](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [count = count + 1](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [# WRONG](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[If you run it you get:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[11.8. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[UnboundLocalError: local variable](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [count](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [referenced before assignment](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Python assumes that](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [count](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [is local, and under that assumption you are reading it before](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [writing it. The solution, again, is to declare](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [count](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) global.

[def example3():](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [global count](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [count += 1](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

[If a global variable refers to a mutable value, you can modify the value without declaring](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [the variable:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[known = {0:0, 1:1}](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[def example4():](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [known[2] = 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[So you can add, remove and replace elements of a global list or dictionary, but if you want](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [to reassign the variable, you have to declare it:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[def example5():](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [global known](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [known = dict()](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[Global variables can be useful, but if you have a lot of them, and you modify them fre-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [quently, they can make programs hard to debug.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [11.8](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Debugging](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[As you work with bigger datasets it can become unwieldy to debug by printing and check-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [ing the output by hand. Here are some suggestions for debugging large datasets:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Scale down the input:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [If possible, reduce the size of the dataset. For example if the pro-](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [gram reads a text file, start with just the first 10 lines, or with the smallest example](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [you can find. You can either edit the files themselves, or (better) modify the program](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [so it reads only the first](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [lines.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[If there is an error, you can reduce](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) n to the smallest value that manifests the error, and then increase it gradually as you find and correct errors.

[Check summaries and types:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Instead of printing and checking the entire dataset, consider](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [printing summaries of the data: for example, the number of items in a dictionary or](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [the total of a list of numbers.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[A common cause of runtime errors is a value that is not the right type. For debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [this kind of error, it is often enough to print the type of a value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Write self-checks:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Sometimes you can write code to check for errors automatically.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [For](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [example, if you are computing the average of a list of numbers, you could check that](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [the result is not greater than the largest element in the list or less than the smallest.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [This is called a “sanity check” because it detects results that are “insane”.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Another kind of check compares the results of two different computations to see if](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [they are consistent. This is called a “consistency check”.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 11. Dictionaries](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Format the output:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Formatting debugging output can make it easier to spot an error. We](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [saw an example in Section 6.9. Another tool you might find useful is the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [pprint](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [mod-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [ule, which provides a](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [pprint](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [function that displays built-in types in a more human-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [readable format (](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [pprint](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [stands for “pretty print”).](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[Again, time you spend building scaffolding can reduce the time you spend debugging.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

#### 11.9 [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

[mapping:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [A relationship in which each element of one set corresponds to an element of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [another set.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[dictionary:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [A mapping from keys to their corresponding values.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[key-value pair:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [The representation of the mapping from a key to a value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[item:](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [In a dictionary, another name for a key-value pair.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

key: [An object that appears in a dictionary as the first part of a key-value pair.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[value:](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [An object that appears in a dictionary as the second part of a key-value pair. This is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [more specific than our previous use of the word “value”.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[implementation:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [A way of performing a computation.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[hashtable:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The algorithm used to implement Python dictionaries.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[hash function:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [A function used by a hashtable to compute the location for a key.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[hashable:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [A type that has a hash function. Immutable types like integers, floats and strings](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [are hashable; mutable types like lists and dictionaries are not.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[lookup:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A dictionary operation that takes a key and finds the corresponding value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[reverse lookup:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [A dictionary operation that takes a value and finds one or more keys that](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [map to it.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

raise statement: A statement that (deliberately) raises an exception.

singleton: [A list (or other sequence) with a single element.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[call graph:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [A diagram that shows every frame created during the execution of a program,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [with an arrow from each caller to each callee.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

memo: [A computed value stored to avoid unnecessary future computation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[global variable:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [A variable defined outside a function. Global variables can be accessed](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [from any function.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[global statement:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [A statement that declares a variable name global.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

flag: [A boolean variable used to indicate whether a condition is true.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[declaration:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [A statement like](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [global](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [that tells the interpreter something about a variable.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[11.10. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

It doesn’t matter what the values are. Then you can use the](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) in [operator as a fast way to](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [check whether a string is in the dictionary.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Write a program that reads a wordlist and finds all the rotate pairs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1.py) [https: //](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [thinkpython. com/ code/ rotate_ pairs. py](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Exercise 11.6.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [Here’s another Puzzler from](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Car Talk](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [(](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [http: // www. cartalk. com/ content/](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [puzzlers](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [):](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[This was sent in by a fellow named Dan O’Leary. He came upon a common one-syllable,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [five-letter word recently that has the following unique property. When you remove the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [first letter, the remaining letters form a homophone of the original word, that is a word](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [that sounds exactly the same. Replace the first letter, that is, put it back and remove](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the second letter and the result is yet another homophone of the original word. And the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [question is, what’s the word?](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Now I’m going to give you an example that doesn’t work. Let’s look at the five-letter](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [word, ‘wrack.’ W-R-A-C-K, you know like to ‘wrack with pain.’ If I remove the first](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [letter, I am left with a four-letter word, ’R-A-C-K.’ As in, ‘Holy cow, did you see the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) rack on that buck! It must have been a nine-pointer!’ It’s a perfect homophone. If you [put the ‘w’ back, and remove the ‘r,’ instead, you’re left with the word, ‘wack,’ which is](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [a real word, it’s just not a homophone of the other two words.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[But there is, however, at least one word that Dan and we know of, which will yield two](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [homophones if you remove either of the first two letters to make two, new four-letter](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [words. The question is, what’s the word?](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[You can use the dictionary from Exercise 11.1 to check whether a string is in the word list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[To](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [check](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [whether](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [two](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [words](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [are](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [homophones,](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [you](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [can](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [use](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [the](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [CMU](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [Pronouncing](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [Dictionary.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [You](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_duck_debugging) [can](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [download](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [it](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [from](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [http: // www. speech. cs. cmu. edu/ cgi-bin/](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [cmudict](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [or](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [from](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ c06d](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [you](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [can](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [also](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [down-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [load](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ pronounce. py](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [, which provides a function named](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [read_dictionary](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [that reads the pronouncing dictionary and returns a Python dictionary that](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [maps from each word to a string that describes its primary pronunciation.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)
