## [Chapter 12](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[This chapter presents one more built-in type, the tuple, and then shows how lists, dictionar-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [ies, and tuples work together. I also present a useful feature for variable-length argument](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [lists, the gather and scatter operators.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[One note: there is no consensus on how to pronounce “tuple”.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Some people say “tuh-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [ple”, which rhymes with “supple”. But in the context of programming, most people say](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [“too-ple”, which rhymes with “quadruple”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [12.1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Tuples are immutable](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[A tuple is a sequence of values.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The values can be any type, and they are indexed by](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [integers, so in that respect tuples are a lot like lists. The important difference is that tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [are immutable.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Syntactically, a tuple is a comma-separated list of values:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[>>> t =](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [b](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [c](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [d](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [e](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[Although it is not necessary, it is common to enclose tuples in parentheses:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

>>> t = ( ' a ' , ' b ' , ' c ' , ' d ' , ' e ' )

[To create a tuple with a single element, you have to include a final comma:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

| >>> t1 = | '   | a     | '   | ,   |
| -------- | --- | ----- | --- | --- |
| <class   | '   | tuple | '   | >   |

[A value in parentheses is not a tuple:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

| >>> t2 = ( | '   | a   | '   | )   |
| ---------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| <class     | '   | str | '   | >   |

[Another way to create a tuple is the built-in function](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [tuple](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [. With no argument, it creates](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [an empty tuple:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> t = tuple()](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> t](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [()](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 12. Tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[If the argument is a sequence (string, list or tuple), the result is a tuple with the elements of](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [the sequence:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> t = tuple(](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [lupins](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ( ' l ' , ' [u](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [p](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [i](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [s](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

Because tuple [is the name of a built-in function, you should avoid using it as a variable](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [name.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Most list operators also work on tuples. The bracket operator indexes an element:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[>>> t = (](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [d](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [e](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> t[0]](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) ' a '

[And the slice operator selects a range of elements.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[>>> t[1:3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [(](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [b](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [c](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [)](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[But if you try to modify one of the elements of the tuple, you get an error:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> t[0] =](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [A](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [TypeError: object doesn](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [t support item assignment](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Because tuples are immutable, you can’t modify the elements.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [But you can replace one](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [tuple with another:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[>>> t = (](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [,) + t[1:]](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [>>> t](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) ( ' A ' , ' b ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [c](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [d](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [e](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[This statement makes a new tuple and then makes](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [refer to it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[The relational operators work with tuples and other sequences; Python starts by comparing](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the first element from each sequence. If they are equal, it goes on to the next elements, and](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [so on, until it finds elements that differ. Subsequent elements are not considered (even if](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [they are really big).](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[>>> (0, 1, 2) < (0, 3, 4)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) True >>> (0, 1, 2000000) < (0, 3, 4) True

#### 12.2 [Tuple assignment](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[It is often useful to swap the values of two variables. With conventional assignments, you](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [have to use a temporary variable. For example, to swap](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py)

[>>> temp = a](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [>>> a = b](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> b = temp](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[This solution is cumbersome;](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [tuple assignment](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is more elegant:](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py)

[>>> a, b = b, a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[12.3. Tuples as return values](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The left side is a tuple of variables; the right side is a tuple of expressions.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Each value](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [is assigned to its respective variable. All the expressions on the right side are evaluated](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [before any of the assignments.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[The number of variables on the left and the number of values on the right have to be the](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [same:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[>>> a, b = 1, 2, 3](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [ValueError: too many values to unpack](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[More generally, the right side can be any kind of sequence (string, list or tuple). For exam-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [ple, to split an email address into a user name and a domain, you could write:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>> addr =](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [monty@python.org](http://www.thinkpython.com/) ['](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [>>> uname, domain = addr.split(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [@](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[The return value from](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [split](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [is a list with two elements; the first element is assigned to](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [uname](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [, the second to](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [domain](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[>>> uname](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [monty](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [>>> domain](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [python.org](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [12.3](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Tuples as return values](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Strictly speaking, a function can only return one value, but if the value is a tuple, the effect](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is the same as returning multiple values. For example, if you want to divide two integers](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and compute the quotient and remainder, it is inefficient to compute](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x//y](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and then](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x%y](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. It](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is better to compute them both at the same time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The built-in function](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [divmod](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [takes two arguments and returns a tuple of two values, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [quotient and remainder. You can store the result as a tuple:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[>>> t = divmod(7, 3)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [>>> t](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) (2, 1)

[Or use tuple assignment to store the elements separately:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> quot, rem = divmod(7, 3)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [>>> quot](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> rem](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py)

[Here is an example of a function that returns a tuple:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def min_max(t):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [return min(t), max(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[max](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [min](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [are built-in functions that find the largest and smallest elements of a sequence.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [min_max](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [computes both and returns a tuple of two values.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 12. Tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [12.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Variable-length argument tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Functions can take a variable number of arguments. A parameter name that begins with](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [*](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [gathers](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [arguments into a tuple. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [printall](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [takes any number of arguments](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [and prints them:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

def printall(*args): [print(args)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[The gather parameter can have any name you like, but](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [args](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [is conventional. Here’s how](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [the function works:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

[>>> printall(1, 2.0,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [(1, 2.0,](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[The complement of gather is](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) scatter [. If you have a sequence of values and you want to pass](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [it to a function as multiple arguments, you can use the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [*](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) operator. For example, divmod [takes exactly two arguments; it doesn’t work with a tuple:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[>>> t = (7, 3)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [>>> divmod(t)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [TypeError: divmod expected 2 arguments, got 1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[But if you scatter the tuple, it works:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[>>> divmod(*t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [(2, 1)](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py)

[Many of the built-in functions use variable-length argument tuples. For example,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [max](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [min](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [can take any number of arguments:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> max(1, 2, 3)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

But [sum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [does not.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> sum(1, 2, 3)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [TypeError: sum expected at most 2 arguments, got 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[As an exercise, write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [sum_all](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [that takes any number of arguments and](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [returns their sum.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

#### 12.5 [Lists and tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

zip [is a built-in function that takes two or more sequences and interleaves them. The name](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [of the function refers to a zipper, which interleaves two rows of teeth.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[This example zips a string and a list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

>>> s = ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [abc](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [>>> t = [0, 1, 2]](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [>>> zip(s, t)](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [<zip object at 0x7f7d0a9e7c48>](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[The result is a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [zip object](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [that knows how to iterate through the pairs. The most common](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) use of [zip](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is in a](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [for](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [loop:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[12.5. Lists and tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> for pair in zip(s, t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [...](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(pair)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [...](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [, 0)](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [, 1)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ( ' c ' , 2)

[A zip object is a kind of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [iterator](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [, which is any object that iterates through a sequence.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Iterators are similar to lists in some ways, but unlike lists, you can’t use an index to select](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [an element from an iterator.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[If you want to use list operators and methods, you can use a zip object to make a list:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>> list(zip(s, t))](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [[(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [, 0), (](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [, 1), (](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [, 2)]](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[The result is a list of tuples; in this example, each tuple contains a character from the string](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [and the corresponding element from the list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[If the sequences are not the same length, the result has the length of the shorter one.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> list(zip(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Anne](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Elk](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [))](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [[(](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [A](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [E](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [), (](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) ' n ' , ' l ' ), ( ' n ' , ' k ' )]

[You can use tuple assignment in a](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [loop to traverse a list of tuples:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[t = [(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, 0), (](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, 1), (](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, 2)]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [for letter, number in t:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [print(number, letter)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Each time through the loop, Python selects the next tuple in the list and assigns the ele-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [ments to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [letter](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [number](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [. The output of this loop is:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[0 a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [1 b](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [2 c](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[If you combine](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [zip](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [for](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [and tuple assignment, you get a useful idiom for traversing two](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [(or more) sequences at the same time. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [has_match](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [takes two sequences,](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [t1](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [t2](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [, and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [if there is an index](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [i](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [such that](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [t1[i] == t2[i]](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[def has_match(t1, t2):](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [for x, y in zip(t1, t2):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) if x == y: [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[If you need to traverse the elements of a sequence and their indices, you can use the built-in](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [function](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [enumerate](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[for index, element in enumerate(](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [abc](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [):](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [print(index, element)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[The result from](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [enumerate](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is an enumerate object, which iterates a sequence of pairs; each](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [pair contains an index (starting from 0) and an element from the given sequence. In this](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [example, the output is](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[0 a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [1 b](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [2 c](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Again.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 12. Tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [12.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Dictionaries and tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Dictionaries have a method called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [items](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [that returns a sequence of tuples, where each](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [tuple is a key-value pair.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[>>> d = {](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [:0,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [:1,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [:2}](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) >>> t = d.items() [>>> t](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [dict_items([(](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [c](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [, 2), (](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [a](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [, 0), (](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [b](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [, 1)])](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[The result is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [dict_items](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [object, which is an iterator that iterates the key-value pairs. You](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [can use it in a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [loop like this:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>> for key, value in d.items():](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [...](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [print(key, value)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [...](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [c 2](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) a 0 [b 1](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[As you should expect from a dictionary, the items are in no particular order.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Going in the other direction, you can use a list of tuples to initialize a new dictionary:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[>>> t = [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [, 0), (](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [, 2), (](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [, 1)]](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [>>> d = dict(t)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> d](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [{](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [: 0,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [: 2,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [: 1}](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Combining](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [dict](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [with](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [zip](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [yields a concise way to create a dictionary:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> d = dict(zip(](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [abc](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, range(3)))](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) >>> d { ' a ' : 0, ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [: 2,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [: 1}](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[The dictionary method](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [update](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [also takes a list of tuples and adds them, as key-value pairs,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to an existing dictionary.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[It is common to use tuples as keys in dictionaries (primarily because you can’t use lists). For](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [example, a telephone directory might map from last-name, first-name pairs to telephone](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [numbers. Assuming that we have defined](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [last](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [first](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [and](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [number](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [, we could write:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[directory[last, first] = number](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

The expression in brackets is a tuple. We could use tuple assignment to traverse this dic- tionary.

[for last, first in directory:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [print(first, last, directory[last,first])](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[This loop traverses the keys in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [directory](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, which are tuples. It assigns the elements of each](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) tuple to [last](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) and first [, then prints the name and corresponding telephone number.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

| There are two ways to represent tuples in a state diagram.    | The more detailed version |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- |
| shows the indices and elements just as they appear in a list. | For example, the tuple    |

[But in a larger diagram you might want to leave out the details. For example, a diagram of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [the telephone directory might appear as in Figure 12.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Here the tuples are shown using Python syntax as a graphical shorthand. The telephone](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [number in the diagram is the complaints line for the BBC, so please don’t call it.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[12.7. Sequences of sequences](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[tuple](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[’Cleese’](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[’John’](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[Figure 12.1: State diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[dict](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[(’Cleese’, ’John’)](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [’08700 100 222’](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[(’Chapman’, ’Graham’)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [’08700 100 222’](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py)

[(’Idle’, ’Eric’)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [’08700 100 222’](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[(’Gilliam’, ’Terry’)](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [’08700 100 222’](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[(’Jones’, ’Terry’)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [’08700 100 222’](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

(’Palin’, ’Michael’) ’08700 100 222’

[Figure 12.2: State diagram.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [12.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [Sequences of sequences](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[I have focused on lists of tuples, but almost all of the examples in this chapter also work](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [with lists of lists, tuples of tuples, and tuples of lists. To avoid enumerating the possible](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [combinations, it is sometimes easier to talk about sequences of sequences.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[In many contexts, the different kinds of sequences (strings, lists and tuples) can be used](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [interchangeably. So how should you choose one over the others?](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[To start with the obvious, strings are more limited than other sequences because the ele-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [ments have to be characters. They are also immutable. If you need the ability to change the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [characters in a string (as opposed to creating a new string), you might want to use a list of](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [characters instead.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Lists are more common than tuples, mostly because they are mutable. But there are a few](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [cases where you might prefer tuples:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

1. [1. In some contexts, like a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [statement, it is syntactically simpler to create a tuple](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [than a list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

2. [2. If you want to use a sequence as a dictionary key, you have to use an immutable type](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [like a tuple or string.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

3. [3. If you are passing a sequence as an argument to a function, using tuples reduces the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [potential for unexpected behavior due to aliasing.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Because tuples are immutable, they don’t provide methods like](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [sort](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [reverse](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [, which](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [modify existing lists. But Python provides the built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [sorted](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [, which takes any](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [sequence and returns a new list with the same elements in sorted order, and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [reversed](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [which takes a sequence and returns an iterator that traverses the list in reverse order.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 12. Tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [12.8](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Lists, dictionaries and tuples are examples of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [data structures](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [; in this chapter we are starting](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [to see compound data structures, like lists of tuples, or dictionaries that contain tuples as](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [keys and lists as values. Compound data structures are useful, but they are prone to what](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) I call shape errors [; that is, errors caused when a data structure has the wrong type, size, or](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [structure. For example, if you are expecting a list with one integer and I give you a plain](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [old integer (not in a list), it won’t work.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[To help debug these kinds of errors, I have written a module called](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [structshape](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [provides a function, also called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [structshape](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [, that takes any kind of data structure as](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [an argument and returns a string that summarizes its shape. You can download it from](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Here’s the result for a simple list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[>>> from structshape import structshape](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [>>> t = [1, 2, 3]](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [>>> structshape(t)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ' [list of 3 int](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[A fancier program might write “list of 3 int](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [s](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [”, but it was easier not to deal with plurals.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [Here’s a list of lists:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[>>> t2 = [[1,2], [3,4], [5,6]]](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> structshape(t2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [list of 3 list of 2 int](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[If the elements of the list are not the same type,](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [structshape](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [groups them, in order, by](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) type:

[>>> t3 = [1, 2, 3, 4.0,](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [, [7], [8], 9]](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [>>> structshape(t3)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ' [list of (3 int, float, 2 str, 2 list of int, int)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[Here’s a list of tuples:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> s =](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [abc](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> lt = list(zip(t, s))](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> structshape(lt)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ' [list of 3 tuple of (int, str)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) '

And here’s a dictionary with 3 items that map integers to strings.

[>>> d = dict(lt)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> structshape(d)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ' [dict of 3 int->str](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[If you are having trouble keeping track of your data structures,](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [structshape](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [can help.](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

#### [12.9](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [Glossary](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[tuple:](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [An immutable sequence of elements.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[tuple assignment:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [An assignment with a sequence on the right side and a tuple of vari-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [ables on the left. The right side is evaluated and then its elements are assigned to the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [variables on the left.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[12.10. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[gather:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [An operation that collects multiple arguments into a tuple.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[scatter:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [An operation that makes a sequence behave like multiple arguments.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[zip object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [The result of calling a built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) zip ; an object that iterates through a [sequence of tuples.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[iterator:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [An object that can iterate through a sequence, but which does not provide list](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [operators and methods.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[data structure:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [A collection of related values, often organized in lists, dictionaries, tuples,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [etc.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[shape error:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [An error caused because a value has the wrong shape; that is, the wrong type](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [or size.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Exercise](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [12.4.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Here’s](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [another](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Car](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Talk](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Puzzler](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [http: // www. cartalk. com/ content/](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [puzzlers](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[What is the longest English word, that remains a valid English word, as you remove its](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [letters one at a time?](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Now, letters can be removed from either end, or the middle, but you can’t rearrange any](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [of the letters. Every time you drop a letter, you wind up with another English word. If](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [you do that, you’re eventually going to wind up with one letter and that too is going](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [to be an English word—one that’s found in the dictionary. I want to know what’s the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [longest word and how many letters does it have?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[I’m going to give you a little modest example: Sprite. Ok? You start off with sprite,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [you take a letter off, one from the interior of the word, take the r away, and we’re left](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [with the word spite, then we take the e off the end, we’re left with spit, we take the s off,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [we’re left with pit, it, and I.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Write a program to find all words that can be reduced in this way, and then find the longest one.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[This exercise is a little more challenging than most, so here are some suggestions:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

1. [1. You might want to write a function that takes a word and computes a list of all the words that](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [can be formed by removing one letter. These are the “children” of the word.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

2. [2. Recursively, a word is reducible if any of its children are reducible. As a base case, you can](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [consider the empty string reducible.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

3. [3. The wordlist I provided,](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [, doesn’t contain single letter words. So you might want](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [to add “I”, “a”, and the empty string.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

4. [4. To improve the performance of your program, you might want to memoize the words that are](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [known to be reducible.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Solution:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ reducible. py](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

## [Chapter 13](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Case study: data structure](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [selection](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[At this point you have learned about Python’s core data structures, and you have seen](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [some of the algorithms that use them. If you would like to know more about algorithms,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [this might be a good time to read Chapter B. But you don’t have to read it before you go](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [on; you can read it whenever you are interested.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[This chapter presents a case study with exercises that let you think about choosing data](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [structures and practice using them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

#### [13.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Word frequency analysis](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[As usual, you should at least attempt the exercises before you read my solutions.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Exercise 13.1.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Write a program that reads a file, breaks each line into words, strips whitespace and](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [punctuation from the words, and converts them to lowercase.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Hint: The](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [string](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [module provides a string named](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [whitespace](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [, which contains space, tab, new-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [line, etc., and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) punctuation which contains the punctuation characters. Let’s see if we can make Python swear:

[>>> import string](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> string.punctuation](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [!"#$%&\](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\\]^_](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [`](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [{|}~](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Also, you might consider using the string methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [strip](http://gutenberg.org/) [,](http://gutenberg.org/) [replace](http://gutenberg.org/) [and](http://gutenberg.org/) [translate](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [Exercise 13.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [Go to Project Gutenberg (](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [http: // gutenberg. org](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [) and download your favorite](http://gutenberg.org/) [out-of-copyright book in plain text format.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Modify your program from the previous exercise to read the book you downloaded, skip over the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [header information at the beginning of the file, and process the rest of the words as before.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Then modify the program to count the total number of words in the book, and the number of times](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [each word is used.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Print the number of different words used in the book. Compare different books by different authors,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [written in different eras. Which author uses the most extensive vocabulary?](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Exercise 13.3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Modify the program from the previous exercise to print the 20 most frequently used](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [words in the book.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Exercise 13.4.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Modify the previous program to read a word list (see Section 9.1) and then print all](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [the words in the book that are not in the word list. How many of them are typos? How many of](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [them are common words that](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [should](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [be in the word list, and how many of them are really obscure?](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [13.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Random numbers](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[Given the same inputs, most computer programs generate the same outputs every time,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [so they are said to be](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [deterministic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [. Determinism is usually a good thing, since we expect](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [the same calculation to yield the same result. For some applications, though, we want the](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [computer to be unpredictable. Games are an obvious example, but there are more.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Making a program truly nondeterministic turns out to be difficult, but there are ways to](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [make it at least seem nondeterministic.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [One of them is to use algorithms that generate](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [pseudorandom](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [numbers. Pseudorandom numbers are not truly random because they are](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [generated by a deterministic computation, but just by looking at the numbers it is all but](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [impossible to distinguish them from random.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [random](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [module provides functions that generate pseudorandom numbers (which I](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [will simply call “random” from here on).](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

| The function       | random | returns a random float between 0.0 and 1.0 (including 0.0 but not 1.0). |
| ------------------ | ------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Each time you call | random | , you get the next number in a long series. To see a sample, run        |

[import random](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[for i in range(10):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x = random.random()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print(x)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[The function](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [randint](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [takes parameters](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) low and [high](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [and returns an integer between](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [low](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [high](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [(including both).](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[>>> random.randint(5, 10)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[>>> random.randint(5, 10)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[To choose an element from a sequence at random, you can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [choice](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[>>> t = [1, 2, 3]](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> random.choice(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[>>> random.choice(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [random](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [module also provides functions to generate random values from continuous](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [distributions including Gaussian, exponential, gamma, and a few more.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Exercise 13.5.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Write a function named](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [choose_from_hist](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [that takes a histogram as defined in](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Section 11.2 and returns a random value from the histogram, chosen with probability in proportion](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [to frequency. For example, for this histogram:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[13.3. Word histogram](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> t = [](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [>>> hist = histogram(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> hist](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [{](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [: 2,](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [: 1}](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[your function should return](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [with probability](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [2/3](https://thinkpython.com/code) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code) [with probability](https://thinkpython.com/code) 1/3 .

#### [13.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [Word histogram](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[You should attempt the previous exercises before you go on.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [You can download my](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book1.py) [solution from](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book1.py](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [You will also need](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Here is a program that reads a file and builds a histogram of the words in the file:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[import string](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[def process_file(filename):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [hist = dict()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [fp = open(filename)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [for line in fp:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [process_line(line, hist)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [return hist](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[def process_line(line, hist):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [line = line.replace(](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ' ' )

[for word in line.split():](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [word = word.strip(string.punctuation + string.whitespace)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [word = word.lower()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [hist[word] = hist.get(word, 0) + 1](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[hist = process_file(](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [emma.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ' )

[This program reads](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [emma.txt](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [, which contains the text of](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Emma](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [by Jane Austen.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

process_file loops through the lines of the file, passing them one at a time to [process_line](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. The histogram](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [hist](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [is being used as an accumulator.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[process_line](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [uses the string method](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [replace](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [to replace hyphens with spaces before using](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [split](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [to break the line into a list of strings. It traverses the list of words and uses](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [strip](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [lower](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to remove punctuation and convert to lower case. (It is a shorthand to say that](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [strings are “converted”; remember that strings are immutable, so methods like](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [strip](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [lower](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py) [return new strings.)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Finally,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [process_line](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [updates the histogram by creating a new item or incrementing an](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [existing one.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[To count the total number of words in the file, we can add up the frequencies in the his-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [togram:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[def total_words(hist):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return sum(hist.values())](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 13. Case study: data structure selection](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The number of different words is just the number of items in the dictionary:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

def different_words(hist): [return len(hist)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Here is some code to print the results:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

| print( | '   | Total number of words:     | '   | , total_words(hist))     |
| ------ | --- | -------------------------- | --- | ------------------------ |
| print( | '   | Number of different words: | '   | , different_words(hist)) |

[And the results:](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt)

[Total number of words: 161080](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [Number of different words: 7214](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

#### [13.4](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Most common words](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[To find the most common words, we can make a list of tuples, where each tuple contains a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [word and its frequency, and sort it.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The following function takes a histogram and returns a list of word-frequency tuples:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[def most_common(hist):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [t = []](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [for key, value in hist.items():](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [t.append((value, key))](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[t.sort(reverse=True)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return t](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[In each tuple, the frequency appears first, so the resulting list is sorted by frequency. Here](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is a loop that prints the ten most common words:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[t = most_common(hist)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [print(](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The most common words are:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [for freq, word in t[:10]:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [print(word, freq, sep=](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [\t](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[I use the keyword argument](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) sep to tell print to use a tab character as a “separator”, rather than a space, so the second column is lined up. Here are the results from Emma :

[The most common words are:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) to the and of i a it [her](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [was](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) she

[This code can be simplified using the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [key](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [parameter of the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [sort](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [function. If you are curi-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [ous, you can read about it at](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [.](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting)

[13.5. Optional parameters](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [13.5](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Optional parameters](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[We have seen built-in functions and methods that take optional arguments. It is possible](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [to write programmer-defined functions with optional arguments, too. For example, here is](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [a function that prints the most common words in a histogram](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[def print_most_common(hist, num=10):](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [t = most_common(hist)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [print(](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [The most common words are:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [for freq, word in t[:num]:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [print(word, freq, sep=](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [\t](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[The first parameter is required; the second is optional. The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [default value](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [of](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [num](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.format) [is 10.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[If you only provide one argument:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[print_most_common(hist)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[num](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [gets the default value. If you provide two arguments:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[print_most_common(hist, 20)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[num](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [gets the value of the argument instead. In other words, the optional argument](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [over-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [rides](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [the default value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[If a function has both required and optional parameters, all the required parameters have](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [to come first, followed by the optional ones.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [13.6](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Dictionary subtraction](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Finding the words from the book that are not in the word list from](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is a problem](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [you might recognize as set subtraction; that is, we want to find all the words from one set](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [(the words in the book) that are not in the other (the words in the list).](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[subtract](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [takes dictionaries](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [d1](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [d2](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [and returns a new dictionary that contains all the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [keys from](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [d1](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [that are not in](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) d2 [. Since we don’t really care about the values, we set them all](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [to None.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[def subtract(d1, d2):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) res = dict() [for key in d1:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [if key not in d2:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [res[key] = None](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [return res](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[To find the words in the book that are not in](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, we can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [process_file](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [to build](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [a histogram for](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [, and then subtract:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[words = process_file(](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [words.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [diff = subtract(hist, words)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[print("Words in the book that aren](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [t in the word list:")](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [for word in diff:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [print(word, end=](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [' '](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Here are some of the results from](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Emma](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py)

[Chapter 13. Case study: data structure selection](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Words in the book that aren](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [t in the word list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [rencontre jane](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [s blanche woodhouses disingenuousness](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [friend](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [s venice apartment ...](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[Some of these words are names and possessives. Others, like “rencontre”, are no longer in](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [common use. But a few are common words that should really be in the list!](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) Exercise 13.6. [Python provides a data structure called](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [set](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [that provides many common set](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [operations.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [You can read about them in Section 19.5, or read the documentation at](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [http:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [// docs. python. org/ 3/ library/ stdtypes. html# types-set](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[Write a program that uses set subtraction to find words in the book that are not in the word list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) Solution: [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ analyze_ book2. py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

#### [13.7](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Random words](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[To choose a random word from the histogram, the simplest algorithm is to build a list with](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [multiple copies of each word, according to the observed frequency, and then choose from](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) the list:

[def random_word(h):](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [t = []](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [for word, freq in h.items():](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [t.extend([word] * freq)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[return random.choice(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[The expression](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [[word] * freq](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [creates a list with](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) freq [copies of the string](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [word](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) extend [method is similar to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [append](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [except that the argument is a sequence.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[This algorithm works, but it is not very efficient; each time you choose a random word, it](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [rebuilds the list, which is as big as the original book. An obvious improvement is to build](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the list once and then make multiple selections, but the list is still big.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[An alternative is:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

1. [1. Use](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [keys](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [to get a list of the words in the book.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

2. Build a list that contains the cumulative sum of the word frequencies (see Exer- [cise 10.2). The last item in this list is the total number of words in the book,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) n .

3. [3. Choose a random number from 1 to](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [. Use a bisection search (See Exercise 10.10) to](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [find the index where the random number would be inserted in the cumulative sum.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

4. [4. Use the index to find the corresponding word in the word list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[Exercise 13.7.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [Write a program that uses this algorithm to choose a random word from the book.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) Solution: [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ analyze_ book3. py](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py)

#### 13.8 [Markov analysis](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[If you choose words from the book at random, you can get a sense of the vocabulary, but](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [you probably won’t get a sentence:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[13.8. Markov analysis](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[this the small regard harriet which knightley](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [s it most things](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[A series of random words seldom makes sense because there is no relationship between](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [successive words. For example, in a real sentence you would expect an article like “the” to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [be followed by an adjective or a noun, and probably not a verb or adverb.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[One way to measure these kinds of relationships is Markov analysis, which characterizes,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [for a given sequence of words, the probability of the words that might come next.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) For [example, the song](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Eric, the Half a Bee](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [begins:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

[Half a bee, philosophically,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [Must, ipso facto, half not be.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [But half the bee has got to be](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [Vis a vis, its entity. D’you see?](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[But can a bee be said to be](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Or not to be an entire bee](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [When half the bee is not a bee](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Due to some ancient injury?](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[In this text, the phrase “half the” is always followed by the word “bee”, but the phrase “the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [bee” might be followed by either “has” or “is”.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The result of Markov analysis is a mapping from each prefix (like “half the” and “the bee”)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [to all possible suffixes (like “has” and “is”).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)
