[Given this mapping, you can generate a random text by starting with any prefix and choos-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [ing at random from the possible suffixes. Next, you can combine the end of the prefix and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the new suffix to form the next prefix, and repeat.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[For example, if you start with the prefix “Half a”, then the next word has to be “bee”,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [because the prefix only appears once in the text.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The next prefix is “a bee”, so the next](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [suffix might be “philosophically”, “be” or “due”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[In this example the length of the prefix is always two, but you can do Markov analysis with](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [any prefix length.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Exercise 13.8.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) Markov analysis:

1. Write a program to read a text from a file and perform Markov analysis. The result should be [a dictionary that maps from prefixes to a collection of possible suffixes. The collection might](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [be a list, tuple, or dictionary; it is up to you to make an appropriate choice. You can test your](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [program with prefix length two, but you should write the program in a way that makes it easy](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [to try other lengths.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

2. [2. Add a function to the previous program to generate random text based on the Markov analysis.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [Here is an example from](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [Emma](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [with prefix length 2:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[He was very clever, be it sweetness or be angry, ashamed or only amused, at such](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [a stroke. She had never thought of Hannah till you were never meant for me?" "I](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [cannot make speeches, Emma:" he soon cut it all himself.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[For this example, I left the punctuation attached to the words. The result is almost syntacti-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [cally correct, but not quite. Semantically, it almost makes sense, but not quite.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[What happens if you increase the prefix length? Does the random text make more sense?](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 13. Case study: data structure selection](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

3. [3. Once your program is working, you might want to try a mash-up: if you combine text from](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [two or more books, the random text you generate will blend the vocabulary and phrases from](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the sources in interesting ways.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[Credit: This case study is based on an example from Kernighan and Pike,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [The Practice of Pro-](https://thinkpython.com/code) [gramming](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [, Addison-Wesley, 1999.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[You should attempt this exercise before you go on;](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [then you can download my so-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [lution from](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [You will also need](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [https://](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

#### [13.9](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [Data structures](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[Using Markov analysis to generate random text is fun, but there is also a point to this](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [exercise: data structure selection. In your solution to the previous exercises, you had to](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [choose:](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

- [• How to represent the prefixes.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

- [• How to represent the collection of possible suffixes.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

- [• How to represent the mapping from each prefix to the collection of possible suffixes.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[The last one is easy: a dictionary is the obvious choice for a mapping from keys to corre-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [sponding values.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[For the prefixes, the most obvious options are string, list of strings, or tuple of strings.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[For the suffixes, one option is a list; another is a histogram (dictionary).](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[How should you choose? The first step is to think about the operations you will need to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [implement for each data structure. For the prefixes, we need to be able to remove words](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [from the beginning and add to the end. For example, if the current prefix is “Half a”, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [the next word is “bee”, you need to be able to form the next prefix, “a bee”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Your first choice might be a list, since it is easy to add and remove elements, but we also](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [need to be able to use the prefixes as keys in a dictionary, so that rules out lists. With tuples,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) you can’t append or remove, but you can use the addition operator to form a new tuple:

[def shift(prefix, word):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [return prefix[1:] + (word,)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

shift [takes a tuple of words,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [prefix](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, and a string,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [word](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, and forms a new tuple that has](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [all the words in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [prefix](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [except the first, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [word](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [added to the end.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[For the collection of suffixes, the operations we need to perform include adding a new](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [suffix (or increasing the frequency of an existing one), and choosing a random suffix.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Adding a new suffix is equally easy for the list implementation or the histogram. Choosing](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [a random element from a list is easy; choosing from a histogram is harder to do efficiently](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [(see Exercise 13.7).](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[So far we have been talking mostly about ease of implementation, but there are other fac-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [tors to consider in choosing data structures. One is run time. Sometimes there is a theoreti-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [cal reason to expect one data structure to be faster than other; for example, I mentioned that](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[13.10. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [operator is faster for dictionaries than for lists, at least when the number of elements](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [is large.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[But often you don’t know ahead of time which implementation will be faster. One option is](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [to implement both of them and see which is better. This approach is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) benchmarking . [A practical alternative is to choose the data structure that is easiest to implement, and then](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [see if it is fast enough for the intended application. If so, there is no need to go on. If not,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [there are tools, like the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [profile](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [module, that can identify the places in a program that take](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [the most time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt)

[The other factor to consider is storage space. For example, using a histogram for the col-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [lection of suffixes might take less space because you only have to store each word once, no](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [matter how many times it appears in the text. In some cases, saving space can also make](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [your program run faster, and in the extreme, your program might not run at all if you run](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [out of memory. But for many applications, space is a secondary consideration after run](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[One final thought: in this discussion, I have implied that we should use one data structure](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [for both analysis and generation. But since these are separate phases, it would also be pos-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [sible to use one structure for analysis and then convert to another structure for generation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [This would be a net win if the time saved during generation exceeded the time spent in](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [conversion.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [13.10](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[When you are debugging a program, and especially if you are working on a hard bug,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [there are five things to try:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Reading:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Examine your code, read it back to yourself, and check that it says what you](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [meant to say.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Running:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Experiment by making changes and running different versions.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Often if you](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [display the right thing at the right place in the program, the problem becomes obvi-](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [ous, but sometimes you have to build scaffolding.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[Ruminating:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Take some time to think! What kind of error is it: syntax, runtime, or seman-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [tic? What information can you get from the error messages, or from the output of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [program? What kind of error could cause the problem you’re seeing? What did you](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [change last, before the problem appeared?](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Rubberducking:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [If you explain the problem to someone else, you sometimes find the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [answer before you finish asking the question.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [Often you don’t need the other](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [person; you could just talk to a rubber duck.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [And that’s the origin of the well-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [known strategy called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [rubber duck debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [I am not making this up; see](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_duck_debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Retreating:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [At some point, the best thing to do is back off, undoing recent changes, until](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [you get back to a program that works and that you understand. Then you can start](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [rebuilding.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 13. Case study: data structure selection](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Beginning programmers sometimes get stuck on one of these activities and forget the oth-](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [ers. Each activity comes with its own failure mode.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[For example, reading your code might help if the problem is a typographical error, but](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [not if the problem is a conceptual misunderstanding. If you don’t understand what your](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [program does, you can read it 100 times and never see the error, because the error is in](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) your head.

[Running experiments can help, especially if you run small, simple tests.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [But if you run](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [experiments without thinking or reading your code, you might fall into a pattern I call](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [“random walk programming”, which is the process of making random changes until the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [program does the right thing. Needless to say, random walk programming can take a long](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py)

[You have to take time to think. Debugging is like an experimental science. You should have](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [at least one hypothesis about what the problem is. If there are two or more possibilities, try](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [to think of a test that would eliminate one of them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[But even the best debugging techniques will fail if there are too many errors, or if the code](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [you are trying to fix is too big and complicated. Sometimes the best option is to retreat,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [simplifying the program until you get to something that works and that you understand.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[Beginning programmers are often reluctant to retreat because they can’t stand to delete a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [line of code (even if it’s wrong). If it makes you feel better, copy your program into another](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [file before you start stripping it down. Then you can copy the pieces back one at a time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Finding a hard bug requires reading, running, ruminating, and sometimes retreating. If](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [you get stuck on one of these activities, try the others.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [13.11](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[deterministic:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Pertaining to a program that does the same thing each time it runs, given](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [the same inputs.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[pseudorandom:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Pertaining to a sequence of numbers that appears to be random, but is](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [generated by a deterministic program.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

default value: The value given to an optional parameter if no argument is provided.

override: [To replace a default value with an argument.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[benchmarking:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [The process of choosing between data structures by implementing alter-](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [natives and testing them on a sample of the possible inputs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[rubber duck debugging:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [Debugging by explaining your problem to an inanimate object](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [such as a rubber duck. Articulating the problem can help you solve it, even if the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [rubber duck doesn’t know Python.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

#### 13.12 [Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Exercise 13.9.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [The “rank” of a word is its position in a list of words sorted by frequency: the most](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [common word has rank 1, the second most common has rank 2, etc.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[13.12. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Zipf’s law describes a relationship between the ranks and frequencies of words in natural languages](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [http: // en. wikipedia. org/ wiki/ Zipf’s_ law](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [). Specifically, it predicts that the frequency,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [f , of the word with rank r is:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[−](https://thinkpython.com/code) [s](https://thinkpython.com/code)

[f](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [cr](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[where s and c are parameters that depend on the language and the text. If you take the logarithm of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [both sides of this equation, you get:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[log](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [f](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [=](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [log](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [−](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [s](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [log](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [r](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[So if you plot log f versus log r, you should get a straight line with slope](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [s and intercept log c.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Write a program that reads a text from a file, counts word frequencies, and prints one line for each](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [word, in descending order of frequency, with log f and log r. Use the graphing program of your](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [choice to plot the results and check whether they form a straight line. Can you estimate the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [s?](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[Solution:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ zipf. py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [. To run my solution, you need the plot-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [ting module](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [matplotlib](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [. If you installed Anaconda, you already have](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [matplotlib](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [; otherwise you](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [might have to install it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py)

[Chapter 13. Case study: data structure selection](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

## [Chapter 14](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Files](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[This chapter introduces the idea of “persistent” programs that keep data in permanent stor-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [age, and shows how to use different kinds of permanent storage, like files and databases.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

#### [14.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Persistence](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Most of the programs we have seen so far are transient in the sense that they run for a short](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [time and produce some output, but when they end, their data disappears. If you run the](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [program again, it starts with a clean slate.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Other programs are](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [persistent](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [: they run for a long time (or all the time); they keep at least](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [some of their data in permanent storage (a hard drive, for example); and if they shut down](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and restart, they pick up where they left off.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Examples of persistent programs are operating systems, which run pretty much whenever](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [a computer is on, and web servers, which run all the time, waiting for requests to come in](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [on the network.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[One of the simplest ways for programs to maintain their data is by reading and writing](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) text files. We have already seen programs that read text files; in this chapter we will see [programs that write them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[An alternative is to store the state of the program in a database. In this chapter I will present](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [a simple database and a module,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [pickle](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, that makes it easy to store program data.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

#### [14.2](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Reading and writing](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[A text file is a sequence of characters stored on a permanent medium like a hard drive,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [flash memory, or CD-ROM. We saw how to open and read a file in Section 9.1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[To write a file, you have to open it with mode](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [w](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [as a second parameter:](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[>>> fout = open(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [output.txt](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [w](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py)

[Chapter 14. Files](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[If the file already exists, opening it in write mode clears out the old data and starts fresh,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [so be careful! If the file doesn’t exist, a new one is created.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[open](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [returns a file object that provides methods for working with the file.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [The](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [write](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [method puts data into the file.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

>>> line1 = "This here ' [s the wattle,\n"](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [>>> fout.write(line1)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[The return value is the number of characters that were written. The file object keeps track](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [of where it is, so if you call](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [write](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [again, it adds the new data to the end of the file.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>> line2 = "the emblem of our land.\n"](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> fout.write(line2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[When you are done writing, you should close the file.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[>>> fout.close()](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[If you don’t close the file, it gets closed for you when the program ends.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [14.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [Format operator](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The argument of](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [write](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [has to be a string, so if we want to put other values in a file, we](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [have to convert them to strings. The easiest way to do that is with](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [str](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> x = 52](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> fout.write(str(x))](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[An alternative is to use the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [format operator](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) , % [. When applied to integers,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [%](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is the modulus](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [operator. But when the first operand is a string,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [%](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [is the format operator.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The first operand is the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [format string](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [, which contains one or more](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [format sequences](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [which specify how the second operand is formatted. The result is a string.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[For example, the format sequence](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) %d ' [means that the second operand should be format-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [ted as a decimal integer:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[>>> camels = 42](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) >>> ' %d ' % camels ' '

[The result is the string](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, which is not to be confused with the integer value](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[A format sequence can appear anywhere in the string, so you can embed a value in a](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) sentence:

>>> ' [I have spotted %d camels.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [% camels](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ' [I have spotted 42 camels.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[If there is more than one format sequence in the string, the second argument has to be a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [tuple. Each format sequence is matched with an element of the tuple, in order.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[The following example uses](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [%d](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [to format an integer,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [%g](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [to format a floating-point num-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ber, and ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [%s](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [to format a string:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

>>> ' [In %d years I have spotted %g %s.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [% (3, 0.1,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [camels](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) ' [In 3 years I have spotted 0.1 camels.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[14.4. Filenames and paths](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The number of elements in the tuple has to match the number of format sequences in the](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [string. Also, the types of the elements have to match the format sequences:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>>](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [%d %d %d](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [% (1, 2)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [TypeError: not enough arguments for format string](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [>>>](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [%d](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [%](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [dollars](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [TypeError: %d format: a number is required, not str](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[In the first example, there aren’t enough elements; in the second, the element is the wrong](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [type.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[For more information on the format operator, see](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [https://docs.python.org/3/library/](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [A more powerful alternative is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [the string format method, which you can read about at](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [https://docs.python.org/3/](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [library/stdtypes.html#str.format](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

#### [14.4](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [Filenames and paths](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Files are organized into](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [directories](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [(also called “folders”). Every running program has a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [“current directory”, which is the default directory for most operations. For example, when](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [you open a file for reading, Python looks for it in the current directory.](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [os](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [module provides functions for working with files and directories (“os” stands for](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [“operating system”).](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [os.getcwd](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [returns the name of the current directory:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[>>> import os](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [>>> cwd = os.getcwd()](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [>>> cwd](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [/home/dinsdale](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[cwd](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [stands for “current working directory”. The result in this example is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [/home/dinsdale](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [which is the home directory of a user named](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [dinsdale](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [.](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[A string like](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [/home/dinsdale](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [that identifies a file or directory is called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [path](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[A simple filename, like](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) memo.txt [is also considered a path, but it is a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [relative path](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [because](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [it relates to the current directory. If the current directory is](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [/home/dinsdale](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [, the filename](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [memo.txt](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [would refer to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) /home/dinsdale/memo.txt .

A path that begins with / does not depend on the current directory; it is called an absolute path [. To find the absolute path to a file, you can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [os.path.abspath](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[>>> os.path.abspath(](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [memo.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [/home/dinsdale/memo.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[os.path](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [provides other functions for working with filenames and paths.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [os.path.exists](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [checks whether a file or directory exists:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[>>> os.path.exists(](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [memo.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[If it exists,](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [os.path.isdir](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [checks whether it’s a directory:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> os.path.isdir(](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [memo.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> os.path.isdir(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [/home/dinsdale](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [True](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 14. Files](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Similarly,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [os.path.isfile](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [checks whether it’s a file.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[os.listdir](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [returns a list of the files (and other directories) in the given directory:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[>>> os.listdir(cwd)](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [[](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [music](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [photos](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [memo.txt](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[To demonstrate these functions, the following example “walks” through a directory, prints](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [the names of all the files, and calls itself recursively on all the directories.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[def walk(dirname):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [for name in os.listdir(dirname):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [path = os.path.join(dirname, name)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[if os.path.isfile(path):](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [print(path)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [else:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [walk(path)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[os.path.join](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [takes a directory and a file name and joins them into a complete path.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

The os [module provides a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [walk](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [that is similar to this one but more ver-](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) satile. [As an exercise, read the documentation and use it to print the names of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [files in a given directory and its subdirectories.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [You can download my solution from](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [14.5](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [Catching exceptions](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[A lot of things can go wrong when you try to read and write files. If you try to open a file](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [that doesn’t exist, you get an](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [FileNotFoundError](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [:](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[>>> fin = open(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [bad_file](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [bad_file](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[If you don’t have permission to access a file:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[>>> fout = open(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [/etc/passwd](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ' , ' w ' ) PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: ' /etc/passwd '

[And if you try to open a directory for reading, you get](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> fin = open(](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [/home](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [IsADirectoryError: [Errno 21] Is a directory:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [/home](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[To avoid these errors, you could use functions like](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [os.path.exists](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [os.path.isfile](http://gutenberg.org/) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [but it would take a lot of time and code to check all the possibilities (if “](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [Errno 21](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [” is any](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [indication, there are at least 21 things that can go wrong).](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[It is better to go ahead and try—and deal with problems if they happen—which is exactly](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [what the](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [try](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [statement does. The syntax is similar to an](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [if...else](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [statement:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

try: [fin = open(](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [bad_file](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) except: [print(](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Something went wrong.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[14.6. Databases](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Python starts by executing the](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [try](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [clause. If all goes well, it skips the](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [except](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [clause and](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [proceeds. If an exception occurs, it jumps out of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [try](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [clause and runs the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [except](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2.py) [clause.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sorting_algorithm)

[Handling an exception with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [try](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [statement is called](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) catching [an exception. In this exam-](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [ple, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [except](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [clause prints an error message that is not very helpful. In general, catching](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [an exception gives you a chance to fix the problem, or try again, or at least end the program](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) gracefully.

#### [14.6](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [Databases](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[A](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [database](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [is a file that is organized for storing data. Many databases are organized like a](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [dictionary in the sense that they map from keys to values. The biggest difference between](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [a database and a dictionary is that the database is on disk (or other permanent storage), so](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [it persists after the program ends.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[The module](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [dbm](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [provides an interface for creating and updating database files.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [As an](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [example, I’ll create a database that contains captions for image files.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[Opening a database is similar to opening other files:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[>>> import dbm](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> db = dbm.open(](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [captions](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[The mode](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [c](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [means that the database should be created if it doesn’t already exist. The](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [result is a database object that can be used (for most operations) like a dictionary.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[When you create a new item,](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [dbm](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [updates the database file.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> db[](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [cleese.png](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [] =](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) ['](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [Photo of John Cleese.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[When you access one of the items,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [dbm](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [reads the file:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py)

[>>> db[](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [cleese.png](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Photo of John Cleese.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[The result is a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) bytes object , which is why it begins with b . A bytes object is similar to a string in many ways. When you get farther into Python, the difference becomes important, [but for now we can ignore it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[If you make another assignment to an existing key,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [dbm](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [replaces the old value:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[>>> db[](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [cleese.png](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [] =](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Photo of John Cleese doing a silly walk.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [>>> db[](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [cleese.png](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py) [Photo of John Cleese doing a silly walk.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Some dictionary methods, like](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [keys](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [items](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [, don’t work with database objects.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [But](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [iteration with a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [loop works:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[for key in db.keys():](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [print(key, db[key])](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[As with other files, you should close the database when you are done:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[>>> db.close()](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 14. Files](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [14.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Pickling](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[A limitation of](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [dbm](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is that the keys and values have to be strings or bytes. If you try to use](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [any other type, you get an error.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

The [pickle](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [module can help. It translates almost any type of object into a string suitable](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [for storage in a database, and then translates strings back into objects.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[pickle.dumps](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [takes an object as a parameter and returns a string representation (](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [dumps](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [is](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [short for “dump string”):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[>>> import pickle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> t = [1, 2, 3]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [>>> pickle.dumps(t)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [b](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [\x80\x03]q\x00(K\x01K\x02K\x03e.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[The format isn’t obvious to human readers; it is meant to be easy for](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) pickle to interpret. [pickle.loads](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [(“load string”) reconstitutes the object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[>>> t1 = [1, 2, 3]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [>>> s = pickle.dumps(t1)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [>>> t2 = pickle.loads(s)](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [>>> t2](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [[1, 2, 3]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Although the new object has the same value as the old, it is not (in general) the same object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[>>> t1 == t2](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1_soln.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [>>> t1 is t2](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) False

[In other words, pickling and then unpickling has the same effect as copying the object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[You can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [pickle](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to store non-strings in a database. In fact, this combination is so com-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [mon that it has been encapsulated in a module called](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [shelve](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py)

#### [14.8](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Pipes](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

Most operating systems provide a command-line interface, also known as a shell . Shells [usually provide commands to navigate the file system and launch applications. For exam-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [ple, in Unix you can change directories with](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [cd](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, display the contents of a directory with](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [ls](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [and launch a web browser by typing (for example)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [firefox](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Any program that you can launch from the shell can also be launched from Python using](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) a [pipe object](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [, which represents a running program.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[For example, the Unix command](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [ls -l](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [normally displays the contents of the current di-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [rectory in long format. You can launch](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [ls](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [with](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [os.popen](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[>>> cmd =](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [ls -l](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_duck_debugging) [>>> fp = os.popen(cmd)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

popen [is deprecated now, which means we are supposed to stop using it and start using the](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [subprocess](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [module. But for simple cases, I find](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [subprocess](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [more complicated than necessary. So I am going to keep using](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) popen [until they take it away.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[14.9. Writing modules](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The argument is a string that contains a shell command. The return value is an object that](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [behaves like an open file. You can read the output from the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [ls](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [process one line at a time](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [with](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [readline](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [or get the whole thing at once with](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [read](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[>>> res = fp.read()](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[When you are done, you close the pipe like a file:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[>>> stat = fp.close()](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [>>> print(stat)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[The return value is the final status of the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [ls](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [process;](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [None](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [means that it ended normally](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [(with no errors).](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[For example, most Unix systems provide a command called](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [md5sum](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [that reads the contents](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [of a file and computes a “checksum”. You can read about MD5 at](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [http://en.wikipedia.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [org/wiki/Md5](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [. This command provides an efficient way to check whether two files have](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [the same contents. The probability that different contents yield the same checksum is very](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [small (that is, unlikely to happen before the universe collapses).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[You can use a pipe to run](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [md5sum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [from Python and get the result:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> filename =](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [book.tex](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [>>> cmd =](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [md5sum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [+ filename](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> fp = os.popen(cmd)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [>>> res = fp.read()](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [>>> stat = fp.close()](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> print(res)](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [1e0033f0ed0656636de0d75144ba32e0](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) book.tex [>>> print(stat)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [None](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

#### [14.9](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Writing modules](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Any file that contains Python code can be imported as a module. For example, suppose](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [you have a file named](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [wc.py](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [with the following code:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

def linecount(filename): [count = 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [for line in open(filename):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [count += 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [return count](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[print(linecount(](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [wc.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [))](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[If you run this program, it reads itself and prints the number of lines in the file, which is 7.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [You can also import it like this:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[>>> import wc](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[Now you have a module object](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [wc](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[>>> wc](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [<module](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [wc](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [from](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [wc.py](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [>](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 14. Files](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The module object provides](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [linecount](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

>>> wc.linecount( ' wc.py ' )

[So that’s how you write modules in Python.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[The only problem with this example is that when you import the module it runs the test](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [code at the bottom. Normally when you import a module, it defines new functions but it](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [doesn’t run them.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Programs that will be imported as modules often use the following idiom:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

| if __name__ ==   | '   | __main__ | '   | :   |
| ---------------- | --- | -------- | --- | --- |
| print(linecount( | '   | wc.py    | '   | ))  |

[__name__](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is a built-in variable that is set when the program starts. If the program is running](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [as a script,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [__name__](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [has the value](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ' __main__ ' ; in that case, the test code runs. Otherwise, [if the module is being imported, the test code is skipped.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[As an exercise, type this example into a file named](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [wc.py](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and run it as a script. Then run](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the Python interpreter and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [import wc](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [. What is the value of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [__name__](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [when the module is](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [being imported?](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[Warning: If you import a module that has already been imported, Python does nothing. It](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [does not re-read the file, even if it has changed.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[If you want to reload a module, you can use the built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [reload](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, but it can be](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [tricky, so the safest thing to do is restart the interpreter and then import the module again.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### 14.10 [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[When you are reading and writing files, you might run into problems with whitespace.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [These errors can be hard to debug because spaces, tabs and newlines are normally invisible:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

>>> s = ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [1 2\t 3\n 4](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [>>> print(s)](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [1 2](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

The built-in function repr can help. It takes any object as an argument and returns a string [representation of the object. For strings, it represents whitespace characters with backslash](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [sequences:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[>>> print(repr(s))](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ' [1 2\t 3\n 4](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[This can be helpful for debugging.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[One other problem you might run into is that different systems use different characters to](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [indicate the end of a line. Some systems use a newline, represented](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [\n](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [. Others use a return](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [character, represented](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [\r](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [Some use both.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [If you move files between different systems,](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [these inconsistencies can cause problems.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[For most systems, there are applications to convert from one format to another. You can](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [find them (and read more about this issue) at](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newline](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Or, of course, you could write one yourself.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[14.11. Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [14.11](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[persistent:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Pertaining to a program that runs indefinitely and keeps at least some of its](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [data in permanent storage.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[format operator:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [An operator,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [%](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [, that takes a format string and a tuple and generates a](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [string that includes the elements of the tuple formatted as specified by the format](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [string.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[format string:](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [A string, used with the format operator, that contains format sequences.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[format sequence:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [A sequence of characters in a format string, like](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [%d](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [, that specifies how a](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [value should be formatted.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[text file:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [A sequence of characters stored in permanent storage like a hard drive.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[directory:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [A named collection of files, also called a folder.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[path:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [A string that identifies a file.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[relative path:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [A path that starts from the current directory.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[absolute path:](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [A path that starts from the topmost directory in the file system.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[catch:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [To prevent an exception from terminating a program using the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [try](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [except](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [state-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [ments.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[database:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [A file whose contents are organized like a dictionary with keys that correspond](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [to values.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[bytes object:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [An object similar to a string.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[shell:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [A program that allows users to type commands and then executes them by starting](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [other programs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[pipe object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [An object that represents a running program, allowing a Python program to](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [run commands and read the results.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

#### [14.12](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Exercises](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

Exercise 14.1. Write a function called sed that takes as arguments a pattern string, a replacement [string, and two filenames; it should read the first file and write the contents into the second file](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [(creating it if necessary). If the pattern string appears anywhere in the file, it should be replaced](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [with the replacement string.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[If an error occurs while opening, reading, writing or closing files, your program should catch the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [exception, print an error message, and exit. Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ sed.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [Exercise 14.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [If you download my solution to Exercise 12.2 from](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [code/ anagram_ sets. py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [, you’ll see that it creates a dictionary that maps from a sorted string of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [letters to the list of words that can be spelled with those letters. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [opst](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [maps to the](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [list](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [[](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [opts](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ' , ' [post](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [pots](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [spot](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [stop](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [tops](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Write a module that imports](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [anagram_sets](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [and provides two new functions:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [store_anagrams](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [should store the anagram dictionary in a “shelf”;](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [read_anagrams](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [should look up a word and return](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [a list of its anagrams. Solution:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ anagram_ db. py](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Chapter 14. Files](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Exercise 14.3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [In a large collection of MP3 files, there may be more than one copy of the same song,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [stored in different directories or with different file names. The goal of this exercise is to search for](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [duplicates.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

1. [1. Write a program that searches a directory and all of its subdirectories, recursively, and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [a list of complete paths for all files with a given suffix (like](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [.mp3](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [). Hint:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [os.path](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [provides](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [several useful functions for manipulating file and path names.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

2. [2. To recognize duplicates, you can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [md5sum](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [to compute a “checksum” for each files. If two](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [files have the same checksum, they probably have the same contents.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

3. [3. To double-check, you can use the Unix command](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [diff](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

Solution: [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ find_ duplicates. py](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

## [Chapter 15](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Classes and objects](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[At this point you know how to use functions to organize code and built-in types to organize](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [data. The next step is to learn “object-oriented programming”, which uses programmer-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [defined types to organize both code and data. Object-oriented programming is a big topic;](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [it will take a few chapters to get there.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Code examples from this chapter are available from](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Point1.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [; solutions to the exercises are available from](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Point1_soln.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

#### [15.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Programmer-defined types](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[We have used many of Python’s built-in types; now we are going to define a new type. As](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [an example, we will create a type called](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [that represents a point in two-dimensional](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [space.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[In mathematical notation, points are often written in parentheses with a comma separating](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [the coordinates. For example,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ( 0, 0 ) represents the origin, and ( x , y ) represents the point x units to the right and y units up from the origin.

[There are several ways we might represent points in Python:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

- [• We could store the coordinates separately in two variables,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) x and y .

- [• We could store the coordinates as elements in a list or tuple.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• We could create a new type to represent points as objects.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Creating a new type is more complicated than the other options, but it has advantages that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [will be apparent soon.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[A programmer-defined type is also called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [class](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [. A class definition looks like this:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[class Point:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ["""Represents a point in 2-D space."""](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 15. Classes and objects](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [blank](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [3.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[y](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [4.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[Figure 15.1: Object diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[The header indicates that the new class is called](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [. The body is a docstring that ex-](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [plains what the class is for. You can define variables and methods inside a class definition,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [but we will get back to that later.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

[Defining a class named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [Point](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [creates a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [class object](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>> Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [<class](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [__main__.Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ' >

Because [Point](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [is defined at the top level, its “full name” is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [__main__.Point](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

[The class object is like a factory for creating objects. To create a Point, you call](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) Point [as if it](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [were a function.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> blank = Point()](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [>>> blank](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [<__main__.Point object at 0xb7e9d3ac>](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)
