[The return value is a reference to a Point object, which we assign to](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [blank](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1.py)

[Creating a new object is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [instantiation](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, and the object is an](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [instance](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [of the class.](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py)

[When you print an instance, Python tells you what class it belongs to and where it is stored](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [in memory (the prefix](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [0x](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [means that the following number is in hexadecimal).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Every object is an instance of some class, so “object” and “instance” are interchangeable.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [But in this chapter I use “instance” to indicate that I am talking about a programmer-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [defined type.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [15.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Attributes](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[You can assign values to an instance using dot notation:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

>>> blank.x = 3.0 [>>> blank.y = 4.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[This syntax is similar to the syntax for selecting a variable from a module, such as](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [math.pi](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) or [string.whitespace](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. In this case, though, we are assigning values to named elements of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [an object. These elements are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [attributes](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[As a noun, “AT-trib-ute” is pronounced with emphasis on the first syllable, as opposed to](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [“a-TRIB-ute”, which is a verb.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Figure 15.1 is a state diagram that shows the result of these assignments. A state diagram](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [that shows an object and its attributes is called an](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [object diagram](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[The variable](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [blank](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [refers to a Point object, which contains two attributes. Each attribute](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [refers to a floating-point number.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[You can read the value of an attribute using the same syntax:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[15.3. Rectangles](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> blank.y](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [4.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> x = blank.x](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> x](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [3.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

The expression [blank.x](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [means, “Go to the object](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [blank](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [refers to and get the value of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) x .” In [the example, we assign that value to a variable named](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) x [. There is no conflict between the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [variable](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [x](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [and the attribute](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [x](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[You can use dot notation as part of any expression. For example:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[>>>](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [(%g, %g)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [% (blank.x, blank.y)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [(3.0, 4.0)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [>>> distance = math.sqrt(blank.x**2 + blank.y**2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>>> distance](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [5.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[You can pass an instance as an argument in the usual way. For example:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def print_point(p):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [(%g, %g)](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [% (p.x, p.y))](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[print_point](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [takes a point as an argument and displays it in mathematical notation. To](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [invoke it, you can pass](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [blank](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [as an argument:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[>>> print_point(blank)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [(3.0, 4.0)](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[Inside the function,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [p](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is an alias for](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) blank , so if the function modifies p , blank [changes.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[As an exercise, write a function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [distance_between_points](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [that takes two Points as](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [arguments and returns the distance between them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [15.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Rectangles](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Sometimes it is obvious what the attributes of an object should be, but other times you have](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [to make decisions. For example, imagine you are designing a class to represent rectangles.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) What attributes would you use to specify the location and size of a rectangle? You can ig- [nore angle; to keep things simple, assume that the rectangle is either vertical or horizontal.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[There are at least two possibilities:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

- [• You could specify one corner of the rectangle (or the center), the width, and the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [height.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

- [• You could specify two opposing corners.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[At this point it is hard to say whether either is better than the other, so we’ll implement the](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [first one, just as an example.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Here is the class definition:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 15. Classes and objects](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Rectangle](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[box](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [width](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [100.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Point](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [height](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [200.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [x](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) 0.0 [corner](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [0.0](https://thinkpython.com/code)

[Figure 15.2: Object diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[class Rectangle:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) ["""Represents a rectangle.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[attributes: width, height, corner.](http://www.thinkpython.com/) ["""](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[The docstring lists the attributes:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [width](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [height](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [are numbers;](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [corner](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [is a Point object](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [that specifies the lower-left corner.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[To represent a rectangle, you have to instantiate a Rectangle object and assign values to the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [attributes:](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[box = Rectangle()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [box.width = 100.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [box.height = 200.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [box.corner = Point()](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [box.corner.x = 0.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [box.corner.y = 0.0](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The expression](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [box.corner.x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [means, “Go to the object](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) box [refers to and select the attribute](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) named [corner](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [; then go to that object and select the attribute named](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Figure 15.2 shows the state of this object. An object that is an attribute of another object is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [embedded](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

#### 15.4 [Instances as return values](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[Functions can return instances. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [find_center](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [takes a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Rectangle](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [as an argu-](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [ment and returns a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [that contains the coordinates of the center of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Rectangle](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[def find_center(rect):](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [p = Point()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [p.x = rect.corner.x + rect.width/2](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [p.y = rect.corner.y + rect.height/2](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [return p](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[Here is an example that passes](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [box](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [as an argument and assigns the resulting Point to](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [center](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py)

[>>> center = find_center(box)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> print_point(center)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [(50, 100)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[15.5. Objects are mutable](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [15.5](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Objects are mutable](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[You can change the state of an object by making an assignment to one of its attributes. For](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [example, to change the size of a rectangle without changing its position, you can modify](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [the values of](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [width](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [height](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[box.width = box.width + 50](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [box.height = box.height + 100](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[You can also write functions that modify objects.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [grow_rectangle](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [takes a](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [Rectangle object and two numbers,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [dwidth](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [dheight](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [, and adds the numbers to the](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [width and height of the rectangle:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[def grow_rectangle(rect, dwidth, dheight):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [rect.width += dwidth](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [rect.height += dheight](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Here is an example that demonstrates the effect:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[>>> box.width, box.height](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [(150.0, 300.0)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [>>> grow_rectangle(box, 50, 100)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [>>> box.width, box.height](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [(200.0, 400.0)](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[Inside the function,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [rect](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [is an alias for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [box](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, so when the function modifies](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [rect](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [box](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [changes.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[As an exercise, write a function named](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [move_rectangle](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [that takes a Rectangle and two](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [numbers named](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [dx](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [dy](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [. It should change the location of the rectangle by adding](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [dx](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [x](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [coordinate of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [corner](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and adding](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) dy to the y [coordinate of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [corner](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

#### [15.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Copying](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Aliasing can make a program difficult to read because changes in one place might have](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [unexpected effects in another place. It is hard to keep track of all the variables that might](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [refer to a given object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Copying an object is often an alternative to aliasing. The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) copy module contains a function called copy that can duplicate any object:

[>>> p1 = Point()](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> p1.x = 3.0](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [>>> p1.y = 4.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[>>> import copy](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [>>> p2 = copy.copy(p1)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[p1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [p2](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [contain the same data, but they are not the same Point.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[>>> print_point(p1)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [(3, 4)](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> print_point(p2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [(3, 4)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> p1 is p2](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [False](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 15. Classes and objects](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[box](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [width](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [100.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [100.0](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [width](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [box2](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[height](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf's_law) [200.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [0.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [200.0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [height](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[corner](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [y](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [0.0](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) corner

[Figure 15.3: Object diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[>>> p1 == p2](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt)

The is [operator indicates that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [p1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [p2](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [are not the same object, which is what we ex-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [pected.](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [But you might have expected](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [==](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [to yield](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [True](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [because these points contain the](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [same data. In that case, you will be disappointed to learn that for instances, the default](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [behavior of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [==](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [operator is the same as the](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) is [operator; it checks object identity, not](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [object equivalence. That’s because for programmer-defined types, Python doesn’t know](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [what should be considered equivalent. At least, not yet.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[If you use](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [copy.copy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [to duplicate a Rectangle, you will find that it copies the Rectangle](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [object but not the embedded Point.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> box2 = copy.copy(box)](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [>>> box2 is box](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [False](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [>>> box2.corner is box.corner](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py)

[Figure 15.3 shows what the object diagram looks like.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [This operation is called a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [shallow](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [copy](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [because it copies the object and any references it contains, but not the embedded](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) objects.

For [most](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [applications,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [this](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [is](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [not](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [what](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [you](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [want.](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [In](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [this](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [invoking](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [grow_rectangle](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [on one of the Rectangles would not affect the other,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [but invoking](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk2.py) [move_rectangle](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [on either would affect both! This behavior is confusing and error-prone.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

| object but also the objects it refers to, and the objects | they      | refer to, and so on. You will not |
| --------------------------------------------------------- | --------- | --------------------------------- |
| be surprised to learn that this operation is called a     | deep copy | .                                 |

>>> box3 = copy.deepcopy(box) >>> box3 is box False [>>> box3.corner is box.corner](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) False

box3 and [box](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [are completely separate objects.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[As an exercise, write a version of](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [move_rectangle](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [that creates and returns a new Rectangle](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [instead of modifying the old one.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

#### 15.7 [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[When you start working with objects, you are likely to encounter some new exceptions. If](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [you try to access an attribute that doesn’t exist, you get an](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [AttributeError](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [:](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting)

[15.8. Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> p = Point()](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [>>> p.x = 3](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> p.y = 4](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> p.z](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [AttributeError: Point instance has no attribute](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code) [z](https://thinkpython.com/code) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code) [If you are not sure what type an object is, you can ask:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [>>> type(p)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [<class](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [__main__.Point](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [>](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [You can also use](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [isinstance](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [to check whether an object is an instance of a class:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [>>> isinstance(p, Point)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [True](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [If you are not sure whether an object has a particular attribute, you can use the built-in](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [function](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [hasattr](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) : [>>> hasattr(p,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [>>> hasattr(p,](https://thinkpython.com/code/zipf.py) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [z](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [False](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [The first argument can be any object; the second argument is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [string](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [that contains the name](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [of the attribute.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[You can also use a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [try](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [statement to see if the object has the attributes you need:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [try:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [x = p.x](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [except AttributeError:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [x = 0](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [This approach can make it easier to write functions that work with different types; more](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [on that topic is coming up in Section 17.9.](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py)

#### [15.8](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Glossary](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[class:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [A programmer-defined type. A class definition creates a new class object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[class object:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [An object that contains information about a programmer-defined type. The](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [class object can be used to create instances of the type.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[instance:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [An object that belongs to a class.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[instantiate:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [To create a new object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[attribute:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [One of the named values associated with an object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[embedded object:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [An object that is stored as an attribute of another object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[shallow copy:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [To copy the contents of an object, including any references to embedded](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [objects; implemented by the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [copy](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [function in the](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [copy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [module.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

| deep copy: | To copy the contents of an object as well as any embedded objects, and any |
| ---------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| copy       | module.                                                                    |

[object diagram:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [A diagram that shows objects, their attributes, and the values of the at-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [tributes.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 15. Classes and objects](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Write a function named](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [rect_circle_overlap](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [that takes a Circle and a Rectangle and returns](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [True if any of the corners of the Rectangle fall inside the Circle. Or as a more challenging version,](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [return True if any part of the Rectangle falls inside the Circle.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

See Chapter 4 for examples using Turtle objects.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

## [Chapter 16](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Classes and functions](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Now that we know how to create new types, the next step is to write functions that take](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [programmer-defined objects as parameters and return them as results.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [In this chapter I](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [also present “functional programming style” and two new program development plans.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Code examples from this chapter are available from](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Time1.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [. Solutions to the exercises are at](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [py](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [16.1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Time](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[As another example of a programmer-defined type, we’ll define a class called](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [records the time of day. The class definition looks like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[class Time:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ["""Represents the time of day.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[attributes: hour, minute, second](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ["""](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[We can create a new](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [object and assign attributes for hours, minutes, and seconds:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[time = Time()](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [time.hour = 11](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [time.minute = 59](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [time.second = 30](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The state diagram for the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [object looks like Figure 16.1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[As an exercise, write a function called](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [print_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [that takes a Time object and prints it in](https://thinkpython.com/code/inlist.py) [the form](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [hour:minute:second](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [. Hint: the format sequence](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [%.2d](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [prints an integer using](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [at least two digits, including a leading zero if necessary.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Write a boolean function called](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [is_after](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [that takes two Time objects,](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [t1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newline) [and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [t2](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, and re-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [turns](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [True](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [if](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [t1](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [follows](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [t2](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [chronologically and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [False](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [otherwise. Challenge: don’t use an](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [if](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [statement.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 16. Classes and functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[time](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [hour](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[minute](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[second](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Figure 16.1: Object diagram.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

#### [16.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Pure functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[In the next few sections, we’ll write two functions that add time values. They demonstrate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [two kinds of functions: pure functions and modifiers. They also demonstrate a develop-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [ment plan I’ll call](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [prototype and patch](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [, which is a way of tackling a complex problem by](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [starting with a simple prototype and incrementally dealing with the complications.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Here is a simple prototype of](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [add_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[def add_time(t1, t2):](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [sum = Time()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [sum.hour = t1.hour + t2.hour](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [sum.minute = t1.minute + t2.minute](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [sum.second = t1.second + t2.second](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [return sum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The function creates a new](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [object, initializes its attributes, and returns a reference to](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [the new object. This is called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [pure function](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [because it does not modify any of the objects](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [passed to it as arguments and it has no effect, like displaying a value or getting user input,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [other than returning a value.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[To test this function, I’ll create two Time objects:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [start](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [contains the start time of a movie,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) like [Monty Python and the Holy Grail](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, and](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [duration](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [contains the run time of the movie,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [which is one hour 35 minutes.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[add_time](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [figures out when the movie will be done.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> start = Time()](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> start.hour = 9](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [>>> start.minute = 45](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [>>> start.second =](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[>>> duration = Time()](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> duration.hour = 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>>> duration.minute = 35](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [>>> duration.second = 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> done = add_time(start, duration)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [>>> print_time(done)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [10:80:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[The result,](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [10:80:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [might not be what you were hoping for.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [The problem is that this](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [function does not deal with cases where the number of seconds or minutes adds up to](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [more than sixty. When that happens, we have to “carry” the extra seconds into the minute](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [column or the extra minutes into the hour column.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[Here’s an improved version:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[16.3. Modifiers](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def add_time(t1, t2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [sum = Time()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [sum.hour = t1.hour + t2.hour](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [sum.minute = t1.minute + t2.minute](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [sum.second = t1.second + t2.second](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[if sum.second >= 60:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [sum.second -= 60](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [sum.minute += 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[if sum.minute >= 60:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [sum.minute -= 60](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [sum.hour += 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py)

[return sum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[Although this function is correct, it is starting to get big. We will see a shorter alternative](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [later.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

#### [16.3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [Modifiers](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Sometimes it is useful for a function to modify the objects it gets as parameters. In that case,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [the changes are visible to the caller. Functions that work this way are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [modifiers](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[increment](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [, which adds a given number of seconds to a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Time](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [object, can be written naturally](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [as a modifier. Here is a rough draft:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def increment(time, seconds):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [time.second += seconds](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[if time.second >= 60:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [time.second -= 60](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [time.minute += 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[if time.minute >= 60:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [time.minute -= 60](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) time.hour += 1

[The first line performs the basic operation; the remainder deals with the special cases we](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [saw before.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Is this function correct? What happens if](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [seconds](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is much greater than sixty?](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[In that case, it is not enough to carry once; we have to keep doing it until](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [time.second](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [less than sixty. One solution is to replace the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [statements with](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [while](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [statements. That](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [would make the function correct, but not very efficient.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [As an exercise, write a correct](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [version of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [increment](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [that doesn’t contain any loops.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Anything that can be done with modifiers can also be done with pure functions. In fact,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [some programming languages only allow pure functions.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [There is some evidence that](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [programs that use pure functions are faster to develop and less error-prone than programs](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [that use modifiers. But modifiers are convenient at times, and functional programs tend to](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [be less efficient.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 16. Classes and functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[In general, I recommend that you write pure functions whenever it is reasonable and resort](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [to modifiers only if there is a compelling advantage.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [This approach might be called a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [functional programming style](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[As an exercise, write a “pure” version of](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [increment](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [that creates and returns a new Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [object rather than modifying the parameter.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

#### [16.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [Prototyping versus planning](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[The development plan I am demonstrating is called “prototype and patch”. For each func-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [tion, I wrote a prototype that performed the basic calculation and then tested it, patching](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [errors along the way.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[This approach can be effective, especially if you don’t yet have a deep understanding](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [of the problem.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [But incremental corrections can generate code that is unnecessarily](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [complicated—since it deals with many special cases—and unreliable—since it is hard to](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [know if you have found all the errors.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[An alternative is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [designed development](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [, in which high-level insight into the problem can](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [make the programming much easier. In this case, the insight is that a Time object is really](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [a three-digit number in base 60 (see](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [). The](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [second](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [attribute is the “ones column”, the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [minute](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [attribute is the “sixties column”, and the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [hour](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [attribute is the “thirty-six hundreds column”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[When we wrote](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [add_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [increment](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, we were effectively doing addition in base 60,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [which is why we had to carry from one column to the next.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[This observation suggests another approach to the whole problem—we can convert Time](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [objects to integers and take advantage of the fact that the computer knows how to do](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [integer arithmetic.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Here is a function that converts Times to integers:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[def time_to_int(time):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [minutes = time.hour * 60 + time.minute](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [seconds = minutes * 60 + time.second](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [return seconds](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

And here is a function that converts an integer to a Time (recall that divmod divides the first [argument by the second and returns the quotient and remainder as a tuple).](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[def int_to_time(seconds):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [time = Time()](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [minutes, time.second = divmod(seconds, 60)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [time.hour, time.minute = divmod(minutes, 60)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return time](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[You might have to think a bit, and run some tests, to convince yourself that these functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [are correct. One way to test them is to check that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [time_to_int(int_to_time(x)) == x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [many values of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [. This is an example of a consistency check.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Once you are convinced they are correct, you can use them to rewrite](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [add_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[def add_time(t1, t2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [seconds = time_to_int(t1) + time_to_int(t2)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return int_to_time(seconds)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[16.5. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This version is shorter than the original, and easier to verify.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [As an exercise, rewrite](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [increment](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [using](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [time_to_int](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [int_to_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[In some ways, converting from base 60 to base 10 and back is harder than just dealing with](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [times. Base conversion is more abstract; our intuition for dealing with time values is better.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[But if we have the insight to treat times as base 60 numbers and make the investment of](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [writing the conversion functions (](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [time_to_int](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [and](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [int_to_time](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [), we get a program that](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [is shorter, easier to read and debug, and more reliable.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[It is also easier to add features later. For example, imagine subtracting two Times to find](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [the duration between them. The naive approach would be to implement subtraction with](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [borrowing. Using the conversion functions would be easier and more likely to be correct.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Ironically, sometimes making a problem harder (or more general) makes it easier (because](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [there are fewer special cases and fewer opportunities for error).](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

#### [16.5](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Debugging](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[A Time object is well-formed if the values of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [minute](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [second](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [are between 0 and 60](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [(including 0 but not 60) and if](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [hour](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [is positive.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [hour](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [minute](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [should be integer values,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [but we might allow](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [second](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [to have a fraction part.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Requirements like these are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [invariants](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [because they should always be true. To put](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [it a different way, if they are not true, something has gone wrong.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Writing code to check invariants can help detect errors and find their causes. For example,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [you might have a function like](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [valid_time](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [that takes a Time object and returns](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [False](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [if it](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [violates an invariant:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[def valid_time(time):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [if time.hour < 0 or time.minute < 0 or time.second < 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return False](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [if time.minute >= 60 or time.second >= 60:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [return True](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[At the beginning of each function you could check the arguments to make sure they are](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) valid:

[def add_time(t1, t2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [if not valid_time(t1) or not valid_time(t2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [raise ValueError(](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [invalid Time object in add_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ' ) [seconds = time_to_int(t1) + time_to_int(t2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return int_to_time(seconds)](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[Or you could use an](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [assert statement](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [, which checks a given invariant and raises an excep-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [tion if it fails:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def add_time(t1, t2):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [assert valid_time(t1) and valid_time(t2)](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [seconds = time_to_int(t1) + time_to_int(t2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return int_to_time(seconds)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[assert](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [statements are useful because they distinguish code that deals with normal condi-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [tions from code that checks for errors.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 16. Classes and functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [16.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[prototype and patch:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A development plan that involves writing a rough draft of a pro-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [gram, testing, and correcting errors as they are found.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[designed development:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [A development plan that involves high-level insight into the](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [problem and more planning than incremental development or prototype develop-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [ment.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[pure function:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [A function that does not modify any of the objects it receives as arguments.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [Most pure functions are fruitful.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[modifier:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [A function that changes one or more of the objects it receives as arguments. Most](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [modifiers are void; that is, they return](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [None](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[functional programming style:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [A style of program design in which the majority of func-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [tions are pure.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[invariant:](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [A condition that should always be true during the execution of a program.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[assert statement:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [A statement that checks a condition and raises an exception if it fails.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

#### [16.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Code examples from this chapter are available from](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Time1.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [; solutions to the exercises are available from](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Time1_soln.py](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Exercise 16.1.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Write a function called](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [mul_time](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [that takes a Time object and a number and returns](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [a new Time object that contains the product of the original Time and the number.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Then use](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [mul_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to write a function that takes a Time object that represents the finishing time](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [in a race, and a number that represents the distance, and returns a Time object that represents the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [average pace (time per mile).](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Exercise 16.2.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [The](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [datetime](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [module provides](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) time [objects that are similar to the Time objects](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [in this chapter, but they provide a rich set of methods and operators. Read the documentation at](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [http: // docs. python. org/ 3/ library/ datetime. html](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

2. [2. Write a program that takes a birthday as input and prints the user’s age and the number of](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [days, hours, minutes and seconds until their next birthday.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

3. [3. For two people born on different days, there is a day when one is twice as old as the other.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [That’s their Double Day.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [Write a program that takes two birth dates and computes their](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [Double Day.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

4. [4. For a little more challenge, write the more general version that computes the day when one](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [person is n times older than the other.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)
