## [Chapter 17](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Classes and methods](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Although we are using some of Python’s object-oriented features, the programs from the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [last two chapters are not really object-oriented because they don’t represent the relation-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [ships between programmer-defined types and the functions that operate on them. The next](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [step is to transform those functions into methods that make the relationships explicit.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Code examples from this chapter are available from](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Time2.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [, and solutions to the exercises are in](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/Point2_](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [soln.py](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

#### [17.1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [Object-oriented features](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[Python is an](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [object-oriented programming language](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [, which means that it provides fea-](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [tures that support object-oriented programming, which has these defining characteristics:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

- [• Programs include class and method definitions.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

- [• Most of the computation is expressed in terms of operations on objects.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

- Objects often represent things in the real world, and methods often correspond to the [ways things in the real world interact.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[For example, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [class defined in Chapter 16 corresponds to the way people record](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [the time of day, and the functions we defined correspond to the kinds of things people do](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [with times. Similarly, the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Rectangle](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [classes in Chapter 15 correspond to the](http://gutenberg.org/) [mathematical concepts of a point and a rectangle.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[So far, we have not taken advantage of the features Python provides to support object-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [oriented programming.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [These features are not strictly necessary; most of them provide](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [alternative syntax for things we have already done. But in many cases, the alternative is](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [more concise and more accurately conveys the structure of the program.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

| For example, in         | Time1.py | there is no obvious connection between the class definition and |
| ----------------------- | -------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| tion takes at least one | Time     | object as an argument.                                          |

[Chapter 17. Classes and methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This observation is the motivation for](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [; a method is a function that is associated](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [with a particular class. We have seen methods for strings, lists, dictionaries and tuples. In](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [this chapter, we will define methods for programmer-defined types.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[Methods are semantically the same as functions, but there are two syntactic differences:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

- [• Methods are defined inside a class definition in order to make the relationship be-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [tween the class and the method explicit.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

- [• The syntax for invoking a method is different from the syntax for calling a function.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[In the next few sections, we will take the functions from the previous two chapters and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [transform them into methods. This transformation is purely mechanical; you can do it by](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [following a sequence of steps. If you are comfortable converting from one form to another,](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [you will be able to choose the best form for whatever you are doing.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

#### 17.2 [Printing objects](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[In Chapter 16, we defined a class named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [Time](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [and in Section 16.1, you wrote a function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [named](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [print_time](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[class Time:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) ["""Represents the time of day."""](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[def print_time(time):](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [print(](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [%.2d:%.2d:%.2d](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) ['](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [% (time.hour, time.minute, time.second))](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[To call this function, you have to pass a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) Time [object as an argument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> start = Time()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> start.hour = 9](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> start.minute = 45](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> start.second = 00](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> print_time(start)](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [09:45:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

To make [print_time](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [a method, all we have to do is move the function definition inside the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) class definition. Notice the change in indentation.

class Time: [def print_time(time):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [print(](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [%.2d:%.2d:%.2d](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [% (time.hour, time.minute, time.second))](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Now there are two ways to call](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. The first (and less common) way is to use](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [function syntax:](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[>>> Time.print_time(start)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [09:45:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py)

[In this use of dot notation,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Time](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is the name of the class, and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [print_time](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [is the name of the](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [method.](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [start](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [is passed as a parameter.](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[The second (and more concise) way is to use method syntax:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> start.print_time()](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) 09:45:00

[17.3. Another example](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[In this use of dot notation,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [print_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [is the name of the method (again), and](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) start is [the object the method is invoked on, which is called the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [subject](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [Just as the subject of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [a sentence is what the sentence is about, the subject of a method invocation is what the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [method is about.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[Inside the method, the subject is assigned to the first parameter, so in this case](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) start is assigned to time .

[By convention, the first parameter of a method is called](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [self](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [, so it would be more common](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [to write](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [print_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[class Time:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [def print_time(self):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [print(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [%.2d:%.2d:%.2d](http://www.thinkpython.com/) ['](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [% (self.hour, self.minute, self.second))](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[The reason for this convention is an implicit metaphor:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

- [• The syntax for a function call,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [print_time(start)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [, suggests that the function is the](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [active agent. It says something like, “Hey](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [print_time](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [! Here’s an object for you to](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [print.”](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

- [• In object-oriented programming, the objects are the active agents. A method invoca-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [tion like](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [start.print_time()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [says “Hey](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [start](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [! Please print yourself.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py)

[This change in perspective might be more polite, but it is not obvious that it is useful. In the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [examples we have seen so far, it may not be. But sometimes shifting responsibility from the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [functions onto the objects makes it possible to write more versatile functions (or methods),](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and makes it easier to maintain and reuse code.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[As an exercise, rewrite](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [time_to_int](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [(from Section 16.4) as a method. You might be tempted](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to rewrite](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [int_to_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [as a method, too, but that doesn’t really make sense because there](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [would be no object to invoke it on.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

#### [17.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Another example](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Here’s a version of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) increment (from Section 16.3) rewritten as a method:

# inside class Time:

[def increment(self, seconds):](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [seconds += self.time_to_int()](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [return int_to_time(seconds)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[This version assumes that](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [time_to_int](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [is written as a method. Also, note that it is a pure](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [function, not a modifier.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Here’s how you would invoke](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [increment](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[>>> start.print_time()](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [09:45:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> end = start.increment(1337)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> end.print_time()](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [10:07:17](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 17. Classes and methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The subject,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [start](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, gets assigned to the first parameter,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [self](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [. The argument,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [, gets](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [assigned to the second parameter,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [seconds](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[This mechanism can be confusing, especially if you make an error.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [For example, if you](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [invoke](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [increment](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [with two arguments, you get:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[>>> end = start.increment(1337, 460)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [TypeError: increment() takes 2 positional arguments but 3 were given](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[The error message is initially confusing, because there are only two arguments in paren-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [theses. But the subject is also considered an argument, so all together that’s three.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[By the way, a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [positional argument](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [is an argument that doesn’t have a parameter name;](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [that is, it is not a keyword argument. In this function call:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[sketch(parrot, cage, dead=True)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[parrot](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [cage](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [are positional, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [dead](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [is a keyword argument.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

#### 17.4 [A more complicated example](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[Rewriting](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [is_after](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [(from Section 16.1) is slightly more complicated because it takes two](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [Time objects as parameters. In this case it is conventional to name the first parameter](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [self](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [and the second parameter](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [other](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[# inside class Time:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[def is_after(self, other):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return self.time_to_int() > other.time_to_int()](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[To use this method, you have to invoke it on one object and pass the other as an argument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> end.is_after(start)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [True](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[One nice thing about this syntax is that it almost reads like English: “end is after start?”](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

#### 17.5 [The init method](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[The init method (short for “initialization”) is a special method that gets invoked when an](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [object is instantiated. Its full name is](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [__init__](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [(two underscore characters, followed by](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) init [, and then two more underscores). An init method for the](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [class might look like](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) this:

[# inside class Time:](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py)

[def __init__(self, hour=0, minute=0, second=0):](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [self.hour = hour](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [self.minute = minute](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [self.second = second](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[It is common for the parameters of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [__init__](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [to have the same names as the attributes. The](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [statement](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[self.hour = hour](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[17.6. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py) [__str__](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py) [method](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[stores the value of the parameter](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [hour](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [as an attribute of](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [self](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[The parameters are optional, so if you call](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [Time](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [with no arguments, you get the default](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [values.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[>>> time = Time()](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [>>> time.print_time()](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [00:00:00](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[If you provide one argument, it overrides](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [hour](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[>>> time = Time (9)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [>>> time.print_time()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [09:00:00](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[If you provide two arguments, they override](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [hour](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [and](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [minute](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[>>> time = Time(9, 45)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>>> time.print_time()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [09:45:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[And if you provide three arguments, they override all three default values.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[As an exercise, write an init method for the](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [class that takes](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [as optional](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [parameters and assigns them to the corresponding attributes.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

#### [17.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [The](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [__str__](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [method](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[__str__](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is a special method, like](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) __init__ [, that is supposed to return a string representa-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [tion of an object.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[For example, here is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [str](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk2.py) [method for Time objects:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk2.py)

[# inside class Time:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def __str__(self):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [%.2d:%.2d:%.2d](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) ' [% (self.hour, self.minute, self.second)](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[When you](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [print](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [an object, Python invokes the](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [str](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [method:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[>>> time = Time(9, 45)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) >>> print(time) [09:45:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[When I write a new class, I almost always start by writing](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [__init__](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, which makes it easier](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [to instantiate objects, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [__str__](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, which is useful for debugging.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[As an exercise, write a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [str](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [method for the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [class. Create a Point object and print it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

#### [17.7](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Operator overloading](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[By](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [defining](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [other](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rubber_duck_debugging) [special](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [methods,](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [you](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [can](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [specify](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [the](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [behavior](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [operators](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [on](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [programmer-defined types. For example, if you define a method named](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) __add__ for the [Time](https://thinkpython.com/code/double.py) [class, you can use the](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [operator on Time objects.](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[Here is what the definition might look like:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 17. Classes and methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[# inside class Time:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def __add__(self, other):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [seconds = self.time_to_int() + other.time_to_int()](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [return int_to_time(seconds)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[And here is how you could use it:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[>>> start = Time(9, 45)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [>>> duration = Time(1, 35)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [>>> print(start + duration)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [11:20:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

[When you apply the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [+](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [operator to Time objects, Python invokes](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [__add__](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [. When you print](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [the result, Python invokes](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [__str__](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [. So there is a lot happening behind the scenes!](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Changing the behavior of an operator so that it works with programmer-defined types is](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) called [operator overloading](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [. For every operator in Python there is a corresponding spe-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [cial method, like](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [__add__](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [. For more details, see](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [http://docs.python.org/3/reference/](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [datamodel.html#specialnames](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[As an exercise, write an](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [add](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [method for the Point class.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

#### [17.8](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [Type-based dispatch](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[In the previous section we added two Time objects, but you also might want to add an](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [integer to a Time object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [The following is a version of](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [__add__](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [that checks the type of](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) other [and invokes either](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [add_time](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [or](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [increment](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) :

[# inside class Time:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[def __add__(self, other):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [if isinstance(other, Time):](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [return self.add_time(other)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [return self.increment(other)](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

def add_time(self, other): seconds = self.time_to_int() + other.time_to_int() [return int_to_time(seconds)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[def increment(self, seconds):](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [seconds += self.time_to_int()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return int_to_time(seconds)](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[The built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [isinstance](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [takes a value and a class object, and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [True](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [if the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [value is an instance of the class.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[If](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [other](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [is a Time object,](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [__add__](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [invokes](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [add_time](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [. Otherwise it assumes that the param-](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [eter is a number and invokes](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [increment](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [. This operation is called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [type-based dispatch](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [because it dispatches the computation to different methods based on the type of the argu-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) ments.

[Here are examples that use the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [+](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [operator with different types:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[17.9. Polymorphism](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> start = Time(9, 45)](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [>>> duration = Time(1, 35)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> print(start + duration)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [11:20:00](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [>>> print(start + 1337)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) 10:07:17

[Unfortunately, this implementation of addition is not commutative.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [If the integer is the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [first operand, you get](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[>>> print(1337 + start)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [int](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book1.py) [instance](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book1.py) '

[The problem is, instead of asking the Time object to add an integer, Python is asking an](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [integer to add a Time object, and it doesn’t know how. But there is a clever solution for this](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [problem: the special method](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) __radd__ [, which stands for “right-side add”. This method](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is invoked when a Time object appears on the right side of the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) + operator. Here’s the [definition:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[# inside class Time:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def __radd__(self, other):](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [return self.__add__(other)](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[And here’s how it’s used:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[>>> print(1337 + start)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [10:07:17](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[As an exercise, write an](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [add](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [method for Points that works with either a Point object or a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [tuple:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

- [• If the second operand is a Point, the method should return a new Point whose](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [coordinate is the sum of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [coordinates of the operands, and likewise for the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [coordinates.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• If the second operand is a tuple, the method should add the first element of the tuple](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [to the](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [coordinate and the second element to the](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [coordinate, and return a new](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [Point with the result.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

#### 17.9 [Polymorphism](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Type-based dispatch is useful when it is necessary, but (fortunately) it is not always neces-](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [sary. Often you can avoid it by writing functions that work correctly for arguments with](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [different types.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Many of the functions we wrote for strings also work for other sequence types. For exam-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [ple, in Section 11.2 we used](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [histogram](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [to count the number of times each letter appears in](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [a word.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def histogram(s):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [d = dict()](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [for c in s:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [if c not in d:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [d[c] = 1](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 17. Classes and methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [d[c] = d[c]+1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return d](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[This function also works for lists, tuples, and even dictionaries, as long as the elements of](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [s](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [are hashable, so they can be used as keys in](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

>>> t = [ ' spam ' , ' egg ' , ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [bacon](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [spam](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [>>> histogram(t)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [{](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [bacon](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [: 1,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [egg](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [: 1,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [spam](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [: 4}](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Functions that work with several types are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [polymorphic](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book2.py) [Polymorphism can fa-](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#printf-style-string-formatting) [cilitate code reuse. For example, the built-in function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [sum](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [, which adds the elements of a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [sequence, works as long as the elements of the sequence support addition.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Since Time objects provide an](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [add](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [method, they work with](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [sum](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[>>> t1 = Time(7, 43)](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [>>> t2 = Time(7, 41)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [>>> t3 = Time(7, 37)](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [>>> total = sum([t1, t2, t3])](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [>>> print(total)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) 23:01:00

[In general, if all of the operations inside a function work with a given type, the function](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [works with that type.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The best kind of polymorphism is the unintentional kind, where you discover that a func-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [tion you already wrote can be applied to a type you never planned for.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### 17.10 [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[It is legal to add attributes to objects at any point in the execution of a program, but if](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [you have objects with the same type that don’t have the same attributes, it is easy to make](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [mistakes. It is considered a good idea to initialize all of an object’s attributes in the init](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [method.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[If you are not sure whether an object has a particular attribute, you can use the built-in](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) function [hasattr](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [(see Section 15.7).](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

Another way to access attributes is the built-in function vars , which takes an object and [returns a dictionary that maps from attribute names (as strings) to their values:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[>>> p = Point(3, 4)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [>>> vars(p)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) { ' y ' : 4, ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [: 3}](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[For purposes of debugging, you might find it useful to keep this function handy:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[def print_attributes(obj):](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [for attr in vars(obj):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [print(attr, getattr(obj, attr))](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[print_attributes](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [traverses the dictionary and prints each attribute name and its corre-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [sponding value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[The built-in function](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [getattr](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [takes an object and an attribute name (as a string) and returns](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [the attribute’s value.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[17.11. Interface and implementation](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [17.11](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Interface and implementation](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[One of the goals of object-oriented design is to make software more maintainable, which](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [means that you can keep the program working when other parts of the system change, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [modify the program to meet new requirements.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[A design principle that helps achieve that goal is to keep interfaces separate from imple-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [mentations. For objects, that means that the methods a class provides should not depend](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [on how the attributes are represented.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[For example, in this chapter we developed a class that represents a time of day. Methods](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [provided by this class include](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [time_to_int](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [is_after](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [, and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [add_time](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py)

[We could implement those methods in several ways. The details of the implementation](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [depend on how we represent time. In this chapter, the attributes of a](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) Time object are hour , [minute](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [second](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[As an alternative, we could replace these attributes with a single integer representing the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [number of seconds since midnight. This implementation would make some methods, like](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is_after](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [, easier to write, but it makes other methods harder.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[After you deploy a new class, you might discover a better implementation. If other parts](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [of the program are using your class, it might be time-consuming and error-prone to change](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [the interface.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[But if you designed the interface carefully, you can change the implementation without](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [changing the interface, which means that other parts of the program don’t have to change.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [17.12](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[object-oriented language:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [A](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [language](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [that](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [provides](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [features,](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [such](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [as](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [programmer-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [defined types and methods, that facilitate object-oriented programming.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[object-oriented programming:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [A style of programming in which data and the operations](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [that manipulate it are organized into classes and methods.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

method: [A function that is defined inside a class definition and is invoked on instances of](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [that class.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[subject:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The object a method is invoked on.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[positional argument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [An argument that does not include a parameter name, so it is not a](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [keyword argument.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[operator overloading:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Changing the behavior of an operator like](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [so it works with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/inlist.py) [programmer-defined type.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[type-based dispatch:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [A programming pattern that checks the type of an operand and in-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [vokes different functions for different types.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[polymorphic:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Pertaining to a function that can work with more than one type.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 17. Classes and methods](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

Write a definition for a class named](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [Kangaroo](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [with the following methods:](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

1. An [__init__](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [method that initializes an attribute named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [pouch_contents](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [to an empty list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book1.py)

2. [2. 3. A [__str__](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [method that returns a string representation of the Kangaroo object and the con-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [tents of the pouch.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Test your code by creating two](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Kangaroo](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [objects, assigning them to variables named](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [kanga](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) roo [, and then adding](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [roo](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [to the contents of](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [kanga](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [’s pouch.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

## [Chapter 18](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[The language feature most often associated with object-oriented programming is](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [inheri-](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [tance](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [. Inheritance is the ability to define a new class that is a modified version of an ex-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [isting class. In this chapter I demonstrate inheritance using classes that represent playing](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [cards, decks of cards, and poker hands.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[If you don’t play poker, you can read about it at](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Poker](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [but you don’t have to; I’ll tell you what you need to know for the exercises.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Code examples from this chapter are available from](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Card.py](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [18.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Card objects](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[There are fifty-two cards in a deck, each of which belongs to one of four suits and one of](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [thirteen ranks. The suits are Spades, Hearts, Diamonds, and Clubs (in descending order in](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [bridge). The ranks are Ace, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, Jack, Queen, and King. Depending on](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [the game that you are playing, an Ace may be higher than King or lower than 2.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[If we want to define a new object to represent a playing card, it is obvious what the at-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) tributes should be: rank and suit . It is not as obvious what type the attributes should be. [One possibility is to use strings containing words like](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Spade](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [for suits and](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [Queen](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [for](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [ranks. One problem with this implementation is that it would not be easy to compare cards](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [to see which had a higher rank or suit.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[An alternative is to use integers to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [encode](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [the ranks and suits. In this context, “encode”](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [means that we are going to define a mapping between numbers and suits, or between](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [numbers and ranks.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [This kind of encoding is not meant to be a secret (that would be](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [“encryption”).](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[For example, this table shows the suits and the corresponding integer codes:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

| Spades   | →   |
| -------- | --- |
| Hearts   | →   |
| Diamonds | →   |
| Clubs    | →   |

[Chapter 18. Inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This code makes it easy to compare cards; because higher suits map to higher numbers, we](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [can compare suits by comparing their codes.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The mapping for ranks is fairly obvious; each of the numerical ranks maps to the corre-](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [sponding integer, and for face cards:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

| Jack  | →   |
| ----- | --- |
| Queen | →   |
| King  | →   |

[I am using the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [→](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [symbol to make it clear that these mappings are not part of the Python](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [program. They are part of the program design, but they don’t appear explicitly in the code.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[The class definition for](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [Card](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [looks like this:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[class Card:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ["""Represents a standard playing card."""](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[def __init__(self, suit=0, rank=2):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [self.suit = suit](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [self.rank = rank](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[As usual, the init method takes an optional parameter for each attribute. The default card](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [is the 2 of Clubs.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[To create a Card, you call](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Card](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [with the suit and rank of the card you want.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[queen_of_diamonds = Card(1, 12)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### 18.2 [Class attributes](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[In order to print Card objects in a way that people can easily read, we need a mapping](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [from the integer codes to the corresponding ranks and suits. A natural way to do that is](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [with lists of strings. We assign these lists to](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [class attributes](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[# inside class Card:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[suit_names = [](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) Clubs ' , ' Diamonds ' , ' Hearts ' , ' Spades ' ] rank_names = [None, ' Ace ' , ' ' , ' ' , ' ' , ' ' , ' ' , ' ' , ' ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Jack](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Queen](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [King](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[def __str__(self):](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [%s of %s](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [% (Card.rank_names[self.rank],](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [Card.suit_names[self.suit])](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Variables like](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [suit_names](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [rank_names](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, which are defined inside a class but outside](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [of any method, are called class attributes because they are associated with the class object](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) Card .

[This term distinguishes them from variables like](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [suit](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [rank](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [, which are called](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [instance](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [attributes](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [because they are associated with a particular instance.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Both kinds of attribute are accessed using dot notation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [For example, in](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [__str__](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newline) [self](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is a Card object,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [self.rank](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is its rank.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Similarly,](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [Card](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [is a class object,](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py) [and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Card.rank_names](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is a list of strings associated with the class.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[18.3. Comparing cards](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[type](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [list](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Card](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [suit_names](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

list

[rank_names](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[card1](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [suit](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

[rank](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[Figure 18.1: Object diagram.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Every card has its own](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [suit](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [rank](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [, but there is only one copy of](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) suit_names and [rank_names](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Putting it all together, the expression](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Card.rank_names[self.rank]](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [means “use the at-](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [tribute](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [rank](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [from the object](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [self](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [as an index into the list](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [rank_names](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [from the class](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [Card](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [and select the appropriate string.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

[The first element of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [rank_names](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [None](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [because there is no card with rank zero. By includ-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [ing](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [None](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [as a place-keeper, we get a mapping with the nice property that the index 2 maps](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [to the string](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [, and so on. To avoid this tweak, we could have used a dictionary instead](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [of a list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1_soln.py)

To save space, I](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [didn’t draw the contents of](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) suit_names [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [rank_names](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

#### [18.3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [Comparing cards](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[For built-in types, there are relational operators (](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [<](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [==](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, etc.) that compare values and de-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [termine when one is greater than, less than, or equal to another. For programmer-defined](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [types, we can override the behavior of the built-in operators by providing a method named](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [__lt__](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, which stands for “less than”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[__lt__](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [takes two parameters,](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [self](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [other](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [if](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [self](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [is strictly less than](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [other](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[The correct ordering for cards is not obvious. For example, which is better, the 3 of Clubs](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [or the 2 of Diamonds? One has a higher rank, but the other has a higher suit. In order to](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [compare cards, you have to decide whether rank or suit is more important.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[The answer might depend on what game you are playing, but to keep things simple, we’ll](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [make the arbitrary choice that suit is more important, so all of the Spades outrank all of the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Diamonds, and so on.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 18. Inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[With that decided, we can write](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [__lt__](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[# inside class Card:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf's_law)

[def __lt__(self, other):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [# check the suits](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [if self.suit < other.suit: return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [if self.suit > other.suit: return False](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[# suits are the same... check ranks](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [return self.rank < other.rank](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[You can write this more concisely using tuple comparison:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[# inside class Card:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[def __lt__(self, other):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [t1 = self.suit, self.rank](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [t2 = other.suit, other.rank](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return t1 < t2](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[As an exercise, write an](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [__lt__](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [method for Time objects. You can use tuple comparison,](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [but you also might consider comparing integers.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

#### [18.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [Decks](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[Now that we have Cards, the next step is to define Decks. Since a deck is made up of cards,](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [it is natural for each Deck to contain a list of cards as an attribute.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The following is a class definition for](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [. The init method creates the attribute](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [cards](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [generates the standard set of fifty-two cards:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[class Deck:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[def __init__(self):](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [self.cards = []](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [for suit in range(4):](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [for rank in range(1, 14):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) card = Card(suit, rank) [self.cards.append(card)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[The easiest way to populate the deck is with a nested loop. The outer loop enumerates the](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [suits from 0 to 3. The inner loop enumerates the ranks from 1 to 13. Each iteration creates](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [a new Card with the current suit and rank, and appends it to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [self.cards](http://gutenberg.org/) [.](http://gutenberg.org/)

#### 18.5 [Printing the deck](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[Here is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [__str__](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [method for](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d)

[# inside class Deck:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[def __str__(self):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [res = []](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[18.6. Add, remove, shuffle and sort](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[for card in self.cards:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [res.append(str(card))](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [\n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.join(res)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[This method demonstrates an efficient way to accumulate a large string: building a list](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [of strings and then using the string method](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [join](https://thinkpython.com/code) [. The built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code) str invokes the __str__ [method on each card and returns the string representation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[Since we invoke](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [join](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [on a newline character, the cards are separated by newlines. Here’s](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [what the result looks like:](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt)

[>>> deck = Deck()](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [>>> print(deck)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [Ace of Clubs](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [2 of Clubs](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [3 of Clubs](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [...](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [10 of Spades](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [Jack of Spades](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [Queen of Spades](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [King of Spades](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[Even though the result appears on 52 lines, it is one long string that contains newlines.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

#### [18.6](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Add, remove, shuffle and sort](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[To deal cards, we would like a method that removes a card from the deck and returns it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [The list method](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [pop](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [provides a convenient way to do that:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[# inside class Deck:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[def pop_card(self):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return self.cards.pop()](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Since](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [pop](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [removes the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [last](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [card in the list, we are dealing from the bottom of the deck.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[To add a card, we can use the list method](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [append](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) :

[# inside class Deck:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

def add_card(self, card): [self.cards.append(card)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[A method like this that uses another method without doing much work is sometimes called](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [veneer](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. The metaphor comes from woodworking, where a veneer is a thin layer of good](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [quality wood glued to the surface of a cheaper piece of wood to improve the appearance.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[In this case](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [add_card](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is a “thin” method that expresses a list operation in terms appropriate](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [for decks. It improves the appearance, or interface, of the implementation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[As another example, we can write a Deck method named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [shuffle](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [using the function](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [shuffle](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [from the](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [random](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [module:](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d)

[# inside class Deck:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[def shuffle(self):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [random.shuffle(self.cards)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 18. Inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Don’t forget to import](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [random](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[As an exercise, write a Deck method named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [sort](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [that uses the list method](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [sort](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [to sort the](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [cards in a](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [sort](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [uses the](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [__lt__](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [method we defined to determine the order.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

#### [18.7](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Inheritance](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Inheritance is the ability to define a new class that is a modified version of an existing class.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [As an example, let’s say we want a class to represent a “hand”, that is, the cards held by](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [one player. A hand is similar to a deck: both are made up of a collection of cards, and both](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [require operations like adding and removing cards.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[A hand is also different from a deck; there are operations we want for hands that don’t](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [make sense for a deck. For example, in poker we might compare two hands to see which](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [one wins. In bridge, we might compute a score for a hand in order to make a bid.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[This relationship between classes—similar, but different—lends itself to inheritance.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [To](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [define a new class that inherits from an existing class, you put the name of the existing](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [class in parentheses:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[class Hand(Deck):](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) ["""Represents a hand of playing cards."""](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[This definition indicates that](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Hand](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [inherits from](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [; that means we can use methods like](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [pop_card](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [add_card](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [for Hands as well as Decks.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[When a new class inherits from an existing one, the existing one is called the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [parent](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [the new class is called the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [child](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[In this example,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [Hand](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [inherits](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [__init__](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [from](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [, but it doesn’t really do what we want:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [instead of populating the hand with 52 new cards, the init method for Hands should ini-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [tialize](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [cards](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [with an empty list.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[If we provide an init method in the](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Hand](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py) [class, it overrides the one in the](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [class:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[# inside class Hand:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[def __init__(self, label=](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [''](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [):](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [self.cards = []](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) self.label = label

[When you create a Hand, Python invokes this init method, not the one in](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[>>> hand = Hand(](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [new hand](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [>>> hand.cards](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [] [>>> hand.label](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) ' [new hand](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[The other methods are inherited from](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Deck](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [, so we can use](https://thinkpython.com/code/inlist.py) [pop_card](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [add_card](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [to deal](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) a card:

[>>> deck = Deck()](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [>>> card = deck.pop_card()](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> hand.add_card(card)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> print(hand)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [King of Spades](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[18.8. Class diagrams](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[A natural next step is to encapsulate this code in a method called](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [move_cards](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py)

# inside class Deck:

[def move_cards(self, hand, num):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [for i in range(num):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [hand.add_card(self.pop_card())](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[move_cards](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [takes two arguments, a Hand object and the number of cards to deal. It modi-](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [fies both](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [self](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [hand](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [, and returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) None .

[In some games, cards are moved from one hand to another, or from a hand back to the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [deck. You can use](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [move_cards](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [for any of these operations:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [self](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [can be either a Deck or a](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [Hand, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [hand](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [, despite the name, can also be a](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [Deck](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Inheritance is a useful feature. Some programs that would be repetitive without inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [can be written more elegantly with it. Inheritance can facilitate code reuse, since you can](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [customize the behavior of parent classes without having to modify them. In some cases,](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [the inheritance structure reflects the natural structure of the problem, which makes the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [design easier to understand.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[On the other hand, inheritance can make programs difficult to read. When a method is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [invoked, it is sometimes not clear where to find its definition. The relevant code may be](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [spread across several modules. Also, many of the things that can be done using inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [can be done as well or better without it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [18.8](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Class diagrams](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[So far we have seen stack diagrams, which show the state of a program, and object dia-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [grams, which show the attributes of an object and their values. These diagrams represent](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [a snapshot in the execution of a program, so they change as the program runs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[They are also highly detailed; for some purposes, too detailed. A class diagram is a more](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [abstract representation of the structure of a program. Instead of showing individual ob-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [jects, it shows classes and the relationships between them.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

There are several kinds of relationship between classes:

- [• Objects in one class might contain references to objects in another class.
