For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [each Rectangle contains a reference to a Point, and each Deck contains references to](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [many Cards.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [This kind of relationship is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [HAS-A](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, as in, “a Rectangle has a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Point.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• One class might inherit from another. This relationship is called](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [IS-A](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [, as in, “a Hand](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [is a kind of a Deck.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

- [• One class might depend on another in the sense that objects in one class take ob-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [jects in the second class as parameters, or use objects in the second class as part of a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [computation. This kind of relationship is called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [dependency](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[A](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [class diagram](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [is a graphical representation of these relationships.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [For example, Fig-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [ure 18.2 shows the relationships between](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Card](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [Deck](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Collatz_conjecture) [and](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting) [Hand](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting) [.](https://wiki.python.org/moin/HowTo/Sorting)

[Chapter 18. Inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[*](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[Card](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf's_law)

[Hand](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Figure 18.2: Class diagram.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[The arrow with a hollow triangle head represents an IS-A relationship; in this case it indi-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [cates that Hand inherits from Deck.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[The standard arrow head represents a HAS-A relationship; in this case a Deck has refer-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [ences to Card objects.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[The star (](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [*](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [) near the arrow head is a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [multiplicity](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [; it indicates how many Cards a Deck has.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [A multiplicity can be a simple number, like](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [, a range, like](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [5..7](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [or a star, which indicates](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [that a Deck can have any number of Cards.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[There are no dependencies in this diagram. They would normally be shown with a dashed](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [arrow. Or if there are a lot of dependencies, they are sometimes omitted.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[A more detailed diagram might show that a Deck actually contains a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [list](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [of Cards, but](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [built-in types like list and dict are usually not included in class diagrams.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [18.9](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1_soln.py) [Debugging](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Inheritance can make debugging difficult because when you invoke a method on an object,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [it might be hard to figure out which method will be invoked.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Suppose you are writing a function that works with Hand objects. You would like it to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [work with all kinds of Hands, like PokerHands, BridgeHands, etc. If you invoke a method](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [like](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [shuffle](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [, you might get the one defined in](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [, but if any of the subclasses override](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [this method, you’ll get that version instead. This behavior is usually a good thing, but it](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [can be confusing.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

Any time you are unsure about the flow of execution through your program, the sim- [plest solution is to add print statements at the beginning of the relevant methods.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) If Deck.shuffle [prints a message that says something like](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Running Deck.shuffle](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, then as](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [the program runs it traces the flow of execution.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[As an alternative, you could use this function, which takes an object and a method name](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [(as a string) and returns the class that provides the definition of the method:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[def find_defining_class(obj, meth_name):](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [for ty in type(obj).mro():](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [if meth_name in ty.__dict__:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [return ty](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[Here’s an example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[>>> hand = Hand()](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [>>> find_defining_class(hand,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [shuffle](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) <class ' [__main__.Deck](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [>](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[18.10. Data encapsulation](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[So the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [shuffle](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [method for this Hand is the one in](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Deck](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[find_defining_class](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [uses the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [mro](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [method to get the list of class objects (types) that will be](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [searched for methods. “MRO” stands for “method resolution order”, which is the sequence](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [of classes Python searches to “resolve” a method name.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Here’s a design suggestion: when you override a method, the interface of the new method](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [should be the same as the old. It should take the same parameters, return the same type,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [and obey the same preconditions and postconditions. If you follow this rule, you will find](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [that any function designed to work with an instance of a parent class, like a Deck, will also](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [work with instances of child classes like a Hand and PokerHand.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[If you violate this rule, which is called the “Liskov substitution principle”, your code will](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [collapse like (sorry) a house of cards.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

#### [18.10](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [Data encapsulation](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The previous chapters demonstrate a development plan we might call “object-oriented](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [design”. We identified objects we needed—like](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [Point](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [Rectangle](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [Time](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [—and defined](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [classes to represent them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [In each case there is an obvious correspondence between the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [object and some entity in the real world (or at least a mathematical world).](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[But sometimes it is less obvious what objects you need and how they should interact. In](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [that case you need a different development plan.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [In the same way that we discovered](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [function interfaces by encapsulation and generalization, we can discover class interfaces](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [by](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [data encapsulation](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Markov analysis, from Section 13.8, provides a good example.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [If you download my](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [code from](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, you’ll see that it uses two global](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [variables—](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [suffix_map](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [prefix](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [—that are read and written from several functions.](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[suffix_map = {}](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [prefix = ()](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[Because these variables are global, we can only run one analysis at a time. If we read two](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [texts, their prefixes and suffixes would be added to the same data structures (which makes](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [for some interesting generated text).](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

[To run multiple analyses, and keep them separate, we can encapsulate the state of each](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [analysis in an object. Here’s what that looks like:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[class Markov:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[def __init__(self):](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [self.suffix_map = {}](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [self.prefix = ()](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Next, we transform the functions into methods. For example, here’s](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [process_word](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[def process_word(self, word, order=2):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [if len(self.prefix) < order:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [self.prefix += (word,)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 18. Inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[try:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [self.suffix_map[self.prefix].append(word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [except KeyError:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [# if there is no entry for this prefix, make one](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [self.suffix_map[self.prefix] = [word]](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[self.prefix = shift(self.prefix, word)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Transforming a program like this—changing the design without changing the behavior—is](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [another example of refactoring (see Section 4.7).](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[This example suggests a development plan for designing objects and methods:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

1. [1. Start by writing functions that read and write global variables (when necessary).](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

2. [2. Once you get the program working, look for associations between global variables](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [and the functions that use them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

3. [3. Encapsulate related variables as attributes of an object.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

4. [4. Transform the associated functions into methods of the new class.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[As an exercise,](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [download my Markov code from](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [markov.py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, and follow the steps described above to encapsulate the global variables](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [as attributes of a new class called](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Markov](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Solution:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [markov2.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### 18.11 [Glossary](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[encode:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [To represent one set of values using another set of values by constructing a map-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [ping between them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[class attribute:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [An attribute associated with a class object.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Class attributes are defined](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [inside a class definition but outside any method.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[instance attribute:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [An attribute associated with an instance of a class.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

veneer: [A method or function that provides a different interface to another function with-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) out doing much computation.

inheritance: [The ability to define a new class that is a modified version of a previously](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [defined class.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[parent class:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The class from which a child class inherits.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[child class:](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py) [A new class created by inheriting from an existing class; also called a “sub-](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [class”.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[IS-A relationship:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [A relationship between a child class and its parent class.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[HAS-A relationship:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [A relationship between two classes where instances of one class con-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [tain references to instances of the other.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[dependency:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [A relationship between two classes where instances of one class use in-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [stances of the other class, but do not store them as attributes.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[18.12. Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[class diagram:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [A diagram that shows the classes in a program and the relationships be-](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [tween them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[multiplicity:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [A notation in a class diagram that shows, for a HAS-A relationship, how](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [many references there are to instances of another class.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[data encapsulation:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [A program development plan that involves a prototype using global](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [variables and a final version that makes the global variables into instance attributes.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

#### [18.12](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [Exercises](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Exercise 18.1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [For the following program, draw a UML class diagram that shows these classes and](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [the relationships among them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[class PingPongParent:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [pass](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[class Ping(PingPongParent):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [def __init__(self, pong):](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [self.pong = pong](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[class Pong(PingPongParent):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [def __init__(self, pings=None):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [if pings is None:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [self.pings = []](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [else:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [self.pings = pings](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project)

[def add_ping(self, ping):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [self.pings.append(ping)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[pong = Pong()](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [ping = Ping(pong)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [pong.add_ping(ping)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) Exercise 18.2. Write a Deck method called deal_hands that takes two parameters, the number of [hands and the number of cards per hand. It should create the appropriate number of Hand objects,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [deal the appropriate number of cards per hand, and return a list of Hands.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Exercise 18.3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [The following are the possible hands in poker, in increasing order of value and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [decreasing order of probability:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[pair:](https://thinkpython.com/code/analyze_book3.py) [two cards with the same rank](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[two pair:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [two pairs of cards with the same rank](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[three of a kind:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [three cards with the same rank](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[straight:](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [five cards with ranks in sequence (aces can be high or low, so](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Ace-2-3-4-5](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [is a straight](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and so is](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [10-Jack-Queen-King-Ace](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [, but](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [Queen-King-Ace-2-3](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [is not.)](https://thinkpython.com/code/metathesis.py)

[flush:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [five cards with the same suit](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[full house:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [three cards with one rank, two cards with another](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 18. Inheritance](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[four of a kind:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [four cards with the same rank](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

straight flush: [five cards in sequence (as defined above) and with the same suit](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[The goal of these exercises is to estimate the probability of drawing these various hands.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

1. [1. Download the following files from](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[Card.py](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [: A complete version of the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Card](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [Deck](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [and](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [Hand](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [classes in this chapter.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

[PokerHand.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [: An incomplete implementation of a class that represents a poker hand, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [some code that tests it.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

2. [2. If you run](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [PokerHand.py](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [, it deals seven 7-card poker hands and checks to see if any of them](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [contains a flush. Read this code carefully before you go on.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

3. [3. Add methods to](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [PokerHand.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) named [has_pair](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [has_twopair](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, etc. that return True or](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [False according to whether or not the hand meets the relevant criteria.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) Your code should [work correctly for “hands” that contain any number of cards (although 5 and 7 are the most](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [common sizes).](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames)

4. [4. Write a method named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [classify](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [that figures out the highest-value classification for a hand](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [and sets the](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [label](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [attribute accordingly. For example, a 7-card hand might contain a flush](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [and a pair; it should be labeled “flush”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py)

5. [5. When you are convinced that your classification methods are working, the next step is to esti-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [mate the probabilities of the various hands. Write a function in](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [PokerHand.py](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [that shuffles](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [a deck of cards, divides it into hands, classifies the hands, and counts the number of times](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [various classifications appear.](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

6. [6. Print a table of the classifications and their probabilities. Run your program with larger and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [larger numbers of hands until the output values converge to a reasonable degree of accu-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [racy. Compare your results to the values at](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [http: // en. wikipedia. org/ wiki/ Hand_](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [rankings](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Solution:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [https: // thinkpython. com/ code/ PokerHandSoln. py](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

## [Chapter 19](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [The Goodies](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[One of my goals for this book has been to teach you as little Python as possible. When](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [there were two ways to do something, I picked one and avoided mentioning the other. Or](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [sometimes I put the second one into an exercise.](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[Now I want to go back for some of the good bits that got left behind. Python provides a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [number of features that are not really necessary—you can write good code without them—](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [but with them you can sometimes write code that’s more concise, readable or efficient, and](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [sometimes all three.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

#### [19.1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Conditional expressions](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[We saw conditional statements in Section 5.4.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Conditional statements are often used to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [choose one of two values; for example:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[if x > 0:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [y = math.log(x)](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [else:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [y = float(](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [nan](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) ['](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[This statement checks whether](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) x is positive. If so, it computes math.log . If not, math.log would raise a ValueError. To avoid stopping the program, we generate a “NaN”, which is [a special floating-point value that represents “Not a Number”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[We can write this statement more concisely using a](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [conditional expression](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) :

[y = math.log(x) if x > 0 else float(](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [nan](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[You can almost read this line like English: “](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [gets log-](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [if](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [is greater than 0; otherwise it](http://gutenberg.org/) [gets NaN”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Recursive functions can sometimes be rewritten using conditional expressions. For exam-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [ple, here is a recursive version of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [factorial](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[def factorial(n):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [if n == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [else:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return n * factorial(n-1)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 19. The Goodies](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[We can rewrite it like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

def factorial(n): [return 1 if n == 0 else n * factorial(n-1)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Another use of conditional expressions is handling optional arguments. For example, here](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [is the init method from](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [GoodKangaroo](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [(see Exercise 17.2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[def __init__(self, name, contents=None):](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [self.name = name](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [if contents == None:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [contents = []](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [self.pouch_contents = contents](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[We can rewrite this one like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[def __init__(self, name, contents=None):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [self.name = name](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [self.pouch_contents = [] if contents == None else contents](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[In general, you can replace a conditional statement with a conditional expression if both](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [branches contain simple expressions that are either returned or assigned to the same vari-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) able.

#### [19.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [List comprehensions](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[In Section 10.7 we saw the map and filter patterns. For example, this function takes a list](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [of strings, maps the string method](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [capitalize](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [to the elements, and returns a new list of](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) strings:

[def capitalize_all(t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [res = []](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [for s in t:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [res.append(s.capitalize())](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [return res](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[We can write this more concisely using a](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [list comprehension](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[def capitalize_all(t):](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [return [s.capitalize() for s in t]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

The bracket operators indicate that we are constructing a new list. The expression inside [the brackets specifies the elements of the list, and the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [clause indicates what sequence](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [we are traversing.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[The syntax of a list comprehension is a little awkward because the loop variable,](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [s](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [in this](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [example, appears in the expression before we get to the definition.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[List comprehensions can also be used for filtering. For example, this function selects only](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [the elements of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [t](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [that are upper case, and returns a new list:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def only_upper(t):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [res = []](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [for s in t:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [if s.isupper():](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [res.append(s)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return res](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[19.3. Generator expressions](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[We can rewrite it using a list comprehension](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[def only_upper(t):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zipf's_law) [return [s for s in t if s.isupper()]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[List comprehensions are concise and easy to read, at least for simple expressions. And they](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [are usually faster than the equivalent for loops, sometimes much faster. So if you are mad](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [at me for not mentioning them earlier, I understand.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py)

[But, in my defense, list comprehensions are harder to debug because you can’t put a print](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [statement inside the loop. I suggest that you use them only if the computation is simple](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [enough that you are likely to get it right the first time. And for beginners that means never.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

#### [19.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [Generator expressions](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[Generator expressions](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [are similar to list comprehensions, but with parentheses instead of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [square brackets:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> g = (x**2 for x in range(5))](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [>>> g](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [<generator object <genexpr> at 0x7f4c45a786c0>](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[The result is a generator object that knows how to iterate through a sequence of values. But](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [unlike a list comprehension, it does not compute the values all at once; it waits to be asked.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [The built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [next](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [gets the next value from the generator:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[>>> next(g)](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[>>> next(g)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation)

[When you get to the end of the sequence,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [next](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [raises a StopIteration exception. You can](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [also use a](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [for](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [loop to iterate through the values:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> for val in g:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [...](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [print(val)](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[The generator object keeps track of where it is in the sequence, so the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) for loop picks up where [next](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [left off. Once the generator is exhausted, it continues to raise](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [StopIteration](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[>>> next(g)](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [StopIteration](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Generator expressions are often used with functions like](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [sum](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [max](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [, and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [min](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number)

[>>> sum(x**2 for x in range(5))](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

#### [19.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [any](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [all](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d)

[Python provides a built-in function,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [any](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, that takes a sequence of boolean values and re-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [turns](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [True](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [if any of the values are](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [True](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [. It works on lists:](https://thinkpython.com/code/typewriter.py)

[Chapter 19. The Goodies](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> any([False, False, True])](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[But it is often used with generator expressions:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[>>> any(letter ==](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [for letter in](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [monty](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) True

[That example isn’t very useful because it does the same thing as the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [in](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [operator. But we](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [could use](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [any](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [to rewrite some of the search functions we wrote in Section 9.3. For example,](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [we could write](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [avoids](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[def avoids(word, forbidden):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [return not any(letter in forbidden for letter in word)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[The function almost reads like English, “](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [word](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [avoids](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [forbidden](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [if there are not any forbid-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [den letters in](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [word](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [.”](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

Using [any](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [with a generator expression is efficient because it stops immediately if it finds a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [True](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [value, so it doesn’t have to evaluate the whole sequence.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Python provides another built-in function,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [all](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, that returns](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [True](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [if every element of the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [sequence is](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [. As an exercise, use](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [all](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [to re-write](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [uses_all](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [from Section 9.3.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

#### [19.5](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [Sets](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[In Section 13.6 I use dictionaries to find the words that appear in a document but not in a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [word list. The function I wrote takes](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [d1](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, which contains the words from the document as](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) keys, and [d2](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, which contains the list of words. It returns a dictionary that contains the keys](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) from d1 [that are not in](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [d2](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[def subtract(d1, d2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [res = dict()](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [for key in d1:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [if key not in d2:](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [res[key] = None](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [return res](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[In all of these dictionaries, the values are](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) None because we never use them. As a result, we waste some storage space.

[Python provides another built-in type, called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [set](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, that behaves like a collection of dic-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [tionary keys with no values. Adding elements to a set is fast; so is checking membership.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [And sets provide methods and operators to compute common set operations.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[For example, set subtraction is available as a method called](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [difference](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [or as an operator,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) - [. So we can rewrite](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [subtract](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [like this:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def subtract(d1, d2):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [return set(d1) - set(d2)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[The result is a set instead of a dictionary, but for operations like iteration, the behavior is](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [the same.](https://thinkpython.com/code/double.py)

[Some of the exercises in this book can be done concisely and efficiently with sets.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [For](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [example, here is a solution to](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [has_duplicates](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, from Exercise 10.7, that uses a dictionary:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[19.6. Counters](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def has_duplicates(t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [d = {}](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [for x in t:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [if x in d:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [d[x] = True](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [return False](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[When an element appears for the first time, it is added to the dictionary. If the same element](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [appears again, the function returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [True](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[Using sets, we can write the same function like this:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[def has_duplicates(t):](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [return len(set(t)) < len(t)](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[An element can only appear in a set once, so if an element in](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) t appears more than once, the [set will be smaller than](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [. If there are no duplicates, the set will be the same size as](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) t .

[We can also use sets to do some of the exercises in Chapter 9. For example, here’s a version](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [of](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [uses_only](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [with a loop:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def uses_only(word, available):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [for letter in word:](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [if letter not in available:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [return False](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [return True](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[uses_only](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [checks whether all letters in](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [word](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [are in](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [available](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [. We can rewrite it like this:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def uses_only(word, available):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return set(word) <= set(available)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [<=](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [operator checks whether one set is a subset of another, including the possibility that](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [they are equal, which is true if all the letters in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [word](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [appear in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [available](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[As an exercise, rewrite](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [avoids](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [using sets.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reducible.py)

#### [19.6](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Counters](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

A Counter is like a set, except that if an element appears more than once, the Counter [keeps track of how many times it appears. If you are familiar with the mathematical idea](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [of a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [multiset](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, a Counter is a natural way to represent a multiset.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Counter is defined in a standard module called](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [collections](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, so you have to import it. You](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [can initialize a Counter with a string, list, or anything else that supports iteration:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> from collections import Counter](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [>>> count = Counter(](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [parrot](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [>>> count](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [Counter({](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [r](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [: 2,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [t](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [: 1,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [o](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [: 1,](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [p](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [: 1,](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [a](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) ['](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [: 1})](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict)

[Counters behave like dictionaries in many ways; they map from each key to the number of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [times it appears. As in dictionaries, the keys have to be hashable.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Unlike dictionaries, Counters don’t raise an exception if you access an element that doesn’t](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [appear. Instead, they return 0:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 19. The Goodies](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> count[](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) []](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[We can use Counters to rewrite](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is_anagram](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [from Exercise 10.6:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[def is_anagram(word1, word2):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [return Counter(word1) == Counter(word2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[If two words are anagrams, they contain the same letters with the same counts, so their](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Counters are equivalent.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Counters provide methods and operators to perform set-like operations, including ad-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [dition, subtraction, union and intersection.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [And they provide an often-useful method,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [most_common](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [, which returns a list of value-frequency pairs, sorted from most common to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [least:](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py)

[>>> count = Counter(](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [parrot](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ' ) [>>> for val, freq in count.most_common(3):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [...](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [print(val, freq)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [r 2](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) p 1 a 1

#### [19.7](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov2.py) [defaultdict](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[The](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [collections](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [module also provides](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [defaultdict](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, which is like a dictionary except that](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [if you access a key that doesn’t exist, it can generate a new value on the fly.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[When you create a defaultdict, you provide a function that’s used to create new values. A](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [function used to create objects is sometimes called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [factory](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [. The built-in functions that](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [create lists, sets, and other types can be used as factories:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> from collections import defaultdict](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [>>> d = defaultdict(list)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Notice that the argument is](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) list , which is a class object, not [list()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [, which is a new list.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [The function you provide doesn’t get called unless you access a key that doesn’t exist.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

[>>> t = d[](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [new key](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ] >>> t []

[The new list, which we’re calling](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [t](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, is also added to the dictionary. So if we modify](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [t](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [, the](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [change appears in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[>>> t.append(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [new value](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) >>> d [defaultdict(<class](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [list](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [>, {](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [new key](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [: [](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [new value](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) []})](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[If you are making a dictionary of lists, you can often write simpler code using](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [defaultdict](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [In my solution to Exercise 12.2, which you can get from](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [https://thinkpython.com/code/](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [anagram_sets.py](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [, I make a dictionary that maps from a sorted string of letters to the list of](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [words that can be spelled with those letters. For example,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [’opst’](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [maps to the list](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [[’opts’,](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [’post’, ’pots’, ’spot’, ’stop’, ’tops’]](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py)

[Here’s the original code:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[19.8. Named tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def all_anagrams(filename):](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [d = {}](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [for line in open(filename):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [word = line.strip().lower()](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [t = signature(word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [if t not in d:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [d[t] = [word]](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [else:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [d[t].append(word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [return d](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[This can be simplified using](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [setdefault](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [, which you might have used in Exercise 11.2:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[def all_anagrams(filename):](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [d = {}](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [for line in open(filename):](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [word = line.strip().lower()](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [t = signature(word)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [d.setdefault(t, []).append(word)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return d](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[This solution has the drawback that it makes a new list every time, regardless of whether](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [it is needed. For lists, that’s no big deal, but if the factory function is complicated, it might](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [be.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[We can avoid this problem and simplify the code using a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [defaultdict](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[def all_anagrams(filename):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [d = defaultdict(list)](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [for line in open(filename):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [word = line.strip().lower()](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [t = signature(word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [d[t].append(word)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return d](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[My solution to Exercise 18.3, which you can download from](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [https://thinkpython.com/](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [code/PokerHandSoln.py](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [, uses](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [setdefault](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [in the function](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [has_straightflush](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [. This solu-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [tion has the drawback of creating a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [Hand](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [object every time through the loop, whether it is](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [needed or not. As an exercise, rewrite it using a defaultdict.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

#### [19.8](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [Named tuples](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Many simple objects are basically collections of related values.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [For example, the Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [object defined in Chapter 15 contains two numbers,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [x](http://gutenberg.org/) [and](http://gutenberg.org/) [y](http://gutenberg.org/) [. When you define a class like](http://gutenberg.org/) [this, you usually start with an init method and a str method:](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[class Point:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[def __init__(self, x=0, y=0):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [self.x = x](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [self.y = y](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[def __str__(self):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [return](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [(%g, %g)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) ['](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [% (self.x, self.y)](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 19. The Goodies](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[This is a lot of code to convey a small amount of information.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [Python provides a more](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [concise way to say the same thing:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[from collections import namedtuple](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Point = namedtuple(](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [, [](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [,](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [])](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[The first argument is the name of the class you want to create. The second is a list of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [attributes Point objects should have, as strings. The return value from](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [namedtuple](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [is a class](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [object:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[>>> Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) <class ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [__main__.Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py)

[Point](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [automatically provides methods like](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [__init__](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [__str__](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [so you don’t have to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [write them.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[To create a Point object, you use the Point class as a function:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[>>> p = Point(1, 2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [>>> p](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [Point(x=1, y=2)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[The init method assigns the arguments to attributes using the names you provided. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [str method prints a representation of the Point object and its attributes.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[You can access the elements of the named tuple by name:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[>>> p.x, p.y](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [(1, 2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py)

[But you can also treat a named tuple as a tuple:](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[>>> p[0], p[1]](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) (1, 2)

[>>> x, y = p](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [>>> x, y](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [(1, 2)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Named tuples provide a quick way to define simple classes. The drawback is that simple](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [classes don’t always stay simple. You might decide later that you want to add methods](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [to a named tuple. In that case, you could define a new class that inherits from the named](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) tuple:

[class Pointier(Point):](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [# add more methods here](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Or you could switch to a conventional class definition.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

#### 19.9 [Gathering keyword args](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[In Section 12.4, we saw how to write a function that gathers its arguments into a tuple:](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[def printall(*args):](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [print(args)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[You can call this function with any number of positional arguments (that is, arguments that](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [don’t have keywords):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[19.10. Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[>>> printall(1, 2.0,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [(1, 2.0,](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[But the](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [*](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [operator doesn’t gather keyword arguments:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[>>> printall(1, 2.0, third=](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [TypeError: printall() got an unexpected keyword argument](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [third](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) ['](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[To gather keyword arguments, you can use the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [**](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set) [operator:](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

[def printall(*args, **kwargs):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [print(args, kwargs)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[You can call the keyword gathering parameter anything you want, but](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [kwargs](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [is a common](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [choice. The result is a dictionary that maps from keywords to values:](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[>>> printall(1, 2.0, third=](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) ' ' ) [(1, 2.0) {](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) ['](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [third](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) ['](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [}](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[If you have a dictionary of keywords and values, you can use the scatter operator,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [**](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [to](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [call a function:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[>>> d = dict(x=1, y=2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [>>> Point(**d)](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [Point(x=1, y=2)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Without the scatter operator, the function would treat](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [as a single positional argument, so](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1_soln.py) [it would assign](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [d](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [to](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [x](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [and complain because there’s nothing to assign to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [y](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py)

[>>> d = dict(x=1, y=2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [>>> Point(d)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Traceback (most recent call last):](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [TypeError: __new__() missing 1 required positional argument:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [y](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) ['](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[When you are working with functions that have a large number of parameters, it is often](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [useful to create and pass around dictionaries that specify frequently used options.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

#### 19.10 Glossary

[conditional expression:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [An expression that has one of two values, depending on a condi-](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [tion.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

[list comprehension:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [An expression with a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [loop in square brackets that yields a new](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [list.](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pdb.html)

[generator expression:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [An expression with a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [loop in parentheses that yields a genera-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [tor object.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[multiset:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [A mathematical entity that represents a mapping between the elements of a set](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and the number of times they appear.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[factory:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [A function, usually passed as a parameter, used to create objects.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Chapter 19. The Goodies](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [19.11](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Exercises](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Exercise 19.1.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [The following is a function that computes the binomial coefficient recursively.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[def binomial_coeff(n, k):](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) ["""Compute the binomial coefficient "n choose k".](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[n: number of trials](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [k: number of successes](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[returns: int](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) ["""](https://thinkpython.com/code/structshape.py) [if k == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [return 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [if n == 0:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [return 0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[res = binomial_coeff(n-1, k) + binomial_coeff(n-1, k-1)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [return res](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[Rewrite the body of the function using nested conditional expressions.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[One note: this function is not very efficient because it ends up computing the same values over and](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [over. You could make it more efficient by memoizing (see Section 11.6). But you will find that it’s](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [harder to memoize if you write it using conditional expressions.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

## [Appendix A](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

[When you are debugging, you should distinguish among different kinds of errors in order](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [to track them down more quickly:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

- [• Syntax errors are discovered by the interpreter when it is translating the source code](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [into byte code. They indicate that there is something wrong with the structure of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [program. Example: Omitting the colon at the end of a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [def](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1.py) [statement generates the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [somewhat redundant message](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [SyntaxError:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [invalid syntax](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py)

- [• Runtime errors are produced by the interpreter if something goes wrong while the](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [program is running. Most runtime error messages include information about where](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the error occurred and what functions were executing. Example: An infinite recur-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [sion eventually causes the runtime error “maximum recursion depth exceeded”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• Semantic errors are problems with a program that runs without producing error mes-](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [sages but doesn’t do the right thing. Example: An expression may not be evaluated](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [in the order you expect, yielding an incorrect result.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[The first step in debugging is to figure out which kind of error you are dealing with. Al-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) though the following sections are organized by error type, some techniques are applicable [in more than one situation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

#### [A.1](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Syntax errors](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Syntax errors are usually easy to fix once you figure out what they are.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [Unfortunately,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [the error messages are often not helpful. The most common messages are](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [SyntaxError:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [invalid syntax](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [SyntaxError:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [invalid token](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [, neither of which is very informa-](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [tive.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[On the other hand, the message does tell you where in the program the problem occurred.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Actually, it tells you where Python noticed a problem, which is not necessarily where the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [error is.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Sometimes the error is prior to the location of the error message, often on the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [preceding line.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Appendix A. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[If you are building the program incrementally, you should have a good idea about where](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [the error is. It will be in the last line you added.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[If you are copying code from a book, start by comparing your code to the book’s code](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [very carefully. Check every character. At the same time, remember that the book might be](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [wrong, so if you see something that looks like a syntax error, it might be.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Here are some ways to avoid the most common syntax errors:](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

1. [1. Make sure you are not using a Python keyword for a variable name.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

2. [2. Check that you have a colon at the end of the header of every compound statement,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [including](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [for](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [while](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [if](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [, and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [def](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [statements.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

3. [3. Make sure that any strings in the code have matching quotation marks. Make sure](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [that all quotation marks are “straight quotes”, not “curly quotes”.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

4. [4. If you have multiline strings with triple quotes (single or double), make sure you](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [have terminated the string properly. An unterminated string may cause an](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [invalid](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [token](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [error at the end of your program, or it may treat the following part of the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [program as a string until it comes to the next string. In the second case, it might not](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [produce an error message at all!](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

5. [5. An unclosed opening operator—](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [{](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, or](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [[](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [—makes Python continue with the next line](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [as part of the current statement. Generally, an error occurs almost immediately in the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [next line.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

6. [6. Check for the classic](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [instead of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [==](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [inside a conditional.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

7. [7. Check the indentation to make sure it lines up the way it is supposed to.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Python](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [can handle space and tabs, but if you mix them it can cause problems. The best way](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to avoid this problem is to use a text editor that knows about Python and generates](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [consistent indentation.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

8. [8. If you have non-ASCII characters in the code (including strings and comments), that](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [might cause a problem, although Python 3 usually handles non-ASCII characters. Be](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [careful if you paste in text from a web page or other source.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[If nothing works, move on to the next section...](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

###### A.1.1 [I keep making changes and it makes no difference.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[If the interpreter says there is an error and you don’t see it, that might be because you and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [the interpreter are not looking at the same code. Check your programming environment to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [make sure that the program you are editing is the one Python is trying to run.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[If you are not sure, try putting an obvious and deliberate syntax error at the beginning of](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [the program. Now run it again.
