If the interpreter doesn’t find the new error, you are not](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [running the new code.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[There are a few likely culprits:](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

- [• You edited the file and forgot to save the changes before running it again.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [Some](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [programming environments do this for you, but some don’t.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[A.2. Runtime errors](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

- [• You changed the name of the file, but you are still running the old name.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

- [• Something in your development environment is configured incorrectly.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

- [• If you are writing a module and using](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [import](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, make sure you don’t give your module](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [the same name as one of the standard Python modules.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

- [• If you are using](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [import](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [to read a module, remember that you have to restart the](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [interpreter or use](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [reload](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [to read a modified file. If you import the module again, it](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [doesn’t do anything.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#types-set)

[If you get stuck and you can’t figure out what is going on, one approach is to start again](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [with a new program like “Hello, World!”, and make sure you can get a known program to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [run. Then gradually add the pieces of the original program to the new one.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

#### [A.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [Runtime errors](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[Once your program is syntactically correct, Python can read it and at least start running it.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [What could possibly go wrong?](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

###### [A.2.1](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [My program does absolutely nothing.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[This problem is most common when your file consists of functions and classes but does](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [not actually invoke a function to start execution. This may be intentional if you only plan](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [to import this module to supply classes and functions.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[If it is not intentional, make sure there is a function call in the program, and make sure the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [flow of execution reaches it (see “Flow of Execution” below).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

###### [A.2.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [My program hangs.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[If a program stops and seems to be doing nothing, it is “hanging”. Often that means that it](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [is caught in an infinite loop or infinite recursion.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

- [• If there is a particular loop that you suspect is the problem, add a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [print](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [statement](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [immediately before the loop that says “entering the loop” and another immediately](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) after that says “exiting the loop”.

[Run the program.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [If you get the first message and not the second, you’ve got an](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [infinite loop. Go to the “Infinite Loop” section below.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

- [• Most of the time, an infinite recursion will cause the program to run for a while and](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [then produce a “RuntimeError: Maximum recursion depth exceeded” error. If that](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [happens, go to the “Infinite Recursion” section below.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[If you are not getting this error but you suspect there is a problem with a recursive](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [method or function, you can still use the techniques in the “Infinite Recursion” sec-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [tion.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

- [• If neither of those steps works, start testing other loops and other recursive functions](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [and methods.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

- [• If that doesn’t work, then it is possible that you don’t understand the flow of execu-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [tion in your program. Go to the “Flow of Execution” section below.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Appendix A. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Infinite Loop](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[If you think you have an infinite loop and you think you know what loop is causing the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [problem, add a](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [statement at the end of the loop that prints the values of the variables](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [in the condition and the value of the condition.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

For example:

[while x > 0 and y < 0 :](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [# do something to x](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt) [# do something to y](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

| print( | '   | x:  | '   | , x) |
| ------ | --- | --- | --- | ---- |
| print( | '   | y:  | '   | , y) |

[Now when you run the program, you will see three lines of output for each time through](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [the loop. The last time through the loop, the condition should be](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [False](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [. If the loop keeps](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [going, you will be able to see the values of](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [x](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [y](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, and you might figure out why they are](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [not being updated correctly.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Infinite Recursion](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Most of the time, infinite recursion causes the program to run for a while and then produce](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [Maximum recursion depth exceeded](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [error.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[If you suspect that a function is causing an infinite recursion, make sure that there is a base](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [case. There should be some condition that causes the function to return without making a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [recursive invocation. If not, you need to rethink the algorithm and identify a base case.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[If there is a base case but the program doesn’t seem to be reaching it, add a](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [state-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [ment at the beginning of the function that prints the parameters. Now when you run the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [program, you will see a few lines of output every time the function is invoked, and you](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [will see the parameter values. If the parameters are not moving toward the base case, you](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [will get some ideas about why not.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

Flow of Execution

[If you are not sure how the flow of execution is moving through your program, add](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [print](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [statements to the beginning of each function with a message like “entering function](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [foo](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [”,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) where [foo](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is the name of the function.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Now when you run the program, it will print a trace of each function as it is invoked.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

###### A.2.3 [When I run the program I get an exception.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[If something goes wrong during runtime, Python prints a message that includes the name](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [of the exception, the line of the program where the problem occurred, and a traceback.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[The traceback identifies the function that is currently running, and then the function that](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [called it, and then the function that called](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [that](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [, and so on.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [In other words, it traces the](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[A.2. Runtime errors](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[sequence of function calls that got you to where you are, including the line number in your](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [file where each call occurred.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The first step is to examine the place in the program where the error occurred and see if](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [you can figure out what happened. These are some of the most common runtime errors:](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

NameError: [You are trying to use a variable that doesn’t exist in the current environment.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [Check if the name is spelled right, or at least consistently. And remember that local](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [variables are local; you cannot refer to them from outside the function where they are](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [defined.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[TypeError:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [There are several possible causes:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

- [• You are trying to use a value improperly. Example: indexing a string, list, or](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [tuple with something other than an integer.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

- [• There is a mismatch between the items in a format string and the items passed](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [for conversion. This can happen if either the number of items does not match or](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [an invalid conversion is called for.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

- [• You are passing the wrong number of arguments to a function. For methods,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [look at the method definition and check that the first parameter is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [self](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [. Then](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [look at the method invocation; make sure you are invoking the method on an](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [object with the right type and providing the other arguments correctly.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[KeyError:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [You are trying to access an element of a dictionary using a key that the dictio-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [nary does not contain. If the keys are strings, remember that capitalization matters.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[AttributeError:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [You are trying to access an attribute or method that does not exist. Check](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [the spelling! You can use the built-in function](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [vars](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to list the attributes that do exist.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[If an AttributeError indicates that an object has](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [NoneType](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [, that means that it is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [So the problem is not the attribute name, but the object.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The reason the object is none might be that you forgot to return a value from a func-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [tion; if you get to the end of a function without hitting a](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [statement, it returns](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [None](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [. Another common cause is using the result from a list method, like](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [sort](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [, that](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [returns](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [None](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

IndexError: The index you are using to access a list, string, or tuple is greater than its [length minus one. Immediately before the site of the error, add a](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [print](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [statement to](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [display the value of the index and the length of the array. Is the array the right size?](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [Is the index the right value?](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[The Python debugger (](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [pdb](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [) is useful for tracking down exceptions because it allows you to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [examine the state of the program immediately before the error. You can read about](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [pdb](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [at](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [https://docs.python.org/3/library/pdb.html](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

###### [A.2.4](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [I added so many](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [statements I get inundated with output.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[One of the problems with using](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/pronounce.py) [statements for debugging is that you can end up](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [buried in output.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [There are two ways to proceed: simplify the output or simplify the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [program.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Appendix A. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[To simplify the output, you can remove or comment out](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [statements that aren’t help-](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [ing, or combine them, or format the output so it is easier to understand.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[To simplify the program, there are several things you can do. First, scale down the problem](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [the program is working on. For example, if you are searching a list, search a](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) small list. If [the program takes input from the user, give it the simplest input that causes the problem.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Second, clean up the program. Remove dead code and reorganize the program to make](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [it as easy to read as possible. For example, if you suspect that the problem is in a deeply](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [nested part of the program, try rewriting that part with simpler structure. If you suspect a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [large function, try splitting it into smaller functions and testing them separately.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Often the process of finding the minimal test case leads you to the bug. If you find that](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [a program works in one situation but not in another, that gives you a clue about what is](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [going on.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Similarly, rewriting a piece of code can help you find subtle bugs. If you make a change](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [that you think shouldn’t affect the program, and it does, that can tip you off.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

#### [A.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [Semantic errors](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[In some ways, semantic errors are the hardest to debug, because the interpreter provides](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [no information about what is wrong. Only you know what the program is supposed to do.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The first step is to make a connection between the program text and the behavior you are](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [seeing. You need a hypothesis about what the program is actually doing. One of the things](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [that makes that hard is that computers run so fast.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[You will often wish that you could slow the program down to human speed, and with](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [some debuggers you can. But the time it takes to insert a few well-placed](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [print](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [statements](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [is often short compared to setting up the debugger, inserting and removing breakpoints,](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [and “stepping” the program to where the error is occurring.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

###### A.3.1 My program doesn’t work.

[You should ask yourself these questions:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

- [• Is there something the program was supposed to do but which doesn’t seem to be](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [happening? Find the section of the code that performs that function and make sure](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [it is executing when you think it should.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

- [• Is something happening that shouldn’t? Find code in your program that performs](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [that function and see if it is executing when it shouldn’t.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

- [• Is a section of code producing an effect that is not what you expected? Make sure that](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [you understand the code in question, especially if it involves functions or methods in](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [other Python modules. Read the documentation for the functions you call. Try them](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [out by writing simple test cases and checking the results.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[A.3. Semantic errors](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[In order to program, you need a mental model of how programs work.](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [If you write a](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [program that doesn’t do what you expect, often the problem is not in the program; it’s in](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [your mental model.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[The best way to correct your mental model is to break the program into its components](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [(usually the functions and methods) and test each component independently. Once you](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [find the discrepancy between your model and reality, you can solve the problem.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[Of course, you should be building and testing components as you develop the program.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [If you encounter a problem, there should be only a small amount of new code that is not](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [known to be correct.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

###### [A.3.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [I’ve got a big hairy expression and it doesn’t do what I expect.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[Writing complex expressions is fine as long as they are readable, but they can be hard to](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [debug. It is often a good idea to break a complex expression into a series of assignments to](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [temporary variables.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[For example:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[self.hands[i].addCard(self.hands[self.findNeighbor(i)].popCard())](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[This can be rewritten as:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[neighbor = self.findNeighbor(i)](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [pickedCard = self.hands[neighbor].popCard()](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [self.hands[i].addCard(pickedCard)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The explicit version is easier to read because the variable names provide additional docu-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [mentation, and it is easier to debug because you can check the types of the intermediate](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [variables and display their values.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py)

[Another problem that can occur with big expressions is that the order of evaluation may](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[x](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[not be what you expect. For example, if you are translating the expression](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[π](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[into Python,](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [you might write:](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[y = x / 2 * math.pi](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[That is not correct because multiplication and division have the same precedence and are](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [evaluated from left to right. So this expression computes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) x π /2.

A good way to debug expressions is to add parentheses to make the order of evaluation explicit:

[y = x / (2 * math.pi)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Whenever you are not sure of the order of evaluation, use parentheses. Not only will the](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [program be correct (in the sense of doing what you intended), it will also be more readable](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [for other people who haven’t memorized the order of operations.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

###### [A.3.3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [I’ve got a function that doesn’t return what I expect.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[If you have a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [return](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [statement with a complex expression, you don’t have a chance to](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [print the result before returning. Again, you can use a temporary variable. For example,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [instead of:](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[return self.hands[i].removeMatches()](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Appendix A. Debugging](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[you could write:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[count = self.hands[i].removeMatches()](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [return count](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[Now you have the opportunity to display the value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [count](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [before returning.](https://thinkpython.com/code)

###### [A.3.4](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [I’m really, really stuck and I need help.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[First, try getting away from the computer for a few minutes. Computers emit waves that](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [affect the brain, causing these symptoms:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

- [• Frustration and rage.](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

- [• Superstitious beliefs (“the computer hates me”) and magical thinking (“the program](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [only works when I wear my hat backward”).](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

- [• Random walk programming (the attempt to program by writing every possible pro-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [gram and choosing the one that does the right thing).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[If you find yourself suffering from any of these symptoms, get up and go for a walk. When](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [you are calm, think about the program. What is it doing? What are some possible causes](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [of that behavior? When was the last time you had a working program, and what did you](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [do next?](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py)

[Sometimes it just takes time to find a bug.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [I often find bugs when I am away from the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [computer and let my mind wander. Some of the best places to find bugs are trains, showers,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [and in bed, just before you fall asleep.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

###### [A.3.5](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hand_rankings) [No, I really need help.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[It happens. Even the best programmers occasionally get stuck. Sometimes you work on a](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [program so long that you can’t see the error. You need a fresh pair of eyes.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Before you bring someone else in, make sure you are prepared. Your program should be as](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [simple as possible, and you should be working on the smallest input that causes the error.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [You should have](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [print](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) statements in the appropriate places (and the output they produce should be comprehensible). You should understand the problem well enough to describe it concisely.

[When you bring someone in to help, be sure to give them the information they need:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

- [• If there is an error message, what is it and what part of the program does it indicate?](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

- [• What was the last thing you did before this error occurred? What were the last lines](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [of code that you wrote, or what is the new test case that fails?](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

- [• What have you tried so far, and what have you learned?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[When you find the bug, take a second to think about what you could have done to find it](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [faster. Next time you see something similar, you will be able to find the bug more quickly.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[Remember, the goal is not just to make the program work. The goal is to learn how to make](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [the program work.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

## [Appendix B](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

# [Analysis of Algorithms](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[This appendix is an edited excerpt from](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Think Complexity](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, by Allen B. Downey,](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [also published by O’Reilly Media (2012). When you are done with this book,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [you might want to move on to that one.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[Analysis of algorithms](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [is a branch of computer science that studies the performance of](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [algorithms, especially their run time and space requirements. See](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [http://en.wikipedia.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [org/wiki/Analysis_of_algorithms](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The practical goal of algorithm analysis is to predict the performance of different algo-](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [rithms in order to guide design decisions.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[During the 2008 United States Presidential Campaign, candidate Barack Obama was asked](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [to perform an impromptu analysis when he visited Google. Chief executive Eric Schmidt](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [jokingly asked him for “the most efficient way to sort a million 32-bit integers.” Obama](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [had apparently been tipped off, because he quickly replied, “I think the bubble sort would](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [be the wrong way to go.” See](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=k4RRi_ntQc8](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[This is true:](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [bubble sort is conceptually simple but slow for large datasets.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [The an-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [swer Schmidt was probably looking for is “radix sort” (](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [Radix_sort](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) .

[The goal of algorithm analysis is to make meaningful comparisons between algorithms,](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [but there are some problems:](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

- [• The relative performance of the algorithms might depend on characteristics of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [hardware, so one algorithm might be faster on Machine A, another on Machine B.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [The general solution to this problem is to specify a](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [machine model](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [and analyze the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [number of steps, or operations, an algorithm requires under a given model.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

- [• Relative performance might depend on the details of the dataset. For example, some](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [sorting algorithms run faster if the data are already partially sorted; other algorithms](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[But if you get a question like this in an interview, I think a better answer is, “The fastest way to sort a million](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [integers is to use whatever sort function is provided by the language I’m using. Its performance is good enough](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [for the vast majority of applications, but if it turned out that my application was too slow, I would use a profiler](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [to see where the time was being spent. If it looked like a faster sort algorithm would have a significant effect on](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [performance, then I would look around for a good implementation of radix sort.”](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Appendix B. Analysis of Algorithms](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[run slower in this case. A common way to avoid this problem is to analyze the](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [worst](https://thinkpython.com/code/homophone.py) [case](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [scenario. It is sometimes useful to analyze average case performance, but that’s](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [usually harder, and it might not be obvious what set of cases to average over.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

- [• Relative performance also depends on the size of the problem. A sorting algorithm](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [that is fast for small lists might be slow for long lists.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [The usual solution to this](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [problem is to express run time (or number of operations) as a function of problem](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [size, and group functions into categories depending on how quickly they grow as](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [problem size increases.](https://thinkpython.com/code/emma.txt)

[The good thing about this kind of comparison is that it lends itself to simple classification](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [of algorithms. For example, if I know that the run time of Algorithm A tends to be pro-](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [portional to the size of the input,](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py) [, and Algorithm B tends to be proportional to](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [, then I](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [expect A to be faster than B, at least for large values of](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[This kind of analysis comes with some caveats, but we’ll get to that later.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

#### B.1 [Order of growth](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py)

[Suppose you have analyzed two algorithms and expressed their run times in terms of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [size of the input: Algorithm A takes 100](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [1 steps to solve a problem with size](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py) [; Algo-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [rithm B takes](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [1 steps.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[The following table shows the run time of these algorithms for different problem sizes:](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

| Input  | Run time of | Run time of |
| ------ | ----------- | ----------- |
| size   | Algorithm A | Algorithm B |
| 1 000  | 100 001     | 1 001 001   |
| 10 000 | 1 000 001   | 100 010 001 |

[At](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [=](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [10, Algorithm A looks pretty bad; it takes almost 10 times longer than Algorithm](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [B. But for](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [=](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [100 they are about the same, and for larger values A is much better.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

The fundamental reason is that for large values of n , any function that contains an n term [will grow faster than a function whose leading term is](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [leading term](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [is the term with](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [the highest exponent.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[For Algorithm A, the leading term has a large coefficient, 100, which is why B does better](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [than A for small](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [. But regardless of the coefficients, there will always be some value of](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) where an [>](https://thinkpython.com/code/sed.py) [bn](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pdb.html) [, for any values of](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [a](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

[The same argument applies to the non-leading terms. Even if the run time of Algorithm A](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) were n [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [1000000, it would still be better than Algorithm B for sufficiently large](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py)

[In general, we expect an algorithm with a smaller leading term to be a better algorithm for](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [large problems, but for smaller problems, there may be a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [crossover point](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [where another](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [algorithm is better. The location of the crossover point depends on the details of the algo-](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [rithms, the inputs, and the hardware, so it is usually ignored for purposes of algorithmic](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [analysis. But that doesn’t mean you can forget about it.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[B.1. Order of growth](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[If two algorithms have the same leading order term, it is hard to say which is better; again,](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [the answer depends on the details. So for algorithmic analysis, functions with the same](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [leading term are considered equivalent, even if they have different coefficients.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral)

[An](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [order of growth](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [is a set of functions whose growth behavior is considered equivalent.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [For example, 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [, 100](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [1 belong to the same order of growth, which is written](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) O ( n ) in [Big-Oh notation](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [and often called](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [linear](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [because every function in the set grows linearly](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [with](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [n](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[All functions with the leading term](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [belong to](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [; they are called](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [quadratic](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[The following table shows some of the orders of growth that appear most commonly in](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [algorithmic analysis, in increasing order of badness.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[Order of](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py) [Name](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [growth](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [O](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [constant](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [log](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[b](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[n](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [logarithmic (for any](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [b](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [O](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [(](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [)](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [linear](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [O](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [log](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[b](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [linearithmic](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [quadratic](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [cubic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [O](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[n](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [exponential (for any](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [c](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[For the logarithmic terms, the base of the logarithm doesn’t matter; changing bases is the](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [equivalent of multiplying by a constant, which doesn’t change the order of growth. Sim-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [ilarly, all exponential functions belong to the same order of growth regardless of the base](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [of the exponent. Exponential functions grow very quickly, so exponential algorithms are](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [only useful for small problems.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [Exercise B.1.](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [Read the Wikipedia page on Big-Oh notation at](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [http: // en. wikipedia. org/](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [wiki/ Big_ O_ notation](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [and answer the following questions:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

1. [1. What is the order of growth of n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [+](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [n](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [? What about](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk2.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk2.py) [n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [? What about n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) + [n](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [?](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

2. [2. What is the order of growth of](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) ( n + [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [·](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [? Before you start multiplying, remember](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [that you only need the leading term.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

3. [3. If f is in O](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) ( g ) , for some unspecified function g, what can we say about a f + b, where a and b are constants?

4. If f [and f](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [are in O](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [g](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [, what can we say about f](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [f](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [?](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

5. [5. If f](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [is in O](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [g](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and f](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is in O](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [h](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, what can we say about f](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [+](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [f](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [?](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods)

6. [6. If f](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is in O](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [g](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [and f](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [is O](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [h](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, what can we say about f](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [·](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [f](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [?](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[Programmers who care about performance often find this kind of analysis hard to swal-](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [low. They have a point: sometimes the coefficients and the non-leading terms make a real](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [difference.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [Sometimes the details of the hardware, the programming language, and the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [characteristics of the input make a big difference. And for small problems, order of growth](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is irrelevant.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[But if you keep those caveats in mind, algorithmic analysis is a useful tool. At least for](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [large problems, the “better” algorithm is usually better, and sometimes it is](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [much](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [better.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [The difference between two algorithms with the same order of growth is usually a constant](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [factor, but the difference between a good algorithm and a bad algorithm is unbounded!](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Appendix B. Analysis of Algorithms](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [B.2](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Analysis of basic Python operations](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[In Python, most arithmetic operations are constant time; multiplication usually takes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [longer than addition and subtraction, and division takes even longer, but these run times](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [don’t depend on the magnitude of the operands. Very large integers are an exception; in](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [that case the run time increases with the number of digits.](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py)

[Indexing operations—reading or writing elements in a sequence or dictionary—are also](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [constant time, regardless of the size of the data structure.](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

A for [loop that traverses a sequence or dictionary is usually linear, as long as all of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [operations in the body of the loop are constant time. For example, adding up the elements](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [of a list is linear:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[total = 0](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [for x in t:](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [total += x](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

[The built-in function](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [sum](http://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#specialnames) [is also linear because it does the same thing, but it tends to be](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [faster because it is a more efficient implementation; in the language of algorithmic analysis,](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [it has a smaller leading coefficient.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[As a rule of thumb, if the body of a loop is in](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py)

[a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[)](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [then the whole loop is in](https://thinkpython.com/code/draw.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py)

[a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [+](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [. The](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time1.py) [exception is if you can show that the loop exits after a constant number of iterations. If a](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [loop runs](https://thinkpython.com/code/walk.py) [k](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [times regardless of](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [, then the loop is in](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[a](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [, even for large](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [k](https://thinkpython.com/code/Point1.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/most_frequent.py)

[Multiplying by](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [k](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [doesn’t change the order of growth, but neither does dividing. So if the](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [body of a loop is in](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[a](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py)

[)](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [and it runs](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [n](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [/](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [k](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [times, the loop is in](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [O](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [(](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [n](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[a](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [+](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, even for large](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [k](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/birthday.py)

[Most string and tuple operations are linear, except indexing and](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [len](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [, which are constant](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [time. The built-in functions](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [min](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [and](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) max [are linear. The run-time of a slice operation is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [proportional to the length of the output, but independent of the size of the input.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[String concatenation is linear; the run time depends on the sum of the lengths of the](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [operands.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[All string methods are linear, but if the lengths of the strings are bounded by a constant—](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [for example, operations on single characters—they are considered constant time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [The](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [string method](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [join](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [is linear; the run time depends on the total length of the strings.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

Most list methods are linear, but there are some exceptions:

- [• Adding an element to the end of a list is constant time on average; when it runs](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [out of room it occasionally gets copied to a bigger location, but the total time for](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [n](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [operations is](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [, so the average time for each operation is](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

- [• Removing an element from the end of a list is constant time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py)

- [• Sorting is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [O](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [log](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Most dictionary operations and methods are constant time, but there are some exceptions:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

- [• The run time of](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) update [is proportional to the size of the dictionary passed as a pa-](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [rameter, not the dictionary being updated.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

- [keys](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [,](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [values](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [and](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [items](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [are constant time because they return iterators. But if you](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [loop through the iterators, the loop will be linear.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[B.3. Analysis of search algorithms](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[The performance of dictionaries is one of the minor miracles of computer science. We will](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py) [see how they work in Section B.4.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Exercise B.2.](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [Read the Wikipedia page on sorting algorithms at](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [http: // en. wikipedia. org/](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [wiki/ Sorting_ algorithm](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py) [and answer the following questions:](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

1. [1. What is a “comparison sort?” What is the best worst-case order of growth for a comparison](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [sort? What is the best worst-case order of growth for any sort algorithm?](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

2. [2. What is the order of growth of bubble sort, and why does Barack Obama think it is “the wrong](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py) [way to go?”](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

3. [3. What is the order of growth of radix sort? What preconditions do we need to use it?](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

4. [4. What is a stable sort and why might it matter in practice?](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

5. [5. What is the worst sorting algorithm (that has a name)?](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py)

6. [6. What sort algorithm does the C library use? What sort algorithm does Python use? Are these](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [algorithms stable? You might have to Google around to find these answers.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

7. [7. Many of the non-comparison sorts are linear, so why does Python use an O](https://thinkpython.com/code/has_duplicates.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [log](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [compar-](https://thinkpython.com/code/Map.py) [ison sort?](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py)

#### [B.3](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [Analysis of search algorithms](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[A](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [search](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [is an algorithm that takes a collection and a target item and determines whether](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [the target is in the collection, often returning the index of the target.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[The simplest search algorithm is a “linear search”, which traverses the items of the collec-](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [tion in order, stopping if it finds the target. In the worst case it has to traverse the entire](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [collection, so the run time is linear.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[The](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [in](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [operator for sequences uses a linear search; so do string methods like](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [find](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/PokerHandSoln.py) [count](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py)

[If the elements of the sequence are in order, you can use a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [bisection search](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [, which is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [O](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [(](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [log](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) [Bisection search is similar to the algorithm you might use to look a word up](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) in a dictionary (a paper dictionary, not the data structure). Instead of starting at the be- [ginning and checking each item in order, you start with the item in the middle and check](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [whether the word you are looking for comes before or after. If it comes before, then you](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [search the first half of the sequence. Otherwise you search the second half. Either way, you](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [cut the number of remaining items in half.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[If the sequence has 1,000,000 items, it will take about 20 steps to find the word or conclude](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [that it’s not there. So that’s about 50,000 times faster than a linear search.](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[Bisection search can be much faster than linear search, but it requires the sequence to be in](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [order, which might require extra work.](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py)

[There is another data structure, called a](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [hashtable](https://thinkpython.com/code/c06d) [that is even faster—it can do a search](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [in constant time—and it doesn’t require the items to be sorted.](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [Python dictionaries are](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [implemented using hashtables, which is why most dictionary operations, including the](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [in](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [operator, are constant time.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Appendix B. Analysis of Algorithms](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

#### [B.4](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Hashtables](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[To explain how hashtables work and why their performance is so good, I start with a simple](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [implementation of a map and gradually improve it until it’s a hashtable.](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[I use Python to demonstrate these implementations, but in real life you wouldn’t write](https://thinkpython.com/code/grid.py) [code like this in Python; you would just use a dictionary! So for the rest of this chapter, you](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [have to imagine that dictionaries don’t exist and you want to implement a data structure](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [that maps from keys to values. The operations you have to implement are:](https://thinkpython.com/code/invert_dict.py)

[add(k, v)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pi.py) [Add a new item that maps from key](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [k](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [to value](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [v](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [. With a Python dictionary,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) d , [this operation is written](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [d[k] = v](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

get(k) : [Look up and return the value that corresponds to key](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [k](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [. With a Python dictionary,](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [d](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [, this operation is written](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) d[k] or d.get(k) .

[For now, I assume that each key only appears once. The simplest implementation of this](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [interface uses a list of tuples, where each tuple is a key-value pair.](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[class LinearMap:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox)

[def __init__(self):](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies) [self.items = []](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[def add(self, k, v):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [self.items.append((k, v))](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[def get(self, k):](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [for key, val in self.items:](https://thinkpython.com/code/markov.py) [if key == k:](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [return val](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [raise KeyError](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers)

[add](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [appends a key-value tuple to the list of items, which takes constant time.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[get](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [uses a](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [for](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [loop to search the list: if it finds the target key it returns the corresponding](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [value; otherwise it raises a](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [KeyError](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [. So](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [get](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [is linear.](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html)

An alternative is to keep the list sorted by key. Then get could use a bisection search, which is O ( log n ) [. But inserting a new item in the middle of a list is linear, so this might not be the](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [best option. There are other data structures that can implement](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [add](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [get](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [in log time,](http://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#string-methods) [but that’s still not as good as constant time, so let’s move on.](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[One way to improve](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [LinearMap](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [is to break the list of key-value pairs into smaller lists.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [Here’s an implementation called](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [BetterMap](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [, which is a list of 100 LinearMaps. As we’ll](https://thinkpython.com/code/koch.py) [see in a second, the order of growth for](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [get](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is still linear, but](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [BetterMap](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is a step on the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [path toward hashtables:](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py)

[class BetterMap:](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def __init__(self, n=100):](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [self.maps = []](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py) [for i in range(n):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [self.maps.append(LinearMap())](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[B.4. Hashtables](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[def find_map(self, k):](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [index = hash(k) % len(self.maps)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spiral) [return self.maps[index]](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

[def add(self, k, v):](https://thinkpython.com/code/Time2_soln.py) [m = self.find_map(k)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/) [m.add(k, v)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[def get(self, k):](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [m = self.find_map(k)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function) [return m.get(k)](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk1.py)

[__init__](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [makes a list of](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [n LinearMap](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [s.](http://www.thinkpython.com/)

[find_map](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is used by](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5) [add](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [and](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [get](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [to figure out which map to put the new item in, or which](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py) [map to search.](https://thinkpython.com/code/Circle.py)

[find_map](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [uses the built-in function](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [hash](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, which takes almost any Python object and returns](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [an integer. A limitation of this implementation is that it only works with hashable keys.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Birthday_paradox) [Mutable types like lists and dictionaries are unhashable.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal)

[Hashable objects that are considered equivalent return the same hash value, but the con-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [verse is not necessarily true: two objects with different values can return the same hash](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [value.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py)

[find_map](https://thinkpython.com/code/do_four.py) [uses the modulus operator to wrap the hash values into the range from 0 to](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [len(self.maps)](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [, so the result is a legal index into the list. Of course, this means that many](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [different hash values will wrap onto the same index. But if the hash function spreads things](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [out pretty evenly (which is what hash functions are designed to do), then we expect](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [/100](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [items per LinearMap.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[Since the run time of](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [LinearMap.get](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [is proportional to the number of items, we expect](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [BetterMap to be about 100 times faster than LinearMap. The order of growth is still linear,](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [but the leading coefficient is smaller. That’s nice, but still not as good as a hashtable.](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt)

[Here (finally) is the crucial idea that makes hashtables fast: if you can keep the maximum](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [length of the LinearMaps bounded,](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort) LinearMap.get is constant time. All you have to do is keep track of the number of items and when the number of items per LinearMap exceeds [a threshold, resize the hashtable by adding more LinearMaps.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

[Here is an implementation of a hashtable:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[class HashMap:](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py)

[def __init__(self):](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [self.maps = BetterMap(2)](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [self.num = 0](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

[def get(self, k):](http://www.speech.cs.cmu.edu/cgi-bin/cmudict) [return self.maps.get(k)](https://thinkpython.com/code/reverse_pair.py)

[def add(self, k, v):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [if self.num == len(self.maps.maps):](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[Appendix B. Analysis of Algorithms](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[self.resize()](https://thinkpython.com/code/letters.py)

[self.maps.add(k, v)](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [self.num += 1](https://thinkpython.com/code/spiral.py)

def resize(self): [new_maps = BetterMap(self.num * 2)](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/3.0/)

[for m in self.maps.maps:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [for k, v in m.items:](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py) [new_maps.add(k, v)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ackermann_function)

[self.maps = new_maps](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann_memo.py)

[__init__](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [creates a](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) [BetterMap](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) and initializes num [, which keeps track of the number of items.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Md5)

| of items and the size of the | BetterMap | : if they are equal, the average number of items per |
| ---------------------------- | --------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| LinearMap is 1, so it calls  | resize    | .                                                    |

resize [makes a new](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [BetterMap](https://thinkpython.com/code/BadKangaroo.py) [, twice as big as the previous one, and then “rehashes” the](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sexagesimal) [items from the old map to the new.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Letter_frequencies)

[Rehashing is necessary because changing the number of LinearMaps changes the denomi-](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [nator of the modulus operator in](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [find_map](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [. That means that some objects that used to hash](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate_pairs.py) [into the same LinearMap will get split up (which is what we wanted, right?).](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e)

[Rehashing is linear, so](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [resize](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [is linear, which might seem bad, since I promised that](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [add](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [would be constant time. But remember that we don’t have to resize every time, so](http://tinyurl.com/thinkpython2e) [add](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [is](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Big_O_notation) [usually constant time and only occasionally linear. The total amount of work to run](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [add](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [times is proportional to](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/wordlist.py) [, so the average time of each](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [add](http://wikipedia.org/wiki/Moby_Project) [is constant time!](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[To see how this works, think about starting with an empty HashTable and adding a se-](http://www.cartalk.com/content/puzzlers) [quence of items. We start with 2 LinearMaps, so the first 2 adds are fast (no resizing re-](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [quired). Let’s say that they take one unit of work each. The next add requires a resize, so](https://thinkpython.com/code/words.txt) [we have to rehash the first two items (let’s call that 2 more units of work) and then add the](https://thinkpython.com/code/list_exercises.py) [third item (one more unit). Adding the next item costs 1 unit, so the total so far is 6 units](http://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html) [of work for 4 items.](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radix_sort)

The next add [costs 5 units, but the next three are only one unit each, so the total is 14 units](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py) [for the first 8 adds.](https://thinkpython.com/code/palindrome_soln.py)

The next [add](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py) [costs 9 units, but then we can add 7 more before the next resize, so the total is](https://thinkpython.com/code/flower.py) [30 units for the first 16 adds.](https://thinkpython.com/code/polygon.py)

[After 32 adds, the total cost is 62 units, and I hope you are starting to see a pattern. After](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [adds, where](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_number) [is a power of two, the total cost is 2](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [−](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [2 units, so the average work per add](https://thinkpython.com/code/pie.py) [is a little less than 2 units. When](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [n](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [is a power of two, that’s the best case; for other values of](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake) [n](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [the average work is a little higher, but that’s not important. The important thing is that it](https://thinkpython.com/code/cartalk3.py) [is](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [O](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [(](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [)](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py) [.](https://thinkpython.com/code/anagram_sets.py)

[Figure B.1 shows how this works graphically. Each block represents a unit of work. The](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [columns show the total work for each add in order from left to right: the first two](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [adds](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [cost](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators) [1 unit each, the third costs 3 units, etc.](http://wiki.python.org/moin/BitwiseOperators)

[B.5. Glossary](https://thinkpython.com/code/interlock.py)

[Figure B.1: The cost of a hashtable add.](https://thinkpython.com/code/rotate.py)

[The extra work of rehashing appears as a sequence of increasingly tall towers with increas-](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [ing space between them. Now if you knock over the towers, spreading the cost of resizing](http://www.thinkpython.com/) [over all adds, you can see graphically that the total cost after](https://thinkpython.com/code/ackermann.py) n adds is 2 n − 2.
